# Book 1: The Eleventh Child, Off to College

## Chapter 1: The Mix-Up

### Part 1: The Administrative Prelude

The journey began on a Wednesday in late August. The seven-hour drive from the flat, humid landscape of Hampton Roads to the rolling hills of the New River Valley was a tense affair. Aubrey sat silently in the passenger seat, watching the world change outside his window, while his mother, Aubrie Sinclair, filled the car with a steady stream of anxious instructions.

They checked into a hotel in Christiansburg, one of the several clustered around the busy I-81 interchange at exit 118C. When they opened the door to their room, there was only one queen-sized bed.

"Oh, dear," his mother said with a sigh that held no real disappointment. "I asked for two beds, but this must have been all they had available with the students coming back to town. Well," she said with a shrug that made it clear it wasn't a question, "I guess we'll just have to make do for a couple of nights."

Aubrey lay stiffly on his side of the bed that night, his mother's breathing a familiar, oppressive presence beside him. He stared into the darkness of the unfamiliar room, counting the hours until Friday, when he would finally have his own bed in his own room. “Aubrey dear, come comfort your mother,” Aubrie said, pulling him close. He felt smothered but was used to it by now. He hated it but put up with it. What could he do anyways? She is his mother and is all he has ever known.

Thursday was spent on the bustling campus of Commonwealth Polytechnic Institute. Their first stop was to get his student ID, a plastic card with the name AUBREY JORDAN FULLER printed starkly beneath his nervous face.

Their next and final stop was the Office of Educational Accessibility, to verify that all his accommodations—both academic and residential—were in place.

"Hello," Aubrie Sinclair said to the counselor. "We're just here to verify that all of my son Aubrey's accommodations are in order for the semester."

The counselor smiled kindly, but addressed her questions directly to the student. "Yes, Aubrey, I have your file right here. We have you down for extra time on exams and access to a university-provided note-taker. Does that all sound correct to you?"

"Yes," Aubrey said, his voice quiet. "That's correct."

"Excellent," the counselor continued, looking at her screen. "And I also see that our office coordinated with Housing to get you an early move-in accommodation due to your disability. You are all set for tomorrow, Friday, at 9:00 AM. You'll be in Bennett Hall, Room 214, Bed A."

With all the administrative tasks verified, they returned to the hotel for one final night in the shared bed. Tomorrow, he would move in. Tomorrow, his new life would begin.

### Part 2: Getting Settled

After his mother, Aubrie, left with a final wave and a promise to call that evening, Aubrey Jordan Fuller found himself truly alone for the first time in his new dorm room. The quiet was a welcome change from the earlier chaos of moving in. He took a long, steadying breath and began to take a proper inventory of the small space—his space.

The room was tiny, clearly designed for two people but now cramped to fit three due to the university's housing crisis. He flipped the main switch, and a large, glaring fluorescent light in the ceiling flickered on, bathing the room in a harsh, sterile glow.

He looked back at the heavy door he'd just entered. From the hallway, he'd tapped his new CPI Poly Pass on the reader, and the door had swung open for him automatically. On the inside, he saw the corresponding silver handicap push button next to the frame.

To his immediate left was a closed door, which he knew led to the shared toilet room. He wheeled over and opened it, confirming what he'd seen on the housing tour: a small, functional room containing only a toilet, but fitted with the necessary ADA-compliant grab bars. He saw the second door on the opposite wall, the one that connected to Room 216.

Back in the main room, he noted the sink and mirror area next to the toilet door. He rolled his wheelchair closer, a small smile touching his lips as he saw the open space underneath. It was a roll-under sink, intentionally designed without a cabinet so he could get close to the basin.

He turned his attention to the rest of the room. A single window was set in the center of the far wall.

- To the far left was a standalone, single Twin XL bed—Bed A, his assigned spot.

- To the far right, in the opposite corner, was a metal bunk bed, also with Twin XL mattresses.

- Both bed setups had their foot facing him, and both were just bare mattresses waiting for the pillows and blankets he'd brought.

He counted the furniture. Two desks, two dressers, and two nightstands. This confirmed the room's original two-person design. His future roommates, the ones assigned to the bunk beds, would have to share a desk and dresser. This was it. College.

He decided to set up his most important tool first: his laptop. He wheeled himself over to his desk, placed the laptop on it, and opened the lid. The battery icon blinked red. He sighed and looked around for an outlet. He spotted one on the wall, but its standard height from the floor was just awkwardly out of comfortable reach from his wheelchair.

He maneuvered his chair closer, stretching to try and plug in the bulky adapter. He could just barely touch the outlet, but he couldn't get the leverage to push the prongs in securely. He grunted with frustration, a small but significant reminder that even in an "accessible" room, the world wasn't always designed with him in mind.

"Hey there, need a hand?"

A friendly voice came from his open doorway. A young woman with a lanyard and an "RA" name tag stood there, her expression kind. "I'm Isabel, one of the RAs for one of the other floors. Just doing my rounds, helping the RA of this floor."

Aubrey felt a flush of embarrassment at being caught struggling. "Uh, yeah. Hi. I'm Aubrey. Just trying to plug this in," he said, gesturing to the power cord.

Isabel saw the problem instantly. "Oh yeah, that's a terrible spot for an outlet. No problem at all. Let's make this easier." He handed her the power strip that he pulled out of his backpack. She plugged the power strip into the wall outlet and then neatly ran the cord up to his desk, securing the strip on top with a piece of reusable adhesive she produced from her pocket. "There," she said with a smile. "Now you can plug anything you want in right from your desk."

"Wow. Thanks. Thank you," Aubrey said, genuinely grateful.

"Don't mention it. Do you need a hand with anything else? Getting your bed set up?"

Aubrey hesitated, then nodded. "Actually, yeah. The pillows and blankets are kind of a pain."

For the next ten minutes, Isabel helped him make his bed, placing his pillows and blankets exactly how he liked them. She even helped him unpack his main suitcase, putting his clothes away in the dresser drawers. She was efficient, friendly, and didn't make him feel like a burden.

"There you go," she said finally. "All set." She paused at the door. "Seriously, Aubrey, if you need any help with anything, you just find me or any of the other RAs, or the hall director. That's what we're here for."

"I will. Thank you, Isabel," he said, "Do you know who my two roommates are? Or when they will arrive?"

Isabel gave him an apologetic look. "I'm sorry, I don't have that information," she explained. "Since I'm not the regular RA for this floor, I don't have access to the detailed room roster with names and arrival times. I'm just helping out with early check-ins today."

She then offered a reassuring smile. "But I do know that the main move-in day starts tomorrow morning. So, you can expect them to start arriving tomorrow at the soonest. Looks like you've got the whole suite to yourself for tonight!"

Aubrey nodded, a wave of both relief and nervousness washing over him. One night of peace before meeting the two strangers who would become his new roommates.

She gave him a final wave and left. Aubrey looked around his room, which now felt much more functional and comfortable. He easily plugged his laptop into the power strip on his desk and watched it spring to life. He spent the rest of the day in his room alone, content in his new space. He had snacks and sandwiches his mom had packed in his bag, and he didn't want to venture outside anyway. The clouds that had been gathering when he arrived had finally opened up, and a steady, pouring rain now washed over the campus, making his quiet, solitary room feel like the coziest place in the world.

As evening fell, the rain continued its soothing rhythm against the window. Aubrey finished the last of his sandwiches and felt a wave of exhaustion from the emotional and physical toll of the day. It was time for bed. He wheeled himself over to his dresser where Isabel had so neatly placed his clothes and pulled out a soft t-shirt and a pair of pajama pants. He looked at them, then down at the jeans and layered shirts he was still wearing.

A familiar knot of anxiety tightened in his stomach. At home, his mother would help him. Her assistance was a routine part of his day, a seamless act of care. Here, alone, the simple act of changing clothes felt like an insurmountable mountain. He thought about what Isabel had said: *if you need any help with anything*. But the thought of asking a complete stranger for help with something so personal filled him with a profound sense of mortification.

He sat there for a long time, weighing the physical discomfort of sleeping in his day clothes against the social agony of asking for help. The denim of his jeans felt restrictive, and he knew he would be hot and twisted in them by morning. With a sigh of resignation, he put the pajamas back in the drawer.

He maneuvered himself from his wheelchair onto his bed, a practiced but still strenuous motion. At least he could get his shoes off. He hooked the heel of one sneaker on the toe of the other and pushed until it came loose, dropping to the floor with a soft thud. He did the same with the second shoe. It was a small measure of comfort, but it came with a sharp cost. He stared at the shoes on the floor. He wouldn't be able to put them back on. He couldn't reach them from his chair, and even if he could, he didn't know how to tie the laces. His mother had never taught him. She had never prepared him for any of this.

Her parting words echoed in his mind, dripping with a pride that now felt corrosive. *"I'm so proud of you, a real college kid now! An adult, living on your own."*

*Living on my own,* he thought with a bitter pang of clarity. She had said it with a beaming smile, but what did it really mean? She had dropped him in a dorm room hours from home without ever teaching him how to dress himself, cook a simple meal, or navigate the hundred small tasks she had always just... done. The honest, painful truth began to settle in his chest: maybe she wasn't proud of his independence. Maybe she was just glad to be rid of the burden. The thought was a cold stone in his gut. All her talk of him being an adult felt like a convenient excuse to finally be free of him.

Lying on top of the covers, still in his constricting day clothes, shoeless and stranded, he felt a profound loneliness he had never known. It wasn't just the physical discomfort; it was the chilling realization that he had been left behind. He closed his eyes, listening to the rain, and eventually drifted into a restless sleep, already dreading the stiff, unprepared morning that awaited him.

### Part 3: An Unlikely Welcome

Aubrey woke up stiff and sore, the restrictive denim of his jeans clinging to him. He’d tossed and turned all night, his sleep shallow and plagued by a recurring sense of falling. He felt a dull ache in his back and a profound exhaustion that had nothing to do with the physical effort of moving in. He was an adult on his own, just as his mother had said with that bright, brittle smile. And he was already failing.

The morning quiet was shattered by the official start of freshman move-in day. The hallway outside his door became a cacophony of shouting, laughing, and the rolling of carts. The noise was a persistent, grating hum that vibrated through the floor, putting him on edge.

He tried to focus on arranging his desk, but the anxiety from the night before, the raw feeling of being a burden, mingled with the sensory assault. After struggling for nearly an hour just to get his books stacked, the exhaustion from his sleepless night and the sensory overload became too much. "I just need to rest," he muttered, his head pounding. He transferred from his wheelchair to his bed—Bed A—and, still fully clothed, pulled the blanket over himself, desperate for just a few minutes of quiet before his roommates arrived. The steady, grating hum from the hallway eventually lulled him into a fitful, uneasy sleep.

Around mid-morning, his door swung open. A young woman with her arms full of bags stopped short. She saw the room, clearly already occupied. And then she saw him: a boy, fast asleep in the single bed.

"Oh! I am so sorry," she said, her eyes wide with confusion. "I must have the wrong room."

Her voice cut through his sleep. Aubrey jolted awake, his heart hammering. He slowly sat up, disoriented, and saw a girl standing in his room. His stomach clenched. He was stiff, uncomfortable, and felt a flush of shame.

"Uh... this is room 214, right?" he asked, his voice hesitant.

"Yeah, but..." She glanced at the name card on the door. "My room is 214. I'm Aubrie Jean Kelly."

Aubey’s own confused gaze followed hers to the door card. He saw her name, and his own right below it: Aubrey Jordan Fuller. He also saw another name: Madison Elizabeth Scott. The names hit him with the force of a physical blow. Females. His roommates were females.

The overwhelming sensory noise from the hallway, the social pressure of a stranger of the opposite gender, and the sudden, terrifying certainty that he was the one in the wrong room... it all converged. In a blind panic, he threw the blanket off and clumsily transferred himself from the bed into his wheelchair. He had to get out. He had to pack. He had to fix this. But the panic was too fast. His control shattered. The world became too loud, too bright, too much. A low, distressed hum started in his throat, and he began to rock back and forth in his wheelchair, his hands flying up to cover his ears. He squeezed his eyes shut, overwhelmed by a complete system failure.

The girl, Aubrie Jean, saw his reaction, and her own expression shifted from confusion to a deep, immediate understanding. She was also autistic, and she recognized the signs of a meltdown instantly.

"It's okay," she said to her parents, who were standing worriedly in the hall behind her. "He's not angry. He's overwhelmed."

She put her things down and took a slow, deliberate step into the room. "Aubrey," she said, her voice quiet and even. "My name is Aubrie. It's okay. You're safe. Focus on my voice".

He continued to rock. "Is it okay if I put my hand on your shoulder?" she asked gently. Slowly, Aubrey gave a short, sharp nod. Aubrie Jean placed her hand firmly on his shoulder. Aubrey’s shoulders shuddered, and he gave another, almost desperate nod, a silent plea for more. Understanding washed over Aubrie’s face. She moved behind his wheelchair and wrapped her arms around his shoulders in a firm, secure hug.

This was the first time in his life he had ever received an unconditional hug.

It was at that exact moment that the door opened again. A second young woman, Madison Scott, entered hauling a large box. She froze, taking in the scene. "Uh... is this room 214?"

Aubrie didn’t break her focus. "It is," she said quietly. "He's really overwhelmed. Could you maybe close the door?"

Madison immediately pushed the door closed, dulling the hallway noise. She looked at Aubrey, at his wrinkled clothes and the sheer distress on his face, and her heart went out to him. "What can I do?" she whispered compassionately.

"Just... be calm," Aubrie replied. "It'll pass. We just have to ride it out".

Madison nodded again, pulling up a desk chair and sitting a few feet away, simply offering a quiet, non-judgmental presence. Over the next hour, as Aubrey’s meltdown slowly subsided into shuddering breaths and exhaustion, the chaotic move-in continued. A girl with vibrant purple hair, Zoe Brooks, poked her head in from the connecting bathroom door, her eyes wide. "Uh, hi. I'm in 216. Is everything okay? There's a... guy in here. Is he helping you two with move in?"

Before Aubrie could explain, two more girls, Hannah Parker and Lauren Barnes, appeared behind Zoe, their expressions ranging from curious to concerned.

Zoe, ever the pragmatist, took one look at the three names on the door card for Room 214 and the exhausted frightened boy in the wheelchair, and her face set with determination. "Okay, something's definitely wrong here. I'm going to get the RA".

A few minutes later, she returned with a harried-looking young man. "I'm Henry, the RA for this floor," he announced, then stopped, his eyes darting between the six students crammed into the small room. He looked at the door card, then at Aubrey. "Aubrey Fuller?" he asked. Aubrey, drained but calm, nodded. A look of dawning horror crossed Henry's face. "Oh no. I think... I think Housing made a mistake. 'Aubrey' is usually a girl's name. I... I have to get the Hall Director".

Henry hurried away, leaving the six of them in an awkward silence. Soon, he returned with a woman in her late twenties who introduced herself as Kelsey, the Hall Director. Her expression was weary but kind.

"Okay, folks," Kelsey began, sighing heavily. "It appears we have a significant clerical error from the Housing Office. Aubrey, they assigned you here assuming you were female".

She then laid out the grim reality of the situation. "To be blunt, the university has a massive housing crisis this year. We've overbooked every dorm. That's why your rooms are 'triples' designed for two people. On top of that, we're already putting students up in the hotel next to campus, and when that filled up, we started using another hotel down the road in Christiansburg, which is a twenty-to-thirty-minute bus ride away". Aubrey glanced up when Kelsey said that. He remembered the signs at the largest of the hotels that was next to the one he stayed at with his mother: something about overflow student housing for the university.

She looked directly at the five young women. "Here's the situation. Aubrey is staying. This is the designated handicap-accessible suite on the floor, and we have no other place for him. For the rest of you, I can start the process of finding you a new room assignment, but I cannot guarantee it won't be in one of those hotels. And I can't guarantee you'd all stay together".

The six strangers looked at each other. The idea of being shipped off to a hotel, separated from the main campus, was deeply unappealing.

Lauren was the first to speak. "So, we can either stay here, with... Aubrey," she said, giving him a small, reassuring smile, "or get shipped off to a hotel thirty minutes away?".

Kelsey nodded. "That's the choice, at least for the next two weeks. The official roommate change window opens then. You could try to move then, but again, no guarantees".

The five women huddled together, whispering. Aubrey watched them, his anxiety returning. After a moment, Aubrie turned to him, including him in their circle. "What do you think?" she asked. "Are you okay with this?".

Aubrey, who had spent most of his life on the outside looking in, was taken aback by the simple act of being asked. "I... I don't want to cause a problem," he stammered.

"It's not your fault," Madison said firmly. "It's the university's mess".

After a minute of quiet consensus, Zoe looked at Kelsey. "We'll tough it out," she declared. "For two weeks, at least. We'll stay".

A wave of relief washed over Kelsey's face. "Thank you," she said, genuinely grateful. "Truly. Let us know if you need anything." She and a relieved-looking Henry took their leave.

In the sudden, heavy quiet, the six of them looked at each other, the reality of their situation sinking in. It was Madison who finally broke the silence, her curiosity getting the better of her.

"Okay, I have to ask," she said, looking at Aubrey with a friendly, puzzled expression. "No offense, but... how did you not notice our names on the door when you moved in yesterday? You must have seen 'Aubrie Kelly' and 'Madison Scott' and realized something was up".

The other girls nodded, clearly wondering the same thing.

Aubrey flushed slightly, shaking his head. "There weren't any names on the door when I got here yesterday," he explained quietly. "My mom and I just had the room number. After she left, I just... stayed in here. I had food she packed for me, and it was raining, so I didn't leave". He looked around at them. "The first time the door opened again after my mom left was this morning, when Aubrie," he gestured to Aubrie Jean, "walked in. That was the first I knew about any of this. They just checked my name off their list and told me I was assigned to Bed A in Room 214. My Student ID unlocked the door to the building entrance, then it unlocked the door to the hallway after they told me my room number, then it unlocked the door to the room, so I didn’t know anything was amiss yet".

"Ohhh," Lauren said, a wave of understanding passing through the group. "So the RAs must have put the name cards up sometime yesterday afternoon after you were already settled in".

"That makes sense," Hannah agreed. The small mystery was solved, another piece of their strange first day clicking into place.

"Well," Aubrie said, breaking the silence with a wry smile. "This is... cozy".

A nervous laugh rippled through the group. The tension broken, Aubrie turned to him. "So, Aubrey and Aubrie, huh? My mom and dad liked the name so much they gave it to me". She stifled a giggle.

He managed a small, pained smile. "My mom's name is Aubrie, too. Different spelling. She said she put 'Aubrey' on my birth certificate as a joke that just kind of... stuck". He looked at Aubrie Jean, then at Madison, and a new, quiet resolve settled in his chest. This was his chance. This whole embarrassing, terrifying situation had been caused by that name. A name he hated, a name that was *hers*. He took a breath, the decision his first, small act of defiance against a lifetime of his mother's control. "But... you can call me Jordan. It's my middle name. I think I'll go by Jordan here".

"Jordan," Aubrie repeated, nodding. "I like it. Hey, what's your major?"

Jordan hesitated, his stomach tightening. He looked down, the name of the major tasting like a trap. "Astrophysics," he replied, his voice barely a whisper. He braced himself, expecting her to look confused or, worse, to feel pity for him.

Instead, her eyes lit up. "No way! Me too!".

His head snapped up. He stared at her, his mind reeling. *What?* This insurmountable mountain his mother had picked out for him, this complex, impossible degree she had chosen just to prove he would fail—this girl, this kind, safe girl, had *chosen* it?

For the first time, a fragile, terrifying hope sparked in his chest. Maybe he wasn't set up to fail. Maybe he would have someone to climb the mountain with him.

"Are you..." he asked, his voice suddenly earnest, "Are you in Astronomy 101 with Dr. Alistair?"

"And the Friday lab section!" she confirmed, her own excitement bubbling over.

"Me too!" he said, the words coming out in a rush of pure relief. A genuine spark of excitement lit his voice for the first time.

"We should sit together, if there aren't assigned seats," she beamed.

Jordan nodded, a slow, watery smile spreading across his face. His mother had pushed him off a cliff. And this girl, this *Aubrie* who was the antidote to his *Aubrie*, had just shown up at the bottom with a safety net and a climbing rope. For the first time all day, for the first time in his life, he felt a sense of belonging. He saw a small, actual light at the end of the tunnel.

### Part 4: Making It Official

It was Madison who brought up the topic, her tone gentle and curious. "So, Jordan," she began, "back there when you were talking to Aubrie, you said you wanted to go by Jordan. Is that... like, for real? Is that what you want all of us to call you?"

Jordan looked at Aubrie, who gave him a small, encouraging smile. He felt a surge of confidence. "Yeah," he said, his voice firmer than it had been all day. "It is. I've always hated 'Aubrey.' I want to be Jordan here." He glanced at the door to their room. "I just wish the card on the door didn't..."

"We can ask Henry to change it," Aubrie said immediately, her support unwavering. "I bet he can print a new one. Let's go see him right now."

A few minutes later, Jordan, Aubrie, and Madison were sitting in the small, functional Resident Advisor office down the hall. Henry sat at his desk, and he motioned for the three of them to take the chairs opposite him. As Jordan wheeled up next to Aubrie, he nervously reached out and took her hand under the table. She responded instantly, her fingers lacing through his, holding his hand firmly in her lap. It was the first time they had held hands in front of anyone else, a quiet, public declaration of their budding connection.

"Hi, Henry," Jordan began, feeling braver with Aubrie's hand in his. "Um... so the name on my door card is 'Aubrey Jordan Fuller.' But I go by my middle name, Jordan. I was wondering if it would be possible to get a new card that just says 'Jordan Fuller'?"

Henry smiled warmly. "Yeah, absolutely, man. Not a problem at all. I can print you a new door card right now." He started typing. "But we should do one better," he continued. "The door card is just for the hallway. You'll want all your professors and the university staff to know. Let me show you something."

He turned his monitor toward them and guided Jordan through the CPI student portal to the "Personal Information" section. He pointed to a field. "Right here. It says 'Preferred Name.' If you type 'Jordan' into this field, that's the name that will show up on all the official class rosters for your instructors. It'll also be the name that shows up for advisors, the library, everywhere."

He then leaned forward, offering some practical advice. "Now, the system can take a day or so to update, and some professors print their rosters on day one and never look again. The best thing you can do is send a quick, polite email to each of your instructors tonight, before classes start tomorrow. Just introduce yourself and let them know you go by Jordan."

"Wow," Jordan said, feeling a sense of empowerment he'd never known. "Okay. Thank you, Henry. So much."

"No problem at all," Henry said, handing him the freshly printed door card that read, simply, Jordan Fuller.

They left the office and headed back to their suite, Jordan and Aubrie still holding hands. He had a mission for the evening: to email his professors and formally introduce himself to the world, for the very first time, as Jordan.

"I'll walk back with you guys and swap that card out right now," Henry offered, grabbing the small, freshly printed name card from his desk.

The three of them walked with him down the short hall back to Room 214, where the other suitemates were now talking quietly in the adjoining room. Henry used a small tool to pop open the clear plastic holder on the door frame. He slid out the old card, with the typed name Aubrey Jordan Fuller, and handed it to Jordan as a small souvenir. Then, he slid in the new one.

The effect was immediate and powerful. There, on the door to his new home, were the words: Jordan Fuller.

Jordan stared at it, a deep sense of satisfaction washing over him. It was real.

"Now, just so you know," Henry said, tapping his own plastic CPI ID card that hung from a lanyard around his neck. "This doesn't change your official university ID. I can change the door card because it's just for display, but the main CPI ID has to match the legal name from your birth certificate for security and official records."

He continued, wanting to be clear. "So your ID card will still say 'Aubrey Jordan Fuller' next to your photo and student number. But don't worry. Because you updated your preferred name in the portal, whenever you swipe your card—at the dining hall, the library, anywhere—the name that will pop up on the staff's screen will be 'Jordan.' It's just the physical card itself that can't be changed without a legal court order."

Jordan nodded, understanding. He touched the wallet in his pocket where his own new ID was sitting. A small, new flicker of dissatisfaction was there, the knowledge that "Aubrey" would still follow him around on that piece of plastic. But for now, this was a huge victory.

"Thank you, Henry," Aubrie said sincerely. "For everything."

"No problem at all," Henry said with a final, warm smile. "Enjoy the rest of your night. Welcome to Bennett Hall, Jordan." He gave a small wave and headed back down the hall to his office.

Jordan, Aubrie, and Madison stood there for a moment longer, just looking at the door. The first official piece of his new life was finally in place.

### Part 5: Logistics of a Co-Ed Suite

The practicalities of their arrangement soon came to the forefront. It was Lauren who brought it up. "Okay, so... logistics," she said, gesturing between the two rooms and the single connecting toilet. "Six people, one toilet, and no privacy. Jordan, you were expecting guy roommates, and we were expecting another girl. How do we make this not... super awkward?"

There was a beat of silence before Madison shrugged, a pragmatic look on her face. "Look, we're all adults. We have one toilet. The doors connect. There's no room to be shy. I say we just agree that what happens in the suite, stays in the suite. No need to hide, no need to turn away. There's literally no room for privacy, so let's not pretend there is."

A surprising murmur of agreement went through the group. It was a radical solution born of necessity, but it felt right.

As evening approached, the five women decided to head to the dining hall for their first official college meal. They were gathering their student IDs when Zoe noticed Jordan sitting quietly by his desk. He looked small and isolated, and a wave of collective understanding passed between the girls. They recognized the look of someone who was used to being left out.

"Hey, Jordan," Lauren said kindly. "We're all going to dinner. You should come with us."

Jordan looked up, surprised. "Oh. You don't have to..."

"We want to," Aubrie insisted, already moving to position herself behind his wheelchair. "You're our suitemate. Our family, for the next two weeks at least. We stick together."

A genuine, wide smile spread across Jordan's face. As the six of them trooped down the hall, with Aubrie pushing his chair, they were a strange and motley crew. In the bustling cafeteria, they found a large table and sat together, sharing stories about their hometowns and their anxieties about starting college. For the first time, Jordan didn't feel like an outsider; he felt like a friend.

Back in the suite, the reality of their shared life continued. As everyone started changing into pajamas, Lauren noticed Jordan’s hesitation. "Everything okay, Jordan?"

He flushed, looking down at his hands. "I... um... I can't... My mom usually helps me." The admission was quiet, laced with shame.

"Hey, not a problem," Lauren said, her tone gentle and completely free of judgment. "You've been in those all day? You must be so uncomfortable. C'mon."

She helped him into his room and, with a kindness that almost brought tears to his eyes, assisted him out of his stiff jeans and into his soft pajama pants. "Okay, shoes," she said, spotting them on the floor. She picked them up, expertly untied the knotted laces he’d kicked them off with, and set them by his desk. "I can help you with these in the morning."

Her simple, practical help felt like a lifeline. He was clean, comfortable, and cared for. When she left, he felt a sense of peace he hadn’t thought possible.

Later that night, the suite was dark and quiet, filled with the soft breathing of six sleeping students. But Jordan’s sleep was not peaceful. It was his room, Room 214, but it was vast, cold, and unnervingly quiet. The friendly clutter of his new life was gone. He saw his five suitemates—Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, and Hannah—standing in the far corners of the room, their backs to him, their shoulders hunched together as they whispered.

He tried to call out to them, but his voice made no sound. He wheeled himself forward, but the floor felt like thick mud, each push of his wheels an exhausting effort. As he got closer, their whispers grew, and he could finally make out their words.

"He can't even get dressed by himself," Lauren said, her voice cold and clinical.

Madison sighed, a sound of pure exhaustion. "Such a burden. We always have to help him."

Zoe's voice was sharp with impatient logic. "I still don't understand how this happened. Why is there a *boy* in our suite? It's just wrong."

"He's just... not like us," Hannah added, her voice full of a pity that felt worse than anger.

Then came the final, most painful cut, from the one who shared his name. "He's broken," Aubrie Jean stated, as if it were a simple, observable fact.

As his heart seized with the panic of being utterly rejected, the door to the room opened. His mother, Aubrie Sinclair, stood there, her face etched with an expression of sad, pitying love.

"Oh, Aubrey, dear," she said, her voice like poisoned honey. "Look at you. Did you really think they would want you? Did you think this 'college' thing would work?"

She gestured to the figures who now stood silently, judging him. "They are just being polite, sweetheart. You're too much for them. You're too much for anyone but me."

She opened her arms wide, her smile now one of serene, confident victory. "But it's okay. You don't have to try anymore. You can stop pretending. Come home. Come back to Mommy. I'm your safe harbor. I am the only one who will ever truly take care of you, because no one else wants the burden."

The figures of his friends all turned at once, their faces blank and cold. In unison, they spoke. "Go home, Aubrey."

He was trapped, cornered by his own worst fears, with nowhere left to go. But then, a new sound entered the cold, vast room.

"Jordan?"

The voice was soft, coming from nowhere and everywhere at once. It was a sound that was out of place, a gentle note of dissonance in the oppressive silence.

"Jordan? It's okay. Wake up."

The voice was a stone tossed into the still, stagnant water. The image of his mother's victorious smile began to waver, like a reflection disturbed. The cold faces of his friends started to blur at the edges, their solid forms becoming unstable. The demented calliope music that had been humming in the background sputtered and died, leaving only the sound of that warm, steady voice. The feeling of being mired in mud was replaced by the real-world tangle of a soft blanket. The chilling dread was being pushed away by a growing warmth against his back.

His eyes snapped open.

The dream shattered completely, leaving only a phantom residue of terror. He wasn't trapped. He was in his bed. And the first thing he became aware of was the source of that warmth, the solid reality of another person pressed against him.

It was Aubrie Jean.

She had heard him, and her voice had been a lifeline, pulling him out of the suffocating darkness. Having gently woken him, she had already turned him onto his side, his back facing her, and had wrapped an arm around his waist, holding him close. She spooned him, resting her chin on his shoulder, her body a warm, solid shield.

"Shh, it's okay," she murmured into his ear, her voice the same one that had just rescued him. "You're okay. I'm here."

Slowly, the last vestiges of the nightmare's terror receded, replaced by the steady rhythm of his own breathing. The nightmare had shown him a false harbor built on control. But here, in the awkward, cramped, unconditional warmth of her embrace, he had finally found a real one.

## Chapter 2: The First Morning

### Part 1: No Privacy

Jordan’s first sensation upon waking on this Sunday morning was warmth. It was a deep, soothing heat that seemed to emanate from behind him, blanketing his entire body. For a moment, he was disoriented. He wasn’t stiff, he wasn’t alone, and the recurring nightmare of falling was absent, replaced by a profound sense of being anchored. He slowly became aware of an arm wrapped firmly but gently around his waist, and the soft, steady rhythm of breathing against his neck. The memories of the night flooded back—the nightmare, the whimpering, and then… Aubrie.

He didn’t dare move. The hug she had given him yesterday during his meltdown… that was the first time anyone had ever hugged him. Ever. His mother’s touch was always devoid of affection. That hug had been a revelation, a powerful surge of security he’d never known. This, waking up wrapped in her arms, was an extension of that miracle.

A few moments later, Aubrie stirred behind him. She blinked, her mind registering the cramped space. A blush crept up her neck, but her immediate, instinctive reaction was concern, amplified by the crush that had been blooming since she first saw him. "Are you okay?" she whispered, her voice still thick with sleep. "You were having a nightmare."

Jordan managed to turn his head slightly. "I'm okay," he said, his voice filled with a gratitude so immense it couldn't be contained in words. "Thank you."

A cheerful voice from above broke the quiet intimacy. "Well, look at you two."

They both looked up. Madison was leaning over the edge of her top bunk, a wide, knowing smirk on her face. *Called it,* Madison thought, silently teasing her new roommate. She’d seen the way Aubrie’s eyes lit up when Jordan talked about astrophysics. She recognized the look. And now, finding her cozied up in his bed? It wasn’t just about comfort; it was a full-blown crush in action. *Guess the 'no privacy' rule is in full effect already.*

Aubrie, catching Madison’s look, felt her blush deepen but held her ground. "He was cold," she said defensively, as if it were the most obvious explanation in the world.

Madison didn't buy it for a second. She rolled her eyes playfully and let out a small giggle. "Oh, I'm *sure* he was," she teased.

Aubrie’s face bloomed into a deep crimson blush. She was caught, but she wasn't about to admit it. Refusing to make eye contact with Madison, she gave Jordan’s shoulder a final, gentle squeeze that lingered just a second too long before she carefully untangled herself from the narrow bed.

The morning proceeded with the now-established controlled chaos of six people navigating a small space. The shower situation was raised by Zoe, and the logical solution was the accessible shower room. Madison, who also found herself fancying the quiet, kind boy in the wheelchair, readily volunteered alongside Aubrie.

In the spacious, tiled shower room, the reality of their situation was laid bare. As Aubrie and Madison helped Jordan, their motivations added a palpable layer of tenderness to their actions. For Aubrie, every touch was infused with a burgeoning affection. For Madison, her practical nature was softened by a genuine fondness.

For Jordan, the experience was earth-shattering. As they helped him out of his clothes, he was confronted with the clinical fact of their nudity. It was the first time he had ever seen a naked female, and the sight was so wrapped up in an act of profound kindness that it bypassed awkwardness and went straight to a feeling of overwhelming trust. They were helping him, and in her own way, Madison was also fancing him, but he didn't know that. All he knew was that they were there for him, and their care was a balm on the lifetime of loneliness he had endured.

Back in the suite, clean and dressed, the sense of camaraderie was stronger than ever. Madison knelt to tie his shoes, her movements efficient but gentle. When she finished, she looked up and smiled. "All set."

Jordan looked at her, then at Aubrie, then towards the door of Room 216 where the others were. He thought of the hug, of waking up safe in Aubrie’s arms. He wanted—no, *needed*—to know that feeling again. He took a breath, his heart pounding with the audacity of his request.

"Madison?" he asked, his voice barely a whisper. "Can… can I have a hug, too?"

Her cheerful expression softened instantly. "Of course, sweetie." She leaned forward, wrapping her arms around his shoulders and pulling him into a warm, strong embrace. It was different from Aubrie's, more solid and straightforward, but just as wonderful.

Lauren and Hannah emerged from their room, drawn by the quiet moment. "What's going on?" Lauren asked with a smile. "Jordan's decided he's a hugger," Madison said, pulling back but keeping a hand on his shoulder.

Jordan felt his face heat up, but he didn't back down. He looked at the other girls. Lauren, who had a longtime boyfriend, beamed and stepped forward immediately. "I give great hugs!" she declared, leaning down to give him a cheerful, sisterly squeeze. "Almost as good as my boyfriend, Tom." Hannah, who was also in a relationship, followed suit, her hug quieter but no less warm.

Finally, Zoe, who had been observing with a small smile, stepped up. "My turn, I guess," she said, her tone pragmatic as always, but her arms, when they wrapped around him, were firm and deeply reassuring.

Five hugs. Five different girls. Each one a gift. As Madison took the handles of his chair to head for breakfast, Jordan felt an incredible lightness. He was surrounded by a fortress of friendship. In Room 214, a gentle, unspoken romantic tension was beginning to simmer between three roommates. And from Room 216, a wave of solid, unwavering platonic support. He was learning, for the first time, what it felt like to be wanted.

### Part 2: Worlds Aligned

The university cafeteria on a Sunday morning was a low hum of activity. As the six of them found a large table by a window, the easy chatter of the group resumed. But a subtle shift was occurring. While Madison, Zoe, Lauren, and Hannah discussed class schedules and debated the merits of different coffee creamers, a quiet, powerful gravity began to pull Jordan and Aubrie into their own orbit.

It started, as it always did with them, with the stars.

"I was looking over the syllabus for Astronomy 101 again last night," Aubrie began, her voice a little softer now that she was speaking just to him. "Dr. Alistair is starting with the life cycle of stars. I can't wait to get into nebula formation".

Jordan’s small smile faded. He flinched, his gaze dropping to the table. "Astrophysics..." he mumbled, the word tasting like failure. "I... I'm really worried about that class. My mom... she just... she signed me up for it. I don't know anything about it. The math..."

He trailed off, the full weight of his mother's trap settling on him. He had been set up to fail.

Aubrie looked at him, her expression shifting from simple excitement to a deep, immediate understanding. She saw his fear. "Hey," she said, leaning forward, her voice quiet but certain. "It's okay. People make it sound harder than it is. It's not just math, Jordan. It's... it's about the patterns."

She saw his gaze flicker up, and she pressed on, speaking their shared, rare language. "People are confusing, right? The rules are always changing". "But the stars... the laws of physics, the way a star collapses into a white dwarf... it’s a process. It has an order. You can understand it".

This was it. This was the core of their connection. For them, being autistic meant finding solace in systems, in the deep, comforting logic of things that made sense. She was handing him a lifeline.

"Look," she said, her voice full of a new promise. "It's my favorite subject. I'm not going to let you fail. We're in the same class, and the same lab. We'll sit together. We'll study together. I'll be right there with you. I promise."

Jordan looked up at her, his expression full of a fragile, dawning hope. His mother had pushed him into an insurmountable maze, and this girl had just handed him a map and a flashlight. *She'll be right there with me.* This was the light at the end of the tunnel. He saw in her a passion he didn't have, but a logic he understood. She saw in him a mind that worked just like hers, one that *could* understand, if just given the chance.

Across the table, Madison watched them, a knowing, affectionate smile playing on her lips. She nudged Zoe gently. "Look at them," she whispered. "They're in their own little nebula over there".

Zoe observed them, her analytical mind cataloging the data. The lowered voices, the intense eye contact, the way he leaned into her space as she spoke. "A strong neuro-social bond is forming," she stated, her clinical observation barely masking her own complex feelings. "His anxiety is visibly decreasing. It's a predictable and positive outcome".

Lauren, overhearing, just smiled. "It's sweet".

The bubble was finally, gently pierced when Madison decided to tease. "You two are going to be insufferable in that class, you know that? The professor is going to ask a question and you'll both just start speaking fluent astrophysics to each other".

Aubrie’s head snapped up, a deep blush coloring her cheeks as she realized how engrossed they had been. "Oh! No, we were just… talking about the syllabus".

"Mmhmm, the 'syllabus'," Madison said with a wink.

Aubrie wasn't ready to admit the depth of what she was feeling. It was too new, too potent. "He's just... easy to talk to," she deflected, busying herself with her orange juice.

After breakfast, as they walked across the main quad to locate their classroom buildings for the next day, Aubrie naturally fell into place beside Jordan's wheelchair. The crisp autumn air was a refreshing change from the stuffy cafeteria.

"You really... like it that much?" Jordan asked quietly, his voice full of a genuine curiosity. "The stars?"

Aubrie didn't have to think. "I do," she said, looking up at the blue sky. "When you look at the stars, I feel small," she said. "But in a good way. Like all the things that are hard down here... they don't matter as much, because we're all just spinning around on a tiny rock in this huge, incredible place".

He looked at her, his expression full of wonder. She wasn't just offering to help him pass; she was offering him a sanctuary. She was showing him the beauty his mother had tried to use as a weapon.

"I... I'd like to feel that way," he said.

As he spoke, his hand, which had been resting on the arm of his chair, brushed against hers as she walked. The contact was brief, accidental, but it sent a jolt of electricity through them both. They pulled their hands away quickly, a shared, shy smile passing between them.

In that look, a universe of unspoken things was communicated. It was a silent acknowledgment that they were two strangers from different worlds who had, against all odds, found they were made of the same stardust.

### Part 3: A Different Kind of Constellation

They returned to the suite after their campus walk, the air still buzzing with the energy of the day. Jordan, feeling the pleasant exhaustion of social and physical activity, retreated to his room to quietly organize his textbooks, leaving the five young women to congregate in Room 216. They collapsed onto the various beds, a comfortable silence settling over them for a moment.

It was Madison who broke it, her voice gentle but direct as she looked at Aubrie. "Okay. Teasing aside. Are we going to talk about the massive gravitational pull happening between you and Jordan, or just let it become a silent supernova?"

Aubrie, who was sitting on the floor leaning against her bunk, hugged her knees to her chest. The blush that rose to her cheeks was faint this time, replaced by a look of deep, serious contemplation. She took a breath, and instead of deflecting, she finally let the truth surface.

"I am," she said, her voice barely above a whisper, yet it filled the room. "I'm completely gone for him. More than I thought was possible this quickly." She looked up, her gaze sweeping over her friends. "It feels... important. Like he's a puzzle piece I didn't know I was missing. But..." She paused, choosing her next words carefully. "I don't think he's just *my* puzzle piece."

Her eyes settled on Madison, then on Zoe. "I see it with you guys, too. I see the way you look at him. And... the strange thing is, it doesn't make me feel angry. It doesn't make me feel jealous."

"It doesn't?" Zoe asked, her voice laced with genuine surprise. Her own logical mind had been trying to categorize her feelings, and jealousy seemed like a predictable, if undesirable, variable.

"No," Aubrie affirmed, shaking her head. "Jealousy feels selfish. And nothing about what we're building here feels selfish. It's the opposite of that." She leaned forward, her passion making her words resonate with a powerful clarity. "Think about him. He's spent his entire life being made to feel like a burden. How could we possibly turn around and make him the center of a bunch of drama or competition? It would just be another way of making him feel like a problem to be solved."

Lauren, who was sharing the single bed with Hannah, nodded thoughtfully. "She has a point. I mean, Hannah and I both have boyfriends, and we love them, but the way we all feel about Jordan is... different. It’s not romantic for us, it’s… protective."

"Exactly," Hannah agreed, grateful Lauren had voiced it perfectly. "It's like he's our family. You don't get jealous over family, you just want them to be happy and safe."

"Right!" Aubrie said, seizing on their words. "So if he's happy talking to Madison, or feels safe with Zoe, or cared for by me... that's all that matters. His happiness is the goal. I don't need to hog him to be part of that. In fact, it feels better not to."

Madison looked at Aubrie, her expression full of a newfound respect. "That's... incredibly mature, Aubrie. And you're right. His happiness comes first. Period."

Zoe nodded slowly, her internal conflict settling into a new, logical framework. "An emotional support system is stronger when it's a network rather than a single point of contact. A cooperative dynamic minimizes conflict and maximizes the subject's well-being." It was the most Zoe way imaginable of saying, *I agree, and I love the idea.*

A quiet, powerful understanding bloomed in the room. It wasn't a formal pact, not yet. But it was a silent agreement, a shared philosophy. They were not rivals for Jordan's affection. They were a constellation, each a different point of light, and their collective purpose was to illuminate his world. Through the open doorway, they could see him in his room, carefully placing a label on a binder, completely unaware that the five women next door had just fundamentally defined the kind, open-hearted, and extraordinary terms of his new life.

## Chapter 3: First Days and Shared Syllabi

### Part 1: Monday – The Shared Mountain

The first Monday of the semester arrived with a nervous, electric energy. The six suitemates, having agreed to walk to their first classes together for moral support, moved as a single, cohesive unit through the bustling campus walkways. While the others peeled off towards buildings for their own classes, Aubrie and Jordan headed for the science complex. Jordan’s anxiety was a low, thrumming hum, but Aubrie’s presence at his side was a steady, calming force.

Their 9:00 AM Astronomy 101 class was in a large, tiered lecture hall already buzzing with the chatter of a hundred other freshmen. As they had planned, they found two seats together in the accessible row near the front.

Jordan stared at the massive projection screen and the models of planets hanging from the ceiling, his hands gripping the armrests of his chair. This was it. His mother’s weapon. The insurmountable mountain she had chosen for him, designed to make him fail.

"This is... a lot," he whispered, his voice tight with anxiety.

"It's okay," Aubrie beamed back, her own excitement a shield against his fear. "It's the real deal. No more high school physics". She saw his panic and gently, discreetly, took his hand under the table. Her grip was firm and certain. "We're in this together, remember? I've got you. I'm not going to let you fail".

Jordan looked at her, at this girl who had climbed onto the mountain just to keep him company. He took a shaky breath and squeezed her hand. With her by his side, he felt like he *could* get through this.

When their professor, Dr. Alistair, began the lecture, a comfortable silence fell between them. For Aubrie, this was her sanctuary, a place where the universe operated on clear, understandable laws. For Jordan, it was a terrifying, new language.

All he knew of the stars before this moment was the small comfort of the glow-in-the-dark plastic stars his mother had stuck on his ceiling as a child. They were a flat, pale imitation. His only other connection was the quiet, lonely act of looking out his bedroom window in Hampton Roads, wondering what those pinpricks of light in the night sky really were.

Now, Dr. Alistair was speaking about stellar nurseries and hydrostatic equilibrium, and Jordan’s anxiety began to spike. He didn't understand. He couldn't follow. This was it. He was going to fail.

He felt a sudden, gentle pressure. Aubrie, sensing his rising panic, discreetly laced her fingers with his. Her hand was warm, her grip firm and steady. She was a grounding presence, a silent, physical promise that he was not alone.

He took a shaky breath and forced himself to listen, not to the complex words, but to the *tone* of the lecture. Dr. Alistair wasn't trying to trick anyone; he was just excited. And beside him, Aubrie was fascinated, hanging on every word.

Jordan looked at their joined hands. He looked at Aubrie's rapt, happy expression. She was his safe person, and she *loved* this. She seemed to really like him, and she had promised to help him. Slowly, the terror in his chest began to recede. His mother’s weapon, this impossible, terrifying subject, suddenly felt just a little less scary.

When Dr. Alistair dismissed the class, the spell was broken. The lecture hall erupted back into noise as students began packing their bags. Jordan let out a breath he felt like he'd been holding for fifty minutes. He had survived. He hadn't understood everything, not even close, but he hadn't panicked.

He looked at Aubrie, who was finally letting go of his hand to gather her notebook. "See?" she whispered, her smile bright. "Not so bad."

He managed a small, genuine smile back. "Not so bad," he agreed.

For the first time, he was actually looking forward to the Friday lab section, even knowing it wouldn't start until the second week of class. His mother's insurmountable mountain suddenly seemed... climbable. He had someone to climb the mountain with.

As they moved out into the hallway, the crowd of students jostled past them. Jordan tensed, but Aubrie, seeing his unease, stepped in front of him, acting as a small buffer. He felt relieved.

"I guess we have to go our separate ways for our other classes," she said, her own expression a little sad at the thought.

"Yeah," Jordan confirmed, his heart sinking a little.

"Well," she said, and then, right there in the crowded hallway, she offered him another hug. This one was different from the first. It wasn't a desperate, grounding act in the middle of a meltdown. It was a warm, reassuring promise. *I'll be back.*

He held on for a moment, absorbing her steadying presence. As she pulled back, she looked at him, not wanting the connection to break.

"Are you free for lunch?" she asked.

He looked at his schedule, his heart giving a small, happy jolt. "Uh, yeah. I am. At noon.".

"Perfect," she beamed. "Meet me at the dining hall? Newman Commons?".

"Yeah. Okay. Lunch," he said, a real smile spreading across his face.

"It's a date," she said, then blushed. "I mean... not a... you know. I'll just see you then."

As she turned and walked away, Jordan watched her go. He still had to face the rest of his morning classes alone, a prospect that still terrified him. But as he maneuvered his wheelchair toward his next building, his mind was at ease.

He had a schedule. He had a *system*.

He knew he would see her Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings for class, and on Fridays for their lab. He knew he would see her in the dorm with him tonight. And now, he knew he would see her in just a couple of hours, for lunch.

His mother's weapon had been defused.

### Part 2: The Broken Elevator

It was noon. Jordan wheeled himself toward Newman Commons, his stomach tight with a mix of hunger and anxiety. This was his first time navigating to a dining hall by himself. He had been there with the group, but this felt different, a small test of the independence he wasn't sure he had.

Newman Commons, like Bennett Hall, was a massive brick building built into one of the campus's notorious hills, with entrances on different levels. He found an accessible entrance on the lower level, near the delivery bays, but he saw no clear path to the main dining area above. He was lost.

His mPhone buzzed in his pocket. It was a text from Aubrie.

Aubrie: Hey! I’m here. Just inside the main entrance, by the stairs. Waiting for you! :)

Jordan’s heart sank. He was at the *delivery* entrance, not the one they had used as a group. He was clearly in the wrong place. He texted her back, his hands fumbling slightly.

Jordan: I think I'm at a different door. The lower level? I'm looking for the elevator but I don't see one.

He wheeled back and forth on the concrete patio, feeling a small jolt of panic. A cafeteria worker, pushing a large cart of supplies, saw him looking lost.

"You looking for the way in, honey?" she asked kindly.

"Yes," Jordan said, relieved. "I'm supposed to meet my friend, but I can't find the elevator."

The worker sighed, her face full of sympathy. "Ah, yeah, that's because the main one's broken. Has been all morning". "Hang on, let me get my manager. He can take you up the freight elevator".

A moment later, a man in a polo shirt with a manager's name tag came out. "Hi there," he said. "Sorry about our elevator. Let's get you the scenic route".

He badged them through a heavy door marked "Staff Only." Jordan followed him into a maze of loud, steamy kitchens. The clanging of pots, the hiss of industrial dishwashers, and the shouting of the line cooks was a sensory assault. The manager led him to a large, metal freight elevator and swiped a key. They rode up, and when the heavy doors opened again, Jordan found himself emerging from an unmarked employee door, right into the main, chaotic dining area.

He saw her immediately. Aubrie was standing right where she said she'd be, looking anxiously toward the entrance.

Her face lit up when she saw him, but her smile faltered as she saw *his* expression.

Jordan looked at her, and the anxiety from the broken elevator, the sensory overload of the kitchen, and the sheer frustration of being lost all combined into a deeper, more profound terror. *What if this isn't real?* The brief, terrifying moment of being lost, of being unable to find her, had felt too much like his old life. He was terrified that this was all a dream, a cruel trick, and that he would wake up any second, back in his mother's apartment, forever at her mercy.

Aubrie saw the raw fright on his face. She didn't know the full cause, only that her friend was scared. She didn't hesitate. She rushed to him, closing the distance, and pulled him into a very, very meaningful hug, holding him tightly, her arms a firm, grounding anchor, right there in the middle of the dining hall.

"Hey," she whispered, her voice fierce. "You're okay. You found me. You're safe."

She felt him trembling, and her heart ached. She realized something was deeply scaring him, more than just a broken elevator. She wished, with a sudden, powerful longing, that she could just stay with him, be his shield for the rest of the day. But she knew she couldn't. They both had their respective afternoon classes.

"Come on," she said, finally pulling back but keeping her hand on his shoulder. "Let's go get lunch. Together".

### Part 3: The First Choice

The hug in the middle of the dining hall had grounded him. Jordan let out a shaky breath as Aubrie pulled back, though she kept a firm, reassuring hand on his shoulder. "Come on," she said, her voice bright. "Let's get lunch. Together."

She pushed his wheelchair toward the long, winding serving lines. The noise and smell of the food was overwhelming. "Hey," Aubrie said, noticing him trying to figure out how to navigate the line with a tray. "It's a pain to try and balance a tray in your lap. Let me just grab yours for you. What do you want?"

Jordan looked up at the massive, illuminated menus above the stations. Pizza. Pasta. A deli. A grill. He froze. He was completely, utterly overwhelmed by the choices. His entire life, food had been a simple, non-negotiable command. His mother made something, and he ate it. If he didn't like what Aubrie Sinclair was serving, he didn't eat. He was never *asked* what he wanted.

"Jordan?" Aubrie asked gently, seeing him staring blankly. "You okay?"

He spotted it. At the grill station, a student was flipping burgers on a flat top. A basket of fresh, golden french fries was right next to it. His mother *never* made burgers, and fries were a rare, frozen-bag-in-the-oven affair. He pointed, his voice small. "Can I... can I have that? A cheeseburger? And... fries?".

"A burger? Heck yeah. Good choice," Aubrie said, not blinking an eye. She had no idea she had just approved two items his mother would have denied him. "What do you want on it?"

He was stunned. "Uh... everything?"

"You got it. One cheeseburger with everything, and a side of fries," she called to the worker. "What about a drink?".

He pointed to the soda fountains, another battleground he'd always lost. "A... a soda?".

"Perfect." She grabbed a cup, filled it with soda, and placed it on the tray next to the burger and fries. She then quickly grabbed her own lunch—a large salad and a water.

They arrived at the register. Jordan, still reeling from the sheer ease of his choices, fumbled in his pocket for his new student ID, nervous about paying.

"Don't worry about it," Aubrie said, already handing her own ID to the cashier. "Put us both on my plan, please".

The cashier swiped, and it was done. Aubrie balanced the two trays and began steering them toward a table. Jordan was speechless. She had just bought him lunch—a meal *he* chose—without a second thought, without a lecture on cost, without a single sigh of inconvenience. She just... *did* it. She was showing this scared boy in a wheelchair that someone *did* care about him, and that he might, just maybe, have found a safe harbor.

### Part 4: Tuesday - An Unexpected Dimension

On Tuesday, the group discovered another strange twist of fate. Madison was the first to arrive for her 10:00 AM Health 201 class. A moment later, Lauren and Hannah walked in.

"No way," Lauren said, laughing. "You're in this class too?"

"It fulfilled the physical education requirement and fit my schedule," Zoe stated, walking up behind them as if it were the most logical thing in the world.

Just as the five of them were marveling at the coincidence, Aubrie and Jordan came through the door. The room erupted in quiet, disbelieving laughter. All six of them, from different majors and backgrounds, had independently signed up for the same two-credit elective: "HSC 201: Dimensions of Human Sexuality."

The professor began by passing out the syllabus. As the suitemates scanned the schedule of topics, they saw the expected units on nutrition, substance abuse, and mental health. Then, their eyes fell on the mid-semester schedule: *Week 7: The Male Reproductive System (Textbook, Ch. 5)* *Week 8: The Female Reproductive System (Textbook, Ch. 6)*

Madison shot a playful, wide-eyed look at Lauren, who was trying very hard not to giggle. But Jordan wasn't laughing. He was staring at the words with an expression of pure, unadulterated curiosity.

His entire life, this information had been forbidden territory. Every year in public school, his mother had diligently checked the box on the permission slip, opting him out of the Family Life and Sexual Education units. She called the topics "inappropriate for him." While his classmates went to health class, he was sent to the library to read. But here it was, listed plainly in a college syllabus. It wasn't inappropriate; it was just biology. It was just another system, like the solar system. And for the first time in his life, no one was going to send him to the library. A quiet, profound sense of freedom settled over him.

### Part 5: An Unconventional Study Session

That Tuesday evening, after their first full day of both classes, the group was relaxing in the suite. The health class, however, was still on Jordan's mind. He wheeled himself over to where the girls were gathered in Room 214, holding the "Dimensions of Human Sexuality" syllabus like a mysterious map.

"That health class today," he began, his voice hesitant but earnest. "All those chapter titles... about the human body... I've never learned about any of that. My mother forcibly opted me out of those classes every year."

A deep, collective empathy filled the room. They understood this wasn't just about academics; it was about a fundamental part of his education that had been denied to him. It was Aubrie who, looking at the confusing diagrams in the open textbook on her desk, formulated a plan rooted in the unique trust their family had built.

"The diagrams in the book are just flat drawings, Jordan. It's hard to learn from that," she said matter-of-factly. "We could... help you. It would be easier to remember if you could connect the clinical terms to physical reality."

The offer was radical, generous, and born of pure, practical kindness. Zoe and Madison nodded in immediate agreement.

Hannah and Lauren, out of respect for their boyfriends, lovingly opted out. "That is an incredibly sweet and amazing thing to do for him," Hannah said. "Just knock on the connecting door when you guys are done." With that, the two of them retreated to Room 216, closing the door behind them.

Jordan looked at the three remaining women, a mix of nervousness and profound gratitude on his face. Aubrie gave him a reassuring smile. "Ready for your first real health class?"

Aubrie moved first, her demeanor calm and entirely devoid of the charged tension that usually accompanied such vulnerability. With practiced efficiency, she unbuttoned her shirt and stepped out of her jeans, leaving them neatly folded on the edge of the bed. Madison and Zoe followed suit, their expressions reflecting only supportive patience.

Jordan sat frozen in his chair, his hands gripping the armrests. He had never seen a woman unclothed, and the sheer reality of it, standing right in front of him, was overwhelming. But there was no seduction in their posture; they stood before him with the objective openness of a medical demonstration.

"It's okay to look, Jordan," Aubrie said softly, sitting on the edge of the mattress, directly across from his chair. She picked up the heavy textbook from the desk and laid it open on her lap, pointing to the anatomical diagram of the female reproductive system.

"The first thing to understand," Madison began, stepping beside Aubrie and adopting the steady, informative tone of a seasoned clinical lecturer, "is that the external female genitalia is collectively referred to as the vulva. The drawings in your book separate everything with distinct lines and bright colors, but in reality, the epithelial and mucosal tissues flow continuously into one another."

Jordan leaned forward slightly, his eyes moving between the diagram on the glossy page and the physical reality in front of him.

Madison gently guided his hand. "These outer folds of adipose tissue and skin," she explained, her touch clinical and precise, "are the labia majora. They contain sebaceous glands and protect the more delicate inner structures."

Jordan traced the line in the book, then looked back, his expression one of intense, respectful concentration. The paralyzing mystery that had been enforced upon him for years was slowly unraveling into simple, biological truth.

"And medial to those," Zoe added, leaning over to point at the diagram, "are the labia minora. These are the inner, hairless mucosal folds. The textbook makes them look perfectly symmetrical, but as you can visually verify, biological morphology is highly varied in size and shape. Anteriorly, they merge to form the prepuce, and they serve to enclose and protect the urethral and vaginal openings."

Aubrie guided Jordan's index finger, placing it gently at the very top apex of the structure. "This," she said calmly, "is the clitoral hood, or prepuce, which protects the glans clitoris. The glans is the external, visible portion of a much larger internal erectile network. While the textbook focuses heavily on the reproductive cycle, this specific structure's primary biological function is somatosensory."

Madison took over the instruction, her voice steady as she directed his attention downward. "The space enclosed by the labia minora is called the vulval vestibule. Directly below the clitoris is the external urethral orifice, or meatus. It is the terminus of the urinary tract, used exclusively for the expulsion of urine."

She pointed just below it. "And posterior to the urethra is the vaginal opening, known medically as the introitus. This is the entrance to the vaginal canal, a fibromuscular tube connecting the vulva to the internal cervix and uterus. It serves as the outflow tract for menstruation and the birth canal. Finally, the area of tissue separating the introitus from the anus is the perineum."

Jordan exhaled a long breath, a look of profound understanding washing over his features. The terrifying, forbidden unknown his mother had weaponized against him was suddenly just... anatomy. Tissue, function, physiology, and biology. There was no arousal, no crossing of boundaries—only an intense, grounding focus.

"It all connects," he murmured, looking at the three women who trusted him enough to demystify the world. "The diagram makes it look like separate, disparate mechanical parts, but it's a single, continuous, integrated physiological system."

"Exactly," Aubrie said with a warm, encouraging smile.

An hour later, the lesson—one conducted with more gentle patience and clinical respect than any university professor could have managed—was over. The atmosphere in Room 214 was calm and studious. The textbook was closed on the desk, its mysteries now significantly clearer.

Jordan let out a long breath, a look of genuine understanding on his face. "Thank you," he said, his voice thick with an emotion that went far beyond academics. "That makes so much more sense than the drawings."

"Any time," Madison said with a cheerful smile as the women began to pull their clothes back on. "That's what we're here for."

Aubrie walked to the connecting door, knocking gently. "We're done!" she called out.

A moment later, the door opened and Hannah and Lauren rejoined the group, their expressions open and friendly, with no hint of awkwardness. The family was whole again.

"Well," Zoe said, glancing at the clock on the wall. "After that successful study session, I believe my stomach is requesting dinner."

A chorus of agreement went through the room, and the six of them headed out together towards the cafeteria, their bond of trust now deeper and more unique than ever before.

### Part 6: Diverging Paths

The group of six exited the dormitory together, a wave of cheerful chatter washing over them as they walked out into the cool evening air. The decision to get dinner had been unanimous, and the mood was light.

"Alright, guys," Hannah said, giving a happy wave as they reached the main campus path. "Mark is waiting for me by the library. I'll catch up with you all later tonight!" She broke off from the group, heading down a branching brick walkway.

"Tom and I are meeting up, too," Lauren added, feeling happy and looking forward to seeing him. "I'll see you back at the suite!" She gave a wave of her own and headed in the opposite direction, toward the main quad.

That left the remaining four—Jordan, Aubrie, Madison, and Zoe—to continue their journey toward the distant, welcoming lights of the cafeteria.

Lauren made her way to the specific bench on the quad where she and Tom always met. It was unoccupied. "He's probably just running a few minutes late," she murmured to herself, pulling out her phone to scroll through social media as she waited. Five minutes passed, then ten. The steady stream of students walking by began to thin as people settled in for dinner.

A small frown touched her lips. This wasn't like him. She sent a quick text: Hey, I'm at our bench. Where are you?

She waited another five minutes, her eyes scanning the quad. No reply. A small pang of disappointment hit her. She tried not to feel annoyed; maybe he got held up by a professor, or his phone died. Shrugging it off with a sigh, she decided she wasn't going to wait around all night. Her friends were waiting.

Back in the bustling cafeteria, Jordan, Aubrie, Madison, and Zoe were just sitting down with their trays when Lauren walked up to their table.

"Hey, I thought you were eating with Tom?" Madison asked, looking up in surprise.

"Yeah, he must have gotten held up or something," Lauren said, trying to sound more casual than she felt as she set her bag down. "He wasn't there and he's not answering his phone. Oh well," she finished with a slightly forced smile, "his loss! That means I don't have to share my fries."

The group welcomed her back without a second thought, Aubrie giving her a comforting smile as they all began to eat. To them, it was just a minor change of plans. As they settled into their meal, the disappointment Lauren felt began to fade, replaced by the easy camaraderie of her new family. There was no sign of Tom anywhere in the crowded dining hall, but she pushed the thought aside and dove into the conversation.

"So, first two days in the books," Madison said through a mouthful of salad. "Verdict?"

"My Advanced Calculus professor is sufficiently challenging," Zoe reported. "The syllabus is well-structured."

"Dr. Alistair is even better than I hoped," Aubrie said, her eyes lighting up as she looked at Jordan. "He actually explained the Schwarzschild radius in a way that made perfect sense."

"The lab is going to be amazing," Jordan agreed. "I saw the equipment list."

"My art history class seems cool," Lauren offered, genuinely enjoying herself now. "Lots of reading, but the paintings are incredible."

As they talked and laughed, Lauren, who was sitting next to Jordan, leaned her head on his shoulder for a moment as Madison told a funny story. It felt nice. Comfortable. A small, sad part of her wished it was Tom's shoulder she was leaning on, that he was here sharing this moment. But he was probably just stuck in a meeting with an instructor, his phone dead. He'd have a good reason. She was sure of it. For now, this was more than okay. This was good. She gave Jordan's arm a friendly, appreciative squeeze, and he smiled back, the five of them a happy little island in the noisy room.

## Chapter 4: The Jerk and The Pact

### Part 1: The Call

The first official day of classes, a Monday, had been a whirlwind. That evening, the six suitemates were scattered throughout the suite, a comfortable, domestic energy settling over them as they worked on assignments. In Room 216, Lauren was on a video call with her boyfriend, Tom, happy to finally connect after a busy day.

"...and my last lecture finished late, so I had to sprint to the dining hall before it closed," Lauren finished, laughing. "It was a crazy day. How was yours?"

"Boring without you," Tom said, smiling. "So, how are the roommates? Not driving you crazy yet?"

"Never! They're the best," Lauren said, her affection for her new friends genuine. "Actually, the most unbelievable thing happened. You're not going to believe it. One of our suitemates... is a guy."

She said it with a laugh, trying to convey the sheer strangeness of the situation. But on the screen, Tom’s smile tightened. "A guy? What are you talking about, Lauren?" The warmth in his voice vanished, replaced by a sharp, suspicious edge.

Lauren’s stomach dropped, but she pushed forward, determined to make him understand the truth. "Okay, just listen, because it's a wild story. Housing made a huge mistake with his name—it's Aubrey, but he goes by Jordan—and they accidentally assigned him to our suite."

"So they moved him, right?" Tom interrupted, his tone demanding.

"Well, no," Lauren said, trying to keep her voice calm. "He's in a wheelchair, and it's the only accessible room on the floor. And honestly, Tom... something amazing happened. He met my roommate, Aubrie. And they have just... completely fallen for each other. It was like... instant. I've never seen anything like it. They're perfect together. So, we all just decided to make it work. It's not weird, it's actually really, really sweet."

She looked at him earnestly, hoping he would see the beauty in the strange situation. Instead, he stared at her, his face twisting into a mask of disbelief.

"Fallen for each other?" he scoffed. "You've been there for what, three days? You expect me to believe in some fairytale love-at-first-sight story? You think I'm an idiot?"

"It's not a fairytale, Tom, it's the truth! It happened!" Lauren insisted, her voice rising with frantic energy. "If you could see them together, you'd understand!"

"Oh, I think I understand perfectly," he sneered, his voice dripping with a venom that shocked her. "It sounds like a convenient story you all cooked up to make me sound like the bad guy for questioning it. What’s really going on over there, Lauren? Is this guy just making his way through the whole suite? Is that it?"

"What? NO!" Lauren cried, horrified by the accusation. "How could you even say that? I love YOU!"

"It doesn't sound like it," Tom spat, his face a mask of cold fury. "It sounds like you're in some weird cult, and I'm not sticking around to find out what happens next. We're done."

And the screen went black. Lauren stared at the blank phone, her breath catching in a choked sob. A moment later, the screen lit up again, not with a call, but with a text message from Tom, stark and cruel against the dark background: Meet me in the lobby. I’m giving you your stuff back. NOW.

### Part 2: The Humiliation

Lauren walked into the main dorm lobby like a zombie, her heart pounding. Tom was standing there by the vending machines, holding a small box of her things. Several students were milling about, chatting and studying. Many of them were Bennett Hall residents who knew the "worst-kept secret" of the dorm: the story about the clerical error in Suite 214/216 was completely true.

When Tom saw her, he didn't lower his voice. He raised it.
