The middle of October brought a chill to the air and the welcome promise of Fall Break. The dorms were not closing for the short Saturday-to-Tuesday respite, and the suite was buzzing with low-key plans—a horror movie marathon, a trip to the pumpkin patch, and a massive, collaborative dinner.

Amidst the cheerful planning, however, Jordan grew quiet and withdrawn. His smiles were strained, and a familiar anxiety settled in his eyes. Madison, ever observant, cornered him by his desk on Friday afternoon.

"Okay, spill it," she said gently. "What's going on? You've been a million miles away all day."

Jordan sighed, the sound heavy with dread. "My mother is coming tomorrow morning," he said, his voice low. "To take me home for the break."

"Oh," Madison said, her cheerful demeanor softening into understanding. "And you don't want to go."

"I can't," Jordan admitted, the words tumbling out. "Every time I talk to her on the phone, she calls me Aubrey, asks when I'm going to stop this 'silly college experiment' and come home for good. She doesn't get it. She doesn't get *us*." He looked around the room, at the comfortable mess of their shared lives. "This is my home now. You guys... you're my family."

Just then, Aubrie Jean walked in and caught the end of his sentence. She came over and placed a hand on his shoulder. "Then you should stay here for the break," she said simply. "With your family. You're an adult, Jordan. The dorms are open. You don't have to go anywhere you don't want to go."

The idea was terrifying, but it was also a lifeline. For the first time, he realized he had a choice.

### Part 2: The Confrontation

Saturday morning arrived not with a knock, but with the buzz of Jordan's phone on his desk. The caller ID read "Mom." He took a deep breath, glancing at Aubrie and Madison, who offered him small, encouraging nods. He answered.

"Aubrey, I'm downstairs in the lobby," his mother's voice chirped, sharp and expectant. "Come down and let me in. We need to get you packed."

"It's Jordan," he said, his own voice quiet but firm. "And I'm not coming down, Mom. I'm staying here for the break."

There was a moment of stunned silence on the other end, followed by an icy shift in tone. "We are not having this discussion over the phone. I am your mother. You will come down here this instant." She hung up before he could reply.

Down in the lobby, Jordan's mother fumed. She paced in front of the locked glass doors that led to the residential hallways, her plan foiled by a university card reader. She was not used to being denied. She watched for several minutes until a freshman boy, his arms full of laundry, came up to the door and swiped his card. As the lock clicked open, he struggled to pull the heavy door.

Seeing her opportunity, Jordan's mother rushed forward, putting on her sweetest, most helpless-looking face. "Oh, thank you, dear, could you hold that for me?"

The boy, just trying to be helpful and polite to someone who looked like a visiting parent, smiled and held the door open for her—a direct violation of the dorm's policy against allowing people to "tailgate" into secure areas.

"Thank you so much," she cooed, slipping past him. The moment she was through, her sweet expression vanished. With a determined fire in her eyes, she stormed down the hall to Room 214 and began pounding on the door.

Madison opened it, her eyes widening in shock to see the woman from the lobby now standing in their hallway. Before she could react, Jordan's mother pushed past her and into the room.

"Aubrey!" she snapped, her voice cutting through the quiet room. "What is the meaning of this? Who are all these girls?"

Her eyes darted from Aubrie Kelly to Madison, then to Zoe, Lauren, and Hannah, who had all gathered from Room 216 upon hearing the commotion. Her mind, poisoned by the very fears she had programmed into her son, leaped to the only conclusion she could imagine. "What have you done?" she shrieked, her voice a mix of fury and bitter accusation. "Did you... did you get all of them pregnant?"

Jordan was TERRIFIED. This was his worst nightmare. His mother had found his secret. She was here. She was right. He *was* a burden. He had failed. He flinched, his body curling inward as he looked at the floor, unable to speak. He was "Aubrey" again, a 16-year-old boy being told he had ruined someone's life.

"That's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard," she retorted, taking his silence as guilt. "This little college fantasy is over. You can't take care of yourself. You need to be home with me." She grabbed his empty suitcase from the closet and threw it on his bed. "Pack your things. Now."

He was defeated. This was it. He had failed his experiment. He slowly, numbly, began to wheel his chair toward his dresser. He was going to do what he was told. He was going to crawl back to mommy.

He looked up, his eyes unfocused, and he saw them. He saw Aubrie Kelly, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, and Hannah, all standing there, watching him, their faces not full of judgment, but of fierce, protective anger *at his mother*. They weren't abandoning him. They were his fortress.

The terrified "Aubrey" receded, and Jordan, the competent young man, took control.

He stopped his wheelchair. He turned to face his mother.

"Stop!"

The word was loud, sharp, and commanding. It shocked the room into silence. Aubrie Sinclair flinched, her eyes wide. He had *never* spoken to her like that.

"This is my suite, Mom," Jordan said, his voice shaking but strong. "My name is Jordan. And I am not leaving. You are not welcome here."

"With all due respect, ma'am," Aubrie Jean Kelly said, immediately stepping forward to stand beside Jordan's chair, her presence a silent shield now that he had found his voice. "He's right. Jordan is happy here. He's succeeding. We take care of each other."

Jordan's mother whirled on her. This was the one. This was "Jessica 2.0," the "devil" who had corrupted her son.

"And who are you to tell me what my son needs?" she sneered. "Does he know what a *burden* he is? Are you helping him get dressed? Are you helping him in the bathroom? You'll get tired of it. Everyone does. He's too much for anyone but *me*."

"He's not a burden," Madison said firmly, stepping up to stand on his other side.

"Oh, I see," Sinclair hissed. "Then you're just trying to trap him. You know how *easy* it is to get a girl pregnant! He can't take care of himself, how can he take care of a baby? You're ruining your lives!"

It was her ultimate, practiced attack. But the 16-year-old boy she had programmed to fear this was gone.

A low, angry growl rumbled in Jordan's chest, a sound of pure disapproval for the eighteen years she had sheltered him.

"I know perfectly well how you get a girl pregnant, Mom," he said, his voice cold and steady. "And it is *not* from talking to her or hugging her".

Aubrie Sinclair's face went pale, her attack completely neutralized. What she didn't know was that these women had already given Jordan the education she had so viciously denied him. In their unconventional health lesson, they had sat with him in a safe, nurturing environment and had already taught him EVERYTHING he needed to know about his body, their bodies, and safe sex. Her ultimate weapon was useless.

The argument escalated, his mother's voice growing more shrill as she realized her weapons were failing and her son was not obeying. Finally, Zoe held up a hand. "That's enough," she said coolly. "You are in this room without an escort, which is a violation of building policy. I'm getting the Hall Director."

### Part 3: The Choice

A few minutes later, Kelsey arrived, her expression calm but firm. Jordan's mother immediately tried to appeal to her. "Thank goodness. My son, Aubrey, is being held here against his will by these girls. He needs to come home with me."

Kelsey didn't look at his mother. She looked directly at Jordan. "Jordan," she said, her voice clear and professional. "You are eighteen years old. You are a legal adult and a resident of this university. The dorms are remaining open for the break. It is entirely your decision whether you wish to stay here or leave with your mother for the break. No one can force you to do anything. What do you want to do?"

The entire room held its breath. This was it. The moment of truth. He looked at his mother's furious, controlling face. Then he looked at the five women—and he knew Mark and Sarah were part of this too—who had shown him more kindness and acceptance in a few short months than he had ever known. His chosen family. He thought about a future free from her control, a future where he could just be *Jordan*. A future where he could, perhaps one day, ask Aubrie Jean to marry him, sealing his freedom forever.

He turned back to Kelsey, his voice steady and clear. "I'm staying here."

A strangled gasp came from his mother. "You can't do this to me, Aubrey!"

"His name is Jordan," Kelsey said coolly. "And he has made his decision. You need to leave now, or I will have campus security escort you from the building."

Defeated and shaking with rage, his mother gave him one last venomous glare and stormed out of the room, slamming the door behind her.

A profound, shuddering silence filled the space, and then it was broken as Jordan let out a breath he felt like he'd been holding for eighteen years. He was exhausted, shaking, but he was free. In an instant, his entire family was around him, their arms wrapping him in a massive group hug, grounding him in the joyful, undeniable truth of his new life. He was finally home.

### Part 4: The Cut Cord

An hour after his mother had stormed out of the lobby, a fragile peace had settled over the suite. The air was still thick with the residue of the confrontation, but it was overshadowed by a profound sense of relief. Jordan, emotionally drained but resolute, was surrounded by his friends, who were chattering quietly, trying to restore a sense of normalcy.

"Okay, I'm declaring this an official holiday," Madison announced cheerfully. "Jordan's Independence Day. And it requires celebratory pizza. A lot of it."

"I'll call it in," Jordan said, a small, tired smile on his face. He picked up his phone from his desk to dial the number for the local pizza place. He hit 'send.'

The call immediately failed, a "Call Ended" message flashing on the screen.

"Huh," he said, frowning. He tried again. The same result. He looked at the top of his screen and saw the cellular bars were completely empty, replaced by a "No Service" message. "That's weird. My phone says I have no service. I can't make a call."

"Try restarting it," Lauren suggested. "Sometimes mine does that."

Jordan restarted the phone, but when it powered back on, the result was the same. No service.

"That doesn't make any sense," Zoe said, pulling out her own phone. "We have the same provider, Jordan. Let me check." She glanced at her screen. "No, my service is fine. I have three bars, which is normal for in here."

She had just eliminated every logical possibility except one. The room went silent as the implication of her statement landed like a stone. Zoe looked at Jordan, her expression serious as she asked the question that was now on everyone's mind. "Jordan, who pays your cell phone bill?"

The answer was whispered, but it echoed in the quiet room. "My mother."

"That spiteful witch," Madison breathed, her face a mask of disbelief and fury. "She couldn't take you with her, so she cut you off. She actually cut off your phone."

The reality of the situation sank in. It wasn't a technical glitch; it was a calculated act of punishment. His mother's final attempt to control him by isolating him, by cutting his cord to the outside world.

"This is a significant safety issue," Zoe said immediately, her brain moving past anger and into problem-solving mode. "The building's WiFi is reliable, but when you are between buildings or anywhere on campus without a signal, you now have no means of communication. That's not acceptable."

"So what do we do?" Lauren asked, looking worried. "We can get him on a new plan, but that'll take a day or two."

"Then until we do, he can't be alone," Aubrie said, her voice filled with a fierce, protective certainty. "Ever. One of us has to be with him at all times when he's out of the suite."

"The buddy system," Madison agreed, nodding as the plan formed. "And we'll need a group chat. Whoever is with him just needs to send a quick text. 'Got Jordan, heading to the library,' or 'With J at the student union.' That way, if any of us need to reach him, we know exactly who to call." She pulled out her phone and immediately started creating a new group message, adding all the girls.

"I'll text Mark and Sarah and add them," Hannah said. "They'll be all in, you know they will."

They all looked at Jordan, who was staring at his now-neutered phone, a look of awe on his face. It was a pain. It was complicated and inconvenient. But it was also the most incredible display of love he had ever witnessed. His mother had tried to sever his connection to the world to punish him for choosing his new family. In doing so, she had only forced that family to wrap itself around him even more tightly, creating a web of communication and care so strong that nothing, not even her, could ever break through it.

## Chapter 10: The Fall Break Relay

### Part 1: The New Lifeline and a Winter Pact

Saturday morning marked the official start of Fall Break, but the mood in Suite 214 was one of focused, problem-solving determination. The anger from the previous night had solidified into a protective shield around Jordan.

"Okay," Madison said, pacing the floor. "A new cell plan is the top priority, but none of us really have the money for that right now. That's a long-term problem we'll have to figure out. So, what do we do *now*, for this break?"

"His hardware is functional. The carrier service is what's been terminated," Zoe stated, her fingers already flying across her laptop keyboard. "A VoIP service is the most logical interim solution."

She turned her laptop around for the others to see. "There are apps that will assign him a new, real phone number that operates over the internet," she explained, making sure Jordan understood. "As long as you're connected to WiFi—*any* WiFi, here in the dorm, at the library, the student union, basically anywhere on campus—you'll be able to make calls and send texts just like normal. It completely bypasses the cellular network."

A collective sense of victory filled the room. Working together, Zoe and Aubrie downloaded the app onto Jordan's phone and guided him through the setup. Within minutes, he had a brand new, fully functional phone number.

"Okay," Aubrie said, her expression turning serious as she looked around the room. "Now for the most important rule. This number does not go to anyone outside of us eight. Especially not your mother. She can email. You decide if and when you answer."

Jordan nodded, looking at his new, private number. He had a lifeline again, one controlled only by his real family.

"This is excellent for communication when he's on campus WiFi," Zoe clarified, bringing them back to the original problem. "But the safety concern for when he's between buildings remains. The Buddy System protocol is still necessary."

As Madison created the 'Jordan Comms' group chat, Lauren voiced the question that was already forming in everyone's minds. "This is a perfect plan for this break. But... what about Winter Break? That's almost a month long. The dorms will be closed. She's going to expect you to go home."

The air grew heavy for a moment, the thought of Jordan being forced back into that house for weeks hanging over them.

"No," Aubrie said, her voice quiet but absolute. "He's not going."

"But where will we go?" Lauren asked.

"Well," Hannah spoke up, "my parents' house is just twenty minutes from here, so I'll be home. Mark and Sarah are local, too. And Zoe's family lives just down 460 in Christiansburg." This was a fact the group knew but hadn't fully considered the implications of; four of their tight-knit circle had chosen to live in the dorms for the independence and experience, not out of necessity. "But you guys," she said, looking at Lauren, Madison, Aubrie, and Jordan, "none of you are from the New River Valley."

"Then the four of you will get a hotel room," Zoe declared, already shifting into planning mode. "The logical solution is an extended-stay hotel near campus. We can find a suite with a kitchenette and a small living/dining area. It can be our home base for the break."

Madison's face lit up. "Yes! One of those suites with two queen beds would be perfect. So, Jordan and one of us in one bed, and the other two in the second. We can take turns sharing with him."

The 'Jordan Comms' chat immediately buzzed with activity as Hannah relayed the plan. Mark: My folks will be happy to have me home, but fair warning, I'm basically gonna live at your hotel room. Don't even think you're getting out of wrestling night. Sarah: Same! My house is boring. I'll bring the snacks. Zoe: I'll map out a budget and a list of potential hotels.

Jordan looked at the faces of his friends in the room and the buzzing phone in his hand. They weren't just solving today's problem. They were building a fortress for his future, walling off his mother's control with concrete plans born of fierce, unwavering love. She had tried to cut one cord, and in response, his family had woven an unbreakable net.

"Okay," Madison said, clapping her hands together with renewed purpose. "The winter pact is made. Now for the Fall Break relay. Who's up first?"

### Part 2: The Handoffs in Action

The Fall Break weekend unfolded as a masterclass in compassionate logistics. The Buddy System, a plan born from crisis, was immediately put into practice, and the "Jordan Comms" group chat became the central nervous system of their family.

On Sunday morning, the first message appeared. Aubrie: With Jordan. Heading to 'The Daily Grind' in the student union for a change of scenery.

They sat for over an hour in the bustling campus coffee shop, sipping lattes and sharing a pastry. Here, surrounded by the university's powerful WiFi signal, Jordan's phone worked perfectly, allowing him to feel a sense of normal, easy connection with the rest of his friends via their chat.

Around noon, a new plan began to form in the group chat. Madison: I'm going stir-crazy. Anyone up for a trip into town? We could walk around downtown. Lauren: YES! I'm in. Aubrie: Great idea. We're at the student union. Meet us at the bus stop out front in 15.

The handoff was seamless. Aubrie went off to meet Hannah for a study session, while Madison and Lauren took over as Jordan's designated buddies for the afternoon adventure. The four of them—Jordan, Madison, Lauren, and Zoe, who decided to tag along—headed for the bus stop. As the large Blue Ridge Transit bus pulled up to the curb, its prominent "BT" logo gleaming in the sun, Madison got a mischievous look in her eye.

As she swiped her university ID, she leaned toward the driver, a friendly-looking man in his fifties. "Just so you know," she said with a playful grin, "we all know the *real* BT is the one back in my hometown, Bluewater."

The driver didn't miss a beat, looking at her in his rearview mirror with a tired, amused smirk. "That's funny, honey. My paycheck says Blue Ridge Transit, and we're the only ones running on game day. Sounds pretty real to me." He nodded toward the back of the bus. "Now find a seat before you hold up the whole route."

Madison laughed, completely satisfied with the familiar exchange. As the bus made its way from the campus grounds down into the heart of town, Jordan's phone went silent, the WiFi connection vanishing, but he felt no anxiety. He knew he wasn't truly disconnected.

When the bus reached their stop downtown, the group began to file out. As Madison passed the driver, she leaned in one last time with a smirk. "Sorry for calling you the fake BT," she whispered conspiratorially. The driver just shook his head, smiling. "Have a good one, Bluewater."

They spent the afternoon wandering the charming, historic streets of downtown Blacksburg. They browsed the windows of the quirky shops on Main Street and eventually ducked into a sprawling local bookstore with comfy armchairs and, most importantly, free WiFi. The moment they sat down, Jordan's phone chimed as it connected. A flood of messages from the "Jordan Comms" chat, which had been active while he was offline, poured in.

Hannah: Mark says to tell you Dr. Destruction's promo last night was epic and he can't wait to break it down with you. Zoe: (Attached PDF) Here is my initial research on hotels for Winter Break. Top three are highlighted. Aubrie: (photo of a squirrel looking menacingly at her from a tree branch) I think this guy wants my notes.

Jordan laughed, showing the picture to Lauren and Madison. His mother had cut his cellular service, but she couldn't cut him off from the constant, loving, chaotic stream of his family's life.

Later that afternoon, a new text came through on the group chat. Mark: Just got off my shift at the campus bookstore. I'm already downtown, I can give you all a ride back up the hill to the dorm if you want?

The final handoff of the day saw them all piling into Mark's slightly beat-up but reliable sedan. Back in the suite that evening, tired and happy, they all agreed the system had worked flawlessly. The Fall Break trial run was an absolute, unqualified success. They had proven they could navigate campus, travel into town, and keep Jordan safe, connected, and happy. His mother's attempt to isolate him had backfired spectacularly; it had only expanded his world.

### Part 3: The Christiansburg Thanksgiving Plan

The Monday of Fall Break, the group of eight was gathered in the dorm lobby for their weekly Wrestling Night tradition when the email from Jordan's mother landed like an ultimatum. The cheerful atmosphere evaporated as Zoe confirmed the university's policy: all residence halls would close for Thanksgiving break, from Wednesday at 1:00 PM to Sunday at 4:00 PM. The deadline was absolute, and Jordan had nowhere to go.

"Okay," Mark said, leaning forward, his voice serious. "So, he's not going home. Where can we all go?"

Zoe, ever the pragmatist, immediately took charge, her mind processing a simple, all-encompassing solution. "My parents live in Christiansburg," she stated calmly. "Their house is built on a hillside, and we have a large, finished basement. It has its own bathroom and a sliding glass door that opens directly to the backyard, so there are no stairs for Jordan to worry about."

She looked around at the entire group, her offer extending to everyone. "There's a pull-out sofa, and my parents have plenty of inflatable mattresses and sleeping bags. You can all come stay with us. All of you."

The offer was direct, simple, and incredibly generous. A wave of relief washed over the group.

Sarah, whose family lived in Radford, grinned. "My house is right there, but crashing in a basement with you guys sounds way more fun! I'm in!"

"Perfect!" Hannah said, beaming. "Mark and I can just hop on the bus to get to our parents' houses here in Blacksburg, and then we can get rides over. We're there."

With a single phone call, Zoe confirmed the plan. "My parents are in," she announced after hanging up. "My mom is actually excited. She says, and I quote, 'A full house for Thanksgiving! Wonderful!' She just needs a headcount for pies."

"Okay," Madison said, thinking through the practicalities. "So for helping Jordan with things like showering, it'll just be us girls, right?"

Mark laughed out loud, a warm, genuine sound. "Absolutely. Hannah, you guys do whatever you need to do to take care of your friend." He shook his head, a grin on his face. "Honestly, the fact that a guy like Tom would freak out about something so basic just proves what a loser he is. I trust you. I trust all of you." Hannah looked at him, her expression full of love and pride.

The plan was now solid. The entire suite family would decamp to Zoe's finished basement in Christiansburg. It would be a massive, chaotic, week-long slumber party. The pact of care would continue, with the women of the group taking turns ensuring Jordan was supported, and Mark being the perfect, trusting ally.

They quickly agreed to the final detail: they would all vacate the dorm on Tuesday night, a full day before his mother was scheduled to arrive. When she showed up on Wednesday morning to collect her son, she would be met with nothing but an empty, silent room. Her power had been completely and utterly neutralized by the unbreakable bond of his chosen family.

### Part 4: Cutting the Digital Cord

Back in the quiet of the suite after their successful planning session in the lobby, the wrestling match on the TV was long forgotten. Jordan was still sitting in front of his laptop, his mother's email still open on the screen. The words seemed to burn with a cold fire: *"Aubrey, you WILL be coming home..."*

He stared at the name she used, at the tone of absolute command. He had a plan to escape her physically, but he realized in that moment that he needed to build a digital wall as well.

"I don't want to get emails from her anymore," he said, his voice quiet but firm. "Not like this. Not ever again."

Zoe, who was sitting at her own desk nearby, swiveled her chair around. "Okay," she said, her mind immediately shifting to logistics. "We can fix that. You can't block her address from the university server, but you *can* change the address she knows about. We'll submit a help desk ticket right now."

She guided him to the CPI Information Technology support portal. Together, they found the correct form and began to fill it out.

IT Support Ticket

Request Type: Student Account Management

User: Jordan Fuller (current email: afuller001)

Subject: Request for Primary Email Address Change

Description:

"Hello, I am writing to request a change to my primary student email address. My current address, afuller001, was generated based on my legal first name, 'Aubrey,' which I do not use. My preferred name, 'Jordan,' is already on file with the registrar.

An external party is using the old 'afuller001' address to send harassing messages. To protect my privacy, I am requesting that the afuller001 alias be permanently deactivated and that a new primary email address be generated based on my preferred name, Jordan Fuller.

Thank you."

Jordan read over the text one last time, took a deep breath, and clicked "Submit." The ticket was sent. A day or two later, he would receive a notification from the Help Desk confirming his new official student email address was jfuller004, and that the old one was no longer active. It was a small, bureaucratic act, but it felt like slamming a steel door shut.

Aubrie Jean, who had been watching over his shoulder, placed a gentle hand on his. "Good," she said softly. "One less way for her to get to you."

He nodded, feeling a sense of control he hadn't known was possible. They had a physical plan for Thanksgiving, and now, they had a digital one.

### Part 5: The Aftermath

On Tuesday night, the last night of Fall Break, the air in the suite was thick with the comfortable, lazy satisfaction of a holiday well-spent. The eight of them were piled into Room 214, sprawled across the beds and floor, finishing off the last slices of cold pizza from what had been an impromptu movie marathon.

"Alright, we gotta jet," Mark said finally, pulling himself up and helping Hannah to her feet. "Real class in the morning."

"Thanks for everything, guys," Sarah added, giving both Lauren and Aubrie a quick, heartfelt hug. "This was... the best."

After they left for their own rooms down the hall, a peaceful quiet settled over the original six. The short break, which could have been a disaster, had instead become a foundational moment for their family.

Later, as he was getting ready for bed, Jordan sat in his wheelchair with his phone in his hands. He opened the "Jordan Comms" group chat, his thumb scrolling up, up, up, through the timeline of his newfound freedom. The log of his entire weekend was there, a testament to the beautiful, intricate system his friends had built for him overnight.

Zoe: With Jordan. Heading to the library. He needs to work on his astronomy paper. Madison: Got him. We're on the quad enjoying the sun! ☀️ Hannah: Everyone's back at the suite. Pizza is a go. Mark is bringing it. Lauren: (photo of Jordan holding a lopsided pumpkin) Sarah and I have Jordan. Pumpkin patch success! Mark: We've secured the lobby couches. GWF Uprising is a go. Tell Jordan that Dr. Destruction is going down tonight. Aubrie: Just me and J now. Grabbing a hot chocolate before heading back up.

He looked at the stream of messages, the casual photos, the easy logistics, and a profound realization washed over him. His mother had tried to punish him for his independence. She had tried to cut his cord, to make his world smaller, to prove that he couldn't survive without her control.

But she had failed spectacularly.

All she had managed to do was give his new family a reason to draw him in even closer. They had taken her act of malice and transformed it into a beautiful, intricate dance of care. They didn't just solve a problem; they created a system of love that was more powerful than any cell phone signal. His mother had tried to leave him stranded on an island, populated only by her rules and her resentment. In response, his family had built him a continent, full of life and laughter and unwavering support.

He looked up from his phone and saw Aubrie watching him from her bed, a soft, knowing smile on her face. He smiled back, a genuine, peaceful smile that needed no words. He was safer, more connected, and more loved than he had ever been in his entire life.

## Chapter 11: The Thanksgiving Exodus

### Part 1: Setting the Board

In the dorm lobby, the group huddled together, their successful Fall Break relay already feeling like a distant memory in the face of the new, urgent Thanksgiving deadline. After they had solidified the "Christiansburg Plan," it was Zoe who brought up the next logical step.

"Just leaving isn't a complete plan," she said, her tone serious. "Your mother is manipulative. When she finds you gone, her first move will be to use the university or the police as a tool. We need to control the narrative *before* she has a chance to create one."

She was right. It wasn't enough to run; they had to build a wall behind them.

That night, back in the quiet of the suite, Jordan, with Aubrie and Zoe looking over his shoulder, drafted a formal email.

To: Kelsey, Hall Director, Bennett Hall; Office of Housing and Residence Life\
From: Jordan Fuller\
Subject: Thanksgiving Break Plan - Jordan Fuller, Room 214

Dear Hall Director Kelsey and the Office of Housing,

This email is to formally notify you that I, Jordan Fuller (Student ID#...), will be spending the upcoming Thanksgiving holiday (Wednesday through Sunday) off-campus with friends. I am safe and will be well cared for.

I feel it is necessary to inform you that my mother, Aubrie Sinclair, may attempt to contact you or visit Bennett Hall under the incorrect assumption that I will be going home with her. She may refer to me by my legal first name, Aubrey. Please be advised that I do not wish to have contact with her at this time. She does not have my permission to enter my residence hall or be granted access to any of my student information.

Thank you for your understanding and for helping to protect my privacy.

He clicked send, the first brick in his wall of independence firmly set in place.

The next morning, he took the next step. With Mark and Hannah as his supportive escort, he went to the Campus Police station. He spoke calmly and clearly to the officer at the desk.

"My name is Jordan Fuller," he began. "I am a resident of Bennett Hall, and I need to make a voluntary statement. I am going to be staying with my friends' family in Christiansburg for the Thanksgiving break. However, I have reason to believe my mother, Aubrie Sinclair, may file a false missing persons report for me, likely using the name 'Aubrey Fuller.' I want it officially on record that I am not missing, I am safe, and I have voluntarily chosen not to have contact with her. My new emergency contacts are Daniel and Laura Brooks at this address..."

He provided all the necessary information. The officer listened patiently, nodding. This was not the first time they had dealt with a difficult family situation involving a student. "Thank you for being proactive, Jordan," the officer said kindly. "We'll put a note on file right now and we'll share it with the Blacksburg and Christiansburg police, as well as the county sheriff. You have a safe holiday."

Leaving the station, Jordan felt a weight lift from his shoulders. He hadn't just run. He had prepared. He had used his voice and his rights as an adult. Now, whatever his mother tried, she would find only closed doors.

### Part 2: The Final Hour

With all official notices filed, a tangible sense of relief and victory settled over Jordan. He had taken control. Back in the suite, the mood shifted from formal resolve to excited urgency as the entire eight-person family converged to pack.

"Okay, people," Zoe announced, looking at the clock on her phone. "The city bus from the CPI Transit Center to Falling Branch Park & Ride runs every fifteen minutes. Our target is the 4:45 PM bus, which gives us just under an hour. Operation Thanksgiving Pack-Up is a go."

What followed was a flurry of purposeful chaos. The group worked as a single, cohesive unit. Lauren and Sarah took charge of gathering toiletries and snacks. Hannah was at Jordan's closet, holding up sweaters for his approval. "Blue or grey?" she'd ask, and he'd give a quick nod.

Mark, without being asked, easily lifted a heavy box of textbooks from a high shelf that no one else could reach, placing it carefully by Jordan's bag. As he worked, he made a point to be relaxed and helpful. He wanted Jordan to see, unequivocally, that he was nothing like Tom. He saw the easy, platonic affection between Hannah and Jordan and felt nothing but happiness. Mark knew the 'official' story about Aubrie being Jordan's girlfriend, but he also understood the deeper, unofficial truth: Jordan was family to all of them, and Mark had no room for the kind of pathetic insecurity that had consumed Tom.

Aubrie and Madison worked together, going through a mental checklist of Jordan's essentials. "Charging cable for the chair?" Aubrie asked. "Got it," Madison confirmed, holding it up. "Noise-canceling headphones?" "In the side pocket of his backpack, right where he said," Madison replied.

Jordan sat in the center of it all, the calm director of his own needs, a quiet smile on his face. He was not being passively cared for; he was being supported by a team, his team.

"Alright," Zoe announced, "bus leaves in twenty. Final sweep."

They did one last check. The rooms, while still full of their long-term possessions, were stripped of the core items needed for the next five days. They grabbed their bags, ready for the short walk to the transit center and the bus ride that would take them to the safety of Zoe's house on Geneva Street, right next to the park and ride lot. Jordan led the way out the door, his chosen family—all seven of them—trailing right behind him.

### Part 3: The Journey

The walk from Bennett Hall to the CPI Transit Center was a sight to behold. The eight friends moved as one organism, a bustling caravan of rolling suitcases, overstuffed duffel bags, and cheerful chatter, with Jordan's wheelchair at the center of their formation.

They arrived at the busy transit center and checked the schedule. The next local Blue Ridge Transit bus to Christiansburg was due in about ten minutes. But just as they were settling in to wait, a different vehicle pulled into an adjacent bay. It was not a typical city bus, but a large, long-distance coach bus, its digital sign cycling between MAROON EXPRESS and BLUEWATER via FALLING BRANCH. With a pneumatic hiss, the doors swung open, and a clear, automated female voice immediately began to announce its route on a loop.

"BLUEWATER TRANSIT MAROON EXPRESS: BLUEWATER via FALLING BRANCH," the voice called out.

Madison's eyes lit up. "GUYS! LOOK!" she whisper-yelled, pointing with excitement. "It's the Maroon Express! It's the *real* BT!"

Seeing the others' confusion, she quickly explained. "That's my hometown bus! It operates express via the highway. It'll get us to Falling Branch in like twenty minutes instead of the forty-five the local route takes. The trade-off is it only comes once an hour, so we just got incredibly lucky! We have to take it!"

Boarding the large coach bus was a more involved process. The driver, with a friendly and practiced air, operated the wheelchair lift located at the center door. The process was a noisy, mechanical affair, a slow, whirring platform that always drew more attention than Jordan liked, but it was a necessary pain. Once on board, he transferred to one of the comfortable coach seats, and his wheelchair was collapsed and stowed below.

Aubrie immediately slid into the seat next to him, and he gratefully leaned against her, the two of them cuddling up for the short, comfortable ride. The rest of their friends found seats nearby, taking over a whole section of the coach and filling it with their happy, excited energy.

Once they were underway and cruising down the highway, Zoe, ever the logistician, immediately got to work. She sent a quick text to her parents, letting them know their arrival time had been cut by more than half. Then, she turned to the group.

"Okay, so, for the house," she began, "My parents have cleared out the whole basement living area for us. We'll have four queen air mattresses, so we'll be pairing up. And just a heads-up, my six brothers and sisters will be around, so be prepared for some chaos."

Madison grinned. "Awesome! Okay, so Hannah and Mark are obviously one pair..."

"I hope your mom makes her sweet potato casserole!" Lauren chimed in.

"We should definitely try to see my parents in Radford one day, too," Sarah added. "And Mark, we can all come visit your family in Blacksburg. We can make a whole tour of it!"

They were so deep in their enthusiastic discussion—planning meals, assigning bed partners, and mapping out a holiday week that would connect all of their local families—that the automated voice announcing their stop took them by surprise.

"Next Stop: Falling Branch Park and Ride."

They looked at each other, grinning. Their journey had been so swift, they had barely made a dent in their planning. It was time to get off the bus. Their Thanksgiving was about to begin.

Disembarking was as smooth as boarding. They stood for a moment at the quiet, suburban bus stop, the air cooler and cleaner than it was on campus. The entrance to Zoe's neighborhood was just one block away, at the corner of U.S. 11 and Simmons Road SE. From there, it was just a few more blocks to the house on Geneva Street. As they began the final leg of their journey, walking down the quiet, tree-lined street, a single house at the end of the cul-de-sac came into view, its porch light a warm, welcoming beacon. They were almost home.

### Part 4: The Sanctuary

The group turned off Simmons Road SE onto the quiet, tree-lined cul-de-sac of Geneva Street. As they approached a handsome two-story house, the front door swung open and a woman with Zoe's kind eyes and a brilliant smile came out onto the porch, waving enthusiastically.

"You made it!" Laura Brooks called out, her voice full of genuine excitement. "We were just tracking your bus online! Welcome, welcome!" She gestured around the side of the house. "Come on around back! Your chariot awaits! Daniel's waiting to let you into your headquarters for the week!"

They followed a stone path that led around the house and began to slope downwards. The property was clearly built on a hill; the front door they had just seen was a full story above the backyard that stretched out below. Waiting at the bottom, standing by a large sliding glass door, was Zoe's father.

As the group started down the grassy slope, Daniel Brooks immediately came forward to meet Jordan. "Alright, Jordan, let me give you a hand here," he said, his voice calm and capable. "This slope's a bit of a beast."

Without any fuss, Daniel took firm control of the back of Jordan's chair, expertly guiding it down the incline, acting as a human brake and ensuring a smooth, safe descent. It was their first interaction, and it was one of immediate, practical trust.

Daniel slid open the glass door, and the group flooded into the finished basement that would be their home away from home. It was perfect. The space was warm and cozy, with a large sectional sofa, a television, and a small kitchenette along one wall. On a coffee table, a tray of snacks was already laid out.

Laura came down the internal stairs, carrying a tray of mugs. "Settle in, everyone. Make yourselves at home," she said, pouring steaming apple cider for them. "You're safe here."

"Alright," Daniel said, clapping his hands together with purpose. "Let's get this place operational." He pulled a large box from a storage closet and took out a single electric pump. "One pump, four queen-sized air mattresses. This might take a minute."

He plugged the pump into a wall outlet, and with a loud whir, the first mattress began to take shape on the floor. The noise drew an audience, and a moment later, Zoe's six older siblings began to filter down the stairs to see the new arrivals and offer their help.

Zoe laughed. "Everyone, this is my chaotic family. My older sisters, Megan and Chloe, and my four older brothers, Caleb, Lucas, Owen, and Jacob."

The Brooks siblings, a friendly and energetic bunch, didn't hesitate for a moment. They jumped right in to help the eight guests get situated. Caleb, the oldest, took over mattress duty from his dad. Lucas and Owen started helping Mark and the girls find spots for their duffel bags, while Jacob immediately engaged Jordan in a conversation about the video game he was playing.

Megan and Chloe took Lauren and Sarah under their wings. "Okay, we've got extra towels and blankets for you all in this closet here," Megan said, opening a door. "And Mom wants to know if anyone has any laundry they need to do."

As the four mattresses filled the floor space, creating a massive, comfortable landscape, Madison surveyed the scene. "Okay, bed assignments are simple," she announced. "Hannah and Mark, you get one." "Claimed!" Hannah laughed, grabbing Mark's hand.

"Jordan gets one," Madison continued. "And he'll need a buddy." "I'll take tonight," Lauren volunteered softly, "if that's okay, Jordan?" He gave her a warm, grateful smile and nodded. "Perfect," Madison said. "That leaves me, Aubrie, Zoe, and Sarah for the other two. Dibs on not being next to the snorer!"

The entire Brooks family helped the eight friends get settled, their unconditional acceptance making the space feel less like a temporary shelter and more like a true home.

### Part 5: Settling into Sanctuary

As the evening wound down, the chaotic energy of arrival softened into a comfortable, sleepy contentment. One by one, the Brooks siblings and their parents said their goodnights, heading up the internal stairs to the first and second floors of the house, leaving the eight friends to their own private domain.

The basement was now their fortress, filled with a sprawling city of four queen-sized air mattresses. The low light from a single lamp cast a warm, cozy glow over the scene.

"You know what sounds amazing right now?" Jordan said, breaking the quiet. "A real bath. Not just a quick shower."

The group looked at the basement bathroom. It was a standard, clean bathroom, but the tub was a simple tub/shower combo, with no grab bars or transfer bench. It wasn't handicap accessible.

"We've got you," Aubrie said immediately, without a hint of hesitation. Lauren stood up beside her. "Yep. The two of us can handle it."

What followed was not an act of romance, but one of pure, practical care, a dance of trust and physical support. Getting Jordan safely into and out of the tub was a careful, coordinated effort. Lauren provided the steady strength, her hands sure and firm, while Aubrie handled the logistics—getting the water to the perfect temperature, making sure towels and soap were within easy reach, and ensuring his dignity was protected throughout the entire process. It was another problem to be solved, and like every other problem they had faced, they solved it together.

Later, with everyone in their pajamas and ready for bed, a peaceful quiet settled over the basement. Hannah and Mark were already whispering to each other on their claimed mattress. The other girls were curled up on theirs. Lauren sat on the edge of the mattress she would be sharing with Jordan tonight, watching as Aubrie gave him a soft goodnight kiss before heading to her own bed.

There was no jealousy. No tension. Only love.

Lauren thought of Tom, of the possessive rage he would have felt at a scene like this. He would have seen rivals, betrayal, and suspicion. But here, there was only trust. Aubrie's complete, radical generosity was the foundation upon which this entire family was built. *It's Tom's loss,* Lauren thought, a surprising firmness in her heart. *And it's Jordan's gain. It's all of our gain.*

Jordan transferred from his chair onto the mattress, settling in beside her. "Goodnight, Lauren," he whispered. "Goodnight, Jordan," she replied, and as he wrapped an arm around her in their now-familiar, comforting spooning embrace, she felt the last of the day's stress melt away.

Across the sprawling, darkened room, a chorus of soft "goodnights" echoed. The sixteen inhabitants of the house on Geneva Street settled in for the night, but for the eight friends in the basement, it was something more. They had completed their exodus. They were safe, they were together, and they were finally home for the holiday.

## Chapter 11 Sidebar: The Unwelcome Arrival

### Part 1: The Fortress

Kelsey, the Hall Director of the notoriously old Eleanor Bennett Commonwealth Hall—affectionately known to generations of students as "The Old Lady on the Hill"—had been on alert since her meeting with Jordan. Understanding the potential for a confrontation, she had taken preemptive measures. On Monday, a building-wide email was sent to all residents reminding them that holding a secure door for any non-resident was a serious security violation.

Furthermore, on Wednesday morning, two uniformed campus police officers were stationed discreetly in the main lobby, ostensibly for a "pre-break safety presence," but in reality, they were there at Kelsey's request. The entire on-duty staff knew the protocol: be polite, be firm, and do not acknowledge the name "Aubrey Fuller."

It was into this carefully prepared environment that Aubrie Sinclair swept just before 10:00 AM, radiating a confidence that was about to be thoroughly dismantled. She marched directly to the front desk on the lower level.

"Good morning," she said. "I'm here to pick up my son, Aubrey Fuller, from Room 214."

The student worker gave a polite, practiced smile. "I'm sorry, ma'am, I don't have a resident listed by that name in our system."

Furious at being stonewalled, Aubrie spotted her opportunity. A student was approaching the secure doors to the residential hallway. As the student swiped his card and the lock clicked, she rushed forward. "Oh, excuse me, dear, could you hold that?"

The student looked at her, then at the large, prominent sign on the door that read "DO NOT HOLD DOOR FOR OTHERS." He remembered the hall-wide email from two days ago. He gave her an apologetic look.

"I'm sorry, ma'am. We're not allowed to," he said, and let the heavy door swing shut, the lock clicking firmly into place.

Aubrie stood there, stunned and humiliated. Her manipulation had failed.

"Ma'am." The voice was calm and professional. She turned to see Kelsey standing there, with the two campus police officers now positioned a few feet behind her, their presence casual but unmissable.

"This is outrageous!" Aubrie sputtered. "I am being denied access to my son!"

"As my staff informed you," Kelsey said, her tone leaving no room for argument, "we are legally prohibited by FERPA from discussing any student's information. And as you've just seen, our residents take security in The Old Lady very seriously. We look out for each other here."

Kelsey gestured subtly towards the officers. "These officers are here to ensure a safe and orderly closing of the hall for the Thanksgiving break. I am sure you don't want a disturbance that would require their direct intervention."

The message was crystal clear. Every path was blocked. Faced with the choice of leaving on her own free will or being escorted out by campus police, Aubrie Sinclair made the only decision she could. With a look of pure venom at the staff who had so thoroughly defeated her, she spun on her heel and stormed out of the building.

### Part 2: All Lines Cut

Sitting in her car, shaking with rage, Aubrie turned to her final, most powerful option: the authorities. She dialed the Blacksburg Police non-emergency number.

"Yes, I need to report a missing person," she said, her voice laced with practiced, maternal panic. "My son. Aubrey Fuller. He's a student at CPI, he's disabled, and I'm very worried."

The dispatcher's voice was calm. "One moment, ma'am." After a pause filled with the soft clicking of a keyboard, she returned. "Ma'am, we actually have a proactive note on file regarding this situation. The student has been in contact with Campus Police to assure them of his safety. His message was passed on to us, as well as to the Christiansburg Police and the Montgomery County Sheriff's office."

The dispatcher continued, "The student has made it clear that he is safe, that he is voluntarily spending the holiday with friends, and that he does not wish to have contact with you at this time. He is not considered a missing person. We cannot take a report. Is there anything else I can help you with?"

Checkmate. Every single avenue was blocked. Aubrie Sinclair lowered the phone, and finally, with no other moves left to make, she put the car in drive and pulled away from the curb, defeated.

## Chapter 12: A Thanksgiving Sanctuary

### Part 1: The Calm Before

The Wednesday before Thanksgiving at the Brooks' house was a perfect symphony of happy, domestic chaos. The morning started with a massive breakfast of pancakes and bacon for all sixteen people, the huge kitchen table supplemented by a folding table borrowed from the basement. The air was filled with the easy laughter of the eight friends blending seamlessly with the boisterous energy of Zoe's six siblings.

In the afternoon, the entire group migrated to the sloping backyard for a wildly disorganized game of two-hand-touch football. The teams were uneven and constantly shifting. Mark, a devoted football fan, tried in vain to call actual plays, but was mostly ignored. Zoe's athletic older brothers, Caleb and Owen, were the clear power players, while Madison surprised everyone with her speed.

Jordan was the designated "All-Time Quarterback." Positioned in his chair near the center of the action, he threw spirals for both teams, a grin plastered on his face. Aubrie Jean acted as his loyal center, handing him the ball before each play and fiercely protecting him from any over-enthusiastic linebackers. He was not on the sidelines; he was the central pivot point of the entire game.

Later, breathless and laughing, they all trooped back inside through the basement's sliding glass door. Laura Brooks was waiting with mugs of hot cider, and the delicious smells of the next day's feast were already wafting down from the kitchen upstairs. As everyone found a place to sit in the now-cozy basement, relaxing after the game, Daniel Brooks waited for a lull in the chatter. His expression shifted from playful to serious, and he cleared his throat to get everyone's attention.

"Okay, everyone, listen up for a second," he said, his calm voice cutting through the room. "Your mom and I noticed something yesterday and again this morning. There was an unfamiliar car parked down on the corner of Geneva, just sitting there for hours. It didn't belong to any of the neighbors."

The cheerful atmosphere immediately became tense. The friends all looked at each other, then instinctively toward Jordan.

"I took down the plate number and called it in to the Christiansburg PD this afternoon," Daniel continued. "They did a drive-by, but the car was gone by then. Officially, there's nothing they can do about a car parked on a public street."

He made direct eye contact with Jordan, his gaze steady and supportive. "Now, we can't prove anything. But we have to assume that your mother hired someone, and that they now know this address. We have to assume this sanctuary isn't a secret anymore."

A heavy silence filled the basement. Jordan felt a familiar knot of anxiety tighten in his stomach, but looking around at the sixteen concerned faces focused on him, it was different. He wasn't scared; he was angry. And he wasn't alone.

Aubrie Jean moved to his side, her hand resting protectively on his shoulder.

"So we escalate security protocols," Zoe said, her voice a firm, logical statement of fact.

"Exactly," Daniel affirmed, nodding at his daughter. "You are all perfectly safe here. This is our home. But we all need to be vigilant. From now on, all the doors stay locked, even when we're home during the day. We don't open the door to anyone we don't know, no exceptions. If anyone sees that car again, you don't engage. You come get me or Laura immediately."

He looked around at the entire group, his expression unwavering. "We are all in this together."

A quiet murmur of agreement went through the room. The enemy now knew their location. But instead of feeling fear, the family in the basement felt their resolve harden. The walls of their fortress had just gotten sixteen people stronger.

### Part 2: A Basement Feast

On Thanksgiving morning, Jordan woke to the soft, rhythmic breathing of his seven best friends sleeping around him. In the dim light of the basement, he saw the comfortable jumble of blankets and air mattresses that filled the large room. The scare from the previous day—the knowledge that his mother’s reach had found them—had been terrifying. But waking up here, literally surrounded by a fortress of his chosen family, he felt an overwhelming sense of gratitude and security. They were all here together. No one could get to him.

A little later, after people had started to stir, Daniel Brooks came down the internal stairs, a cheerful smile on his face. "Happy Thanksgiving, everyone!" he boomed. He looked around at the sleepy group. "Just so everyone knows the plan for the day: we're having Thanksgiving right down here."

He saw the surprised looks on their faces and held up a hand. "Your mom and I talked about it last night," he explained, his gaze settling on Jordan with kind firmness. "After the business with that car yesterday, your safety is our number one priority, Jordan. We're not taking any chances. So, my crew and I will handle all the cooking upstairs, and we'll bring the entire feast down to you when it's ready. You all just relax, watch the parade, and enjoy the day. No one needs to worry about a thing."

The group exchanged grateful glances. The Brooks family wasn't just offering them shelter; they were altering their own family traditions to create a shield.

The day unfolded in a cocoon of warmth and safety. In the morning, they all gathered around the big TV, joined by several of Zoe's siblings, to watch The Hudson's Thanksgiving Day Parade, loudly critiquing the floats and singing along to the musical numbers. The air was filled with the smell of roasting turkey wafting down from the kitchen above.

Around 3:00 PM, the "ferry service" began. Zoe's brothers and sisters started a steady procession down the stairs, their arms laden with platters. First came the magnificent roasted turkey, carved by Daniel himself. It was followed by bowls of mashed potatoes, stuffing, green bean casserole, cranberry sauce, and fresh-baked rolls. The coffee table and every available flat surface were soon groaning under the weight of the feast.

Their Thanksgiving dinner was loud, chaotic, and perfect. They sat on the floor, on the sofa, on the edges of their air mattresses, plates balanced on their laps. The sounds of sixteen people laughing, talking over each other, and arguing playfully about who got the last dinner roll filled the basement. Jordan sat in the middle of it all, a plate full of food he'd never had before, a genuine, unburdened smile on his face. This was what a holiday was supposed to feel like.

Later, as he was helping clear away some plates, Daniel Brooks put a hand on his shoulder. "Thank you, Mr. Brooks," Jordan said, his voice thick with emotion. "For... all of this."

Daniel smiled warmly. "You're family, Jordan," he said simply. "This is what we do."

### Part 3: The Turkey Brawl

As 8:00 PM approached, the remnants of the massive feast were cleared away, and a new kind of anticipation filled the basement. The plates and platters were replaced with bowls of popcorn and cups of soda. The eight friends claimed their spots on the air mattresses and sofa, their eyes glued to the television. It was time for the main event of the holiday: the *Global Wrestling Federation's* annual two-hour special, the "GWF Turkey Brawl."

Just as the bombastic opening theme music began, Laura and Daniel Brooks appeared at the bottom of the stairs, followed by all six of Zoe's siblings.

"You don't think you're having all the fun down here by yourselves, do you?" Daniel said with a grin. "There's no way we're missing this."

The whole Brooks clan found places to sit, squeezing onto the sofa and grabbing pillows for the floor. The basement was now packed with all sixteen of them, a massive, boisterous audience ready for the show.

The main event was a championship match featuring the group's favorite villain, the massive and arrogant Dr. Destruction. He was on the verge of cheating his way to another victory, with the referee distracted and the current champion nearly defeated.

"Come on, ref, turn around!" Mark yelled at the screen, throwing a piece of popcorn in frustration.

Just as Dr. Destruction raised the championship belt to use as a weapon, the arena lights went out. A single, familiar chord of electric guitar blasted through the speakers—the entrance music of the very babyface hero he had put on the shelf months ago.

The arena on the screen erupted. And so did the basement on Geneva Street.

Everyone jumped to their feet. Madison was screaming, Mark was high-fiving Caleb, and even Zoe had a rare, wide grin of pure shock and excitement on her face. The returning hero stormed the ring and tackled Dr. Destruction before the villain could even react. What followed was not a match, but pure, cathartic revenge. The hero hit all his signature moves, leaving Dr. Destruction laid out flat in the center of the ring.

As the hero stood tall over his vanquished foe, he looked over at the dazed champion, who was slowly pulling himself up in the corner. He gave the champion a sharp, definitive nod, as if to say, "He's all yours. Finish it."

The hero then slid out of the ring, his revenge complete. The champion, seeing his opportunity, climbed to the top rope, delivered his own spectacular finishing move onto the unconscious Dr. Destruction, and rolled him over for the cover.

"ONE! TWO! THREE!" the entire basement roared in unison with the referee's count.

The room exploded in cheers. The champion had retained his title, and the hero had gotten his retribution. As the celebration died down and the show went to its closing credits, Jordan looked around the crowded room at his entire chosen family, all laughing and high-fiving. He realized this silly, over-the-top spectacle was a perfect metaphor for his own life. He had faced a villain who seemed unbeatable, but his own heroes had returned to help him win the fight. He caught Aubrie's eye from across the room, and she gave him a quiet, knowing smile that understood everything.

"Alright," Daniel Brooks announced, his voice full of good cheer as he stretched. "That's enough excitement for one night. We're all heading up."

One by one, the Brooks family said their goodnights, leaving the eight friends alone in their basement sanctuary. A comfortable, sleepy quiet began to settle over them as they got ready for bed.

"Okay, everyone, listen up," Madison whispered loudly, full of energy even as she climbed onto her air mattress. "Get some sleep. We have a very important mission tomorrow. Operation: Jordan's First Black Friday. BIG DAY TOMORROW!"

A chorus of sleepy "goodnights" filled the room as the lights went down. Tucked away safely in their fortress, the eight of them drifted off to sleep, ready for their next adventure together.

## Chapter 13: Operation Black Friday

### Part 1: The Mission Briefing

The Brooks' basement was a hive of quiet activity long before the sun rose on Black Friday. The air smelled of freshly brewed coffee, and Laura Brooks was already bringing down trays laden with a filling breakfast of scrambled eggs and bacon.

Upstairs, Laura and Daniel Brooks were already in motion. Soon, they came down the stairs bearing trays laden with scrambled eggs, bacon, toast, and fruit. It was a massive, filling breakfast.

"Eat up, everyone," Daniel said, his tone warm but serious. "There will be no stopping for food. We go, we get what we need, and we come straight back." Alongside the breakfast plates, Sarah and one of Zoe's sisters were already packing a bag with snacks and water bottles. It might have seemed like overkill, but Daniel was taking absolutely no chances. He watched Jordan laugh with his sons, a fierce, protective instinct flaring in his chest. In a few short months, the boy had burrowed his way into Daniel's heart. This wasn't just his daughter's friend; he was starting to feel like one of his own. The thought that anyone would want to harm him was unacceptable, and Daniel would do everything in his power to prevent it.

After breakfast, it was time for final preparations. Hannah and Lauren, being the strongest of the women, helped Jordan into the bathroom one last time to get washed up and ready for the long day. Mark watched them go with a proud, easy smile, his complete lack of jealousy a quiet testament to his trust. Across the room, Aubrie felt a similar sense of peaceful certainty, watching the boy she loved be cared for by women she trusted as sisters. This was their normal.

With everyone completely ready, Daniel gathered the eight friends and his two oldest sons in the main area of the basement.

"Okay, everyone, listen up," he said, his voice a low, authoritative rumble. "The convoy will be three vehicles. Caleb, you'll take your SUV with Owen, Sarah, and Jacob. You take point. Mark, you've got Hannah and the rest in your sedan; you'll have our six. I'll drive the minivan. Laura will ride shotgun."

He then turned to Jordan. "Jordan, you'll be in the second row of the van for security. Who do you want with you?"

Jordan didn't hesitate. He couldn't explain why, couldn't articulate the specific physics of the comfort she provided, but faced with the overwhelming prospect of the Black Friday chaos, the only thing that felt truly safe was the thought of her arms around him. "Aubrie," he said quietly.

"Okay," Daniel nodded, his expression softening as he looked at Aubrie. "You're with him. Lauren, Madison, Zoe—you three are in the back row. We're keeping the core team together."

He looked around at the assembled group. "We all know the primary objective: the Commonwealth Wireless corporate store at Spradlin Farms to get Jordan his new mPhone and plan. That is our only mandatory stop. Everything else is secondary. Caleb and Owen, you're our eyes in the parking lot. You see anything that looks out of place, you text the 'Jordan Comms' group, and the mission is scrubbed. Everyone clear?"

A chorus of determined "yeses" answered him. They piled out of the basement through the sliding glass door, a small, brave army heading out into the cold morning, ready for their mission.

### Part 2: The Drive

The three-car convoy pulled out onto Geneva Street in a smooth, coordinated line. Inside Daniel Brooks's minivan, the atmosphere was thick with tension. Laura, in the front passenger seat, kept her eyes fixed on the side mirror. In the back, Madison, Zoe, and Lauren were uncharacteristically quiet, their gazes flicking to the windows.

Daniel reached the end of the road, where he could either turn right to get on the U.S. 460 highway for a direct, five-minute shot to the shopping center, or he could go straight and take the longer, winding route of Business U.S. 460 through town. He didn't hesitate. He went straight. He would sacrifice speed for unpredictability, hoping to shake off anyone who might be following.

He chose the longer, thirteen-minute local route, staying on U.S. 11 to wind his way through the heart of Christiansburg on Business U.S. 460. It was slower, with more stoplights and town traffic, but it was also far less predictable. It offered dozens of turns and opportunities to spot a tail, a trade-off he was more than willing to make for a greater sense of security.

In the second-row captain's chair, Jordan's heart was pounding against his ribs. Every passing sedan, every dark-colored SUV, was a potential threat in his mind. He was on high alert, scanning the traffic behind them for the gray car, for a spy who was, at that moment, likely reading the morning paper over a cup of coffee miles away, the Brooks family already a forgotten case file.

But Jordan didn't know that. All he knew was a deep, primal fear. He felt a desperate, overwhelming need for comfort, a craving he couldn't explain in words but felt in every nerve ending. In his mind, the name "Aubrie" had split into two opposing forces: one was the source of this terror, the woman he had fled. The other was the antidote.

There was only one Aubrie he wanted right now. And her name was Aubrie Jean Kelly.

His hand trembled as he reached across the small space between their chairs. She met him instantly, her hand taking his, her grip warm and firm. That was all the invitation he needed. He leaned into her, resting his head against her shoulder. She responded by wrapping her arm around him, pulling him into a tight, secure hug right there in the seat. He closed his eyes, focusing on the steady rhythm of her breathing, and let the feeling of safety she provided wash over him.

A few minutes later, the convoy signaled and turned into the chaotic parking lot of the Spradlin Farms shopping center. Daniel found a spot and put the van in park, but no one moved. He picked up his phone.

"Caleb, Mark, report. Anything?" he said into the "Jordan Comms" chat.

Caleb's voice came back instantly through the car's speakers. "Negative, Dad. We're clear. Had a clear view the whole way. No sign of anything."

Mark's voice followed. "All clear back here, too. We're good."

A collective sigh of relief went through the minivan. Daniel's evasive route had worked. Unbeknownst to him, it had worked because there was no one to evade. He turned around in his seat, a look of grim satisfaction on his face.

"Okay, team," he said. "We're clear. Let's go get a phone."

### Part 3: The First Purchase

The three-car convoy parked strategically in the bustling Spradlin Farms lot. As planned, Caleb and Owen's SUV and Mark's sedan took up positions that provided a clear view of the area around Daniel's minivan. The "outer perimeter" got out first, scanning the area before giving the "all clear" on the group chat.

Daniel slid open the minivan door. With practiced ease, the group helped Jordan transfer into his wheelchair. The moment he was settled, Sarah stepped forward, wrapping him in a snug, protective hug that seemed to block out the noise of the crowded parking lot. It was a gesture he leaned into gratefully. He would only accept this kind of physical comfort from the females of his trusted Group of 8, and today it was Sarah's turn to be his primary anchor.

"I've got him," she said to the group, taking her place behind his wheelchair.

They entered the Commonwealth Wireless store as a single, multi-layered unit. It was busy, but the group used their numbers to create a pocket of calm around Jordan. While Mark and the Brooks brothers discreetly monitored the entrance, the rest of them focused on the mission.

Daniel approached a representative. "Hello," he said calmly. "We'd like to add a line to our family plan today."

What followed was a beautiful display of teamwork. As Daniel handled the account paperwork, Hannah and Madison showed Jordan the different mPhone models. He chose a sleek, dark blue, the simple act of making his own choice feeling monumental.

"Okay, you definitely need a case for that," Zoe said, already pulling up reviews on her phone and pointing him toward the most durable, practical options.

"Don't forget headphones!" chimed in Megan, one of Zoe's sisters.

Her brother, Owen, pointed to a display. "If you're getting an mPhone, you've gotta get the mPods Pro. The noise-canceling is insane."

Chloe, Zoe's other sister, appeared a moment later with a small box. "And you'll need this," she said, showing Jordan a charging brick that accepted two USB-C cables. "One for the phone and one for the mPods."

The transaction was complete in under fifteen minutes. The associate handed the newly activated mPhone to Jordan. Daniel smiled as he signed the final paper. "Welcome to the Brooks family plan, son."

Back outside in the crisp November air, Jordan looked down at the new phone in his hands—a device completely unknown to his mother, with a number only his true family had. He felt a surge of exhilaration.

"That was fun," he said, looking around at the bustling shopping center. "Can we... can we try another store?"

A nervous flicker crossed Daniel's face as he scanned the chaotic parking lot. He had accomplished the primary mission. But seeing the genuine excitement on Jordan's face, he couldn't say no. "Alright," he agreed, his voice firm. "But we stay on high alert. The second anything feels off, we are gone. No questions asked." He was searching for a threat that, unbeknownst to him, wasn't there.

They all piled back into the vehicles, but this time, there was a shift. "My turn," Madison said cheerfully, sliding into the captain's chair next to Jordan in the minivan. The hug rotation continued.

As they pulled out of their parking spot, Daniel made a new executive decision. "Alright team," he announced. "New objective. Blue Tech." He had noticed Jordan's old, slow laptop during the holiday prep. "That laptop of yours has seen better days. We're getting you an upgrade for your school work."

Madison squeezed Jordan's shoulder as their van led the convoy toward the massive electronics store. "Ready for the big leagues, Jordan?" she asked with a grin. "This is going to be a lot more crowded than a little cell phone store!"

### Part 4: The Big Box Gauntlet

The convoy arrived at the even more chaotic Blue Tech parking lot. The "outer perimeter"—Caleb, Owen, and Mark—did a thorough sweep before giving the all-clear. As Jordan transferred into his wheelchair, Hannah stepped forward.

"My turn," she said, giving him a firm, grounding hug before taking the handles of his chair. Mark looked on, a look of immense pride on his face. He was proud of his girlfriend for being so compassionate and proud of his friend for trusting her. Across the way, Aubrie watched the same exchange and felt an identical sense of peaceful certainty.

The moment they entered the store, a wall of noise and light hit them. Blue Tech was a sensory nightmare. Jordan tensed immediately, and even Aubrie grew pale. Zoe's sister, Megan, acted fast, helping Jordan get his new mPods Pro into his ears to muffle the cacophony. Seeing Aubrie was also struggling, Lauren smoothly took over pushing the wheelchair from Hannah, allowing Hannah to drop back and offer her own quiet support to Aubrie.

Daniel guided the group through the thickest crowds. "Laptops are in the back-left corner. Let's make a beeline," he commanded. He was scanning constantly, his head on a swivel. He briefly tensed when he saw a man in a different colored shirt seemingly watching them, only to relax when the man turned, revealing the "Blue Tech Repair Squad" logo on his back. Daniel let out a quiet breath, chiding himself for being jumpy over a threat that, unbeknownst to him, wasn't even there.

They reached the laptop section, and Mark took the lead. "Alright, Jordan, let's find you a beast," he said, his eyes scanning the displays. He pointed to a sleek, powerful machine. "This one. The new Pinnacle Precisionist 5000. Great processor, dedicated graphics card... this will handle anything the university throws at you." He looked at the spec sheet. "Ah, crap."

"What is it?" Jordan asked.

"It comes with Quantum OS 8.1 Home," Mark explained. "For what you're doing, you really need the Pro version. It has much better security features and remote desktop capabilities. The school's IT department can help you way easier if you have Pro."

Just as Jordan's face fell slightly, Mark's eyes lit up. "Wait here." He darted over to a nearby aisle filled with software and gift cards. He returned a moment later, holding a small cardboard card. "And here we go. The Quantum OS Home to Pro upgrade pack."

They took both items to the checkout, where Daniel was ready to pay. As the cashier rang up the laptop, Mark stepped forward and placed the small upgrade card on the counter.

"I've got this part, Mr. Brooks," he said firmly. He then turned to Jordan with a warm smile. "Consider it a welcome-to-the-family gift. I want you to have the best, most secure setup possible."

It was more than just a hundred-dollar piece of software. It was a clear, tangible statement. It was Mark's way of saying, I am so secure in my relationship with Hannah, and so comfortable with her friendship with you, that I am personally investing in your well-being. It was the ultimate anti-Tom gesture, an act of pure, trusting friendship.

Daniel, understanding the weight of the gesture, simply nodded at Mark with deep respect. After they paid, he was all business. "Okay, we have what we came for. Let's get Jordan back to the van."

Safely back in the minivan, the doors slid shut, and the rotation continued. "My turn," Zoe said softly, sliding into the captain's chair next to Jordan. Seeing he looked drained, she reached into the snack bag. "You must be hungry," she said in her practical way. "It's not quite lunchtime yet, but here."

She handed him a granola bar, and he took it gratefully. The mission was a success. He was safe, and he was cared for, surrounded by a family that thought of everything.

### Part 5: Return to Sanctuary

As the three-car convoy pulled out of the Spradlin Farms parking lot, Jordan saw the large, illuminated sign for the New River Mall Christiansburg mall. A part of him, the curious part that had been sheltered his whole life, felt a pang of desire to see the inside of a real shopping mall for the first time. But it was a small voice, easily drowned out by the overwhelming exhaustion and sensory residue from their trip to Blue Tech. The frantic energy of the crowds, the cacophony of noise and light—it had been too much. More than anything, he wanted to be back in the quiet safety of his dorm room in "The Old Lady," but he knew that wasn't an option. The next best thing was the basement on Geneva Street.

Having accomplished their main objectives, Daniel’s priority shifted from evasion to speed. He chose the direct five-minute highway route that connected with I-81. His knuckles were white on the steering wheel the whole time, his eyes constantly scanning the rearview mirror for a tail that wasn't there.

They pulled onto Geneva Street, the quiet cul-de-sac a welcome sight. But as they approached the house, Caleb’s voice came over the group chat. *"Unfamiliar vehicle parked in front of the Miller's house. Silver SUV."*

Instantly, the group’s training kicked in. As they got out of the cars, they formed a tight, protective bubble around Jordan, shielding him from view as they moved quickly toward the side of the house and the sloping path to the backyard. Just as they reached the basement door, Laura Brooks sent a text to the group. Laura B: *False alarm, everyone. I just texted Susan Miller. It's their new car. They got a Black Friday deal!*

A collective, nervous laugh went through the group. They were safe.

Inside the basement, the adrenaline from the day finally began to drain from Jordan, leaving him shaky and overstimulated. He needed to decompress. He looked across the room and saw Hannah cuddled up comfortably with Mark on one of the air mattresses, a picture of quiet contentment. His eyes then found Lauren. He didn't have to say a word.

She saw the look on his face and immediately came over, kneeling in front of his wheelchair and pulling him into a firm, grounding hug. "It's okay," she whispered, her voice a soothing balm. "You're safe now. We're home. That was a lot, but you did it." This was Tom's ultimate loss, she thought, and Jordan's immeasurable gain. To be cared for so completely, and to be able to care for someone so deeply, was a gift Tom would never understand.

After a few minutes, once Jordan's breathing had steadied, the excitement returned. It was time to set up the new laptop.

Mark took charge, unboxing the new Pinnacle Precisionist 5000 with the reverence of a true tech enthusiast. "Alright," he said, rubbing his hands together. "Let's get this beast running."

He spent the next hour with Jordan, meticulously setting up the machine to his exact preferences, customizing the layout, and installing the programs he'd need for his classes. Most importantly, he went through and uninstalled all the useless "bloatware" that came pre-packaged on the machine.

"You don't need this third-party antivirus trial," he explained, pointing at the screen. "The built-in Quantum Defender on OS 8.1 Pro is all you need, as long as you don't do stupid things like clicking on rogue links that claim to be from your bank."

By the time he was done, the laptop was a clean, powerful, and efficient tool, perfectly tailored to Jordan. The Black Friday mission was a complete success. Jordan sat before his new machine, a gift from his family, in the heart of their sanctuary, completely safe and utterly cherished.

### Part 6: Truth or Dare

They returned to the Brooks' house that evening, tired, laden with shopping bags, but buzzing with the energy of a successful mission. After stowing their new treasures, they all collapsed in their fortress in the basement, full and happy, with no classes or work to worry about.

"Okay, I'm officially too tired to move, but too hyped up to sleep," Madison announced, sitting up from her air mattress. "This is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity for a massive game of Truth or Dare!"
