"We entered a contest to write a song for *this* stadium. We wanted to make something loud enough to scare the hell out of anyone who dared to come into our house!"

The crowd roared, a deafening sound of recognition.

"For thirty years, you guys have been stomping your feet to our song every single Saturday!" Ryan shouted. "So tonight, for the 30th Anniversary... let's see if we can break the seismographs! IGNITION!"

TING!

The single, high-pitched metallic note rang out. Clear and cold.

BOOM!

60,000 people stomped in unison. The ground shook.

TING! BOOM!

The rhythm sped up—*TING-BOOM, TING-BOOM*—building immense tension.

Then, Ryan slammed his hand down on the strings. The heavy, distorted industrial riff exploded, and the stage lights blasted Maroon and Orange into the night.

"The lights go dark, the tension builds..."

Ryan’s vocals were gritty and powerful, a call to battle. When the chorus hit, the entire stadium screamed the response.

"IGNITION!" (Crowd: IGNITION!) "This is the Forger's Call!"

Jordan threw his head back, screaming the words along with his family, feeling the vibration of the stomp in his chest. This wasn't just a song; it was a physical force.

As the song crashed to its chaotic, pyrotechnic finale, Ryan shouted one last time over the feedback.

"WELCOME TO THE CRUCIBLE!"

The crowd noise was deafening. Ryan smiled, breathless.

"Thank you! Oh, it feels so good to be home," he said, wiping sweat from his brow. "We wrote this next one about that feeling... that feeling of finally finding your own personal paradise... right here on this campus."

He signaled the band, ready for the next track. Jordan sat back, his heart racing, his face glowing. The concert had begun.

### Part 4: The Anthem in the Dark

The sun had fully set, and the anticipation in **The Crucible** was reaching a fever pitch. Jordan, Aubrie, Madison, and Megan were settled into their prime spots in **Section 104, Row A**. They were right against the barrier, perfectly positioned for the show.

A massive camera boom, suspended on a crane, swooped down over the crowd, its lens capturing the sea of maroon and orange. Jordan cheered, waving his **Ceremonial Ticket Stub** at the lens, hoping to make the final cut.

"Get that thing out of my face!"

The shout came from directly behind them. Jordan turned his head. A man in the row behind the accessible section—likely a guest of an alumnus given his lack of school colors—was standing up, yelling at a nearby security supervisor.

"I paid good money for these seats!" the man shouted, pointing at the camera crane. "I didn't pay to have a camera blocking my view every two minutes. I didn't sign a release for this! You can't film me without my permission!"

The security supervisor, a woman who looked like she had been dealing with this all night, didn't even blink. She calmly pointed a finger toward the massive digital scoreboard at the end of the field.

On the screen, in fifty-foot-tall letters, was the disclaimer: **NOTICE: FILMING IN PROGRESS.**

"Sir," the supervisor said, her voice polite but bored. "You consented when you scanned your ticket. It was on the purchase page. It is on the signs at the gate. It is on the scoreboard right now. If you do not wish to be recorded, you are free to leave the venue. Otherwise, please sit down."

The man sputtered, looking around for support. "This is ridiculous! Who even does a live album anymore?"

"We do!" Madison yelled, turning around in her seat with a dazzling, defiant grin. "We're Forger Nation! Smile for the camera!"

The students around the man laughed and cheered. Realizing he was outnumbered and legally wrong, the man grumbled and sat back down, crossing his arms.

Jordan turned back to the stage, sharing a conspiratorial grin with Aubrie. He looked at the camera boom swinging overhead again. He didn't care about his permission; he *wanted* the world to see him here. He wanted the proof that he had survived.

"Ready for your close-up?" Aubrie asked, squeezing his hand.

"Born ready," Jordan said.

Moments later, the stadium lights suddenly cut out, plunging The Crucible into a profound, absolute darkness. A roar went up from the crowd of 60,000, but it wasn't the aggressive, territorial roar of a football game that Jordan remembered with such dread. This was different. It was a wave of pure, unified anticipation.

A single, brilliant white spotlight snapped on, cutting through the black void to hit the center of the stage. It illuminated **Cassidy O’Connell**, the band's lead singer. She was standing at the mic stand, gripping it with both hands, vibrating with energy.

Behind her, the band kicked in with a soft melody that shook the ground under Jordan's wheels. It was loud, powerful, and unfamiliar. Jordan leaned forward, intrigued. He didn't know this track.

Cassidy leaned into the mic, her voice booming over the massive sound system, shouting over the roaring crowd and the driving beat.

"Blacksburg! We've been all over the world... but there is no better feeling... Than ending this entire tour... right here at home, in The Crucible!"

The crowd screamed, a sound of pure love. Jordan felt the vibration in his chest, rattling his ribs. Two years ago, that vibration had felt like a threat. Tonight, it felt like a heartbeat.

"Are you with us one last time, Forger Nation?" Cassidy shouted. "Let's go!"

The band surged forward, the guitars crunching and the drums thundering. Jordan watched, mesmerized by the spectacle, the lights sweeping over the sea of maroon and orange. He was enjoying the show, feeling safe between Aubrie and Madison, but he still didn't know what was coming.

Then, at exactly one minute and fifteen seconds in, the instrumental intro cut to a rhythmic pulse, and Cassidy began to sing.

Woke up this morning, the world was gray... Didn't know the colors had all faded away...

The lyrics hit Jordan like a physical shockwave.

His eyes widened, and a gasp stuck in his throat. He knew those words. He knew them by heart. It was **"Electric Color"**—his favorite song, his anthem. But this wasn't the synth-pop track from the 1987 record Mark had played for him in the basement. This was something new. It was a massive, roaring rock anthem version, transformed for the stadium.

Been living in a room with the curtains drawn tight... Then you flipped a switch and turned on the light...

Jordan’s heart exploded with pure, unadulterated joy. The realization washed over him, hot and electric. They were playing *his* song.

In that single instant, the dark, traumatic memory of the "Homecoming Humiliation"—the 38-0 scoreboard, the panic, the flight at halftime—was simply deleted. It was overwritten by this moment. The gray world of his mother's control, the fear of this stadium, the shame of his past... it all evaporated under the bright, crashing waves of the music.

He turned his head and looked at Aubrie. She was watching him, a brilliant smile on her face, singing along. She was the one who had flipped the switch.

He reached out and grabbed her hand, squeezing it as hard as he could. She squeezed back, anchoring him in the present, far away from the gray, and ready for the color to explode.

### Part 5: The Anthem Explodes

The driving beat from the verse didn't let up; it intensified. As Cassidy finished the line about flipping the switch, the tension on stage broke, and the song exploded into the massive, hook-heavy chorus.

Oh, I'm seeing in electric color!

The stage erupted with light. Brilliant beams of neon teal, magenta, and violet shot into the night sky, illuminating the entire bowl of The Crucible. Massive stadium drums thundered, and a soaring synthesizer melody filled the air, lifting the crowd off their feet in a single, unified wave of joy.

Jordan didn't flinch. He didn't cover his ears. He threw his head back and laughed. The sheer, overwhelming volume of the moment washed over him, cleansing him of the fear that had lived in this space for two years. He wasn't scared. The noise wasn't an assault; it was a song. It was the sound of 60,000 people sharing happiness.

It's an electric-colored feeling, And it all started with you!

Madison grabbed his left hand and sang at the top of her lungs, her face glowing in the stage lights. Jordan squeezed back, singing with her, his voice lost in the roar but his spirit soaring.

He looked to his right. Zoe, usually the most reserved and analytical member of their family, was dancing in the aisle. She wasn't analyzing the acoustics or checking the time; she was spinning, her arms in the air, lost in the rhythm. Even Mark and Hannah were swaying together, caught up in the anthem.

The song moved into the bridge. The beat got stronger, the tension building with every measure. The lights pulsed faster. The energy was reaching a fever pitch.

The beat is getting stronger I can't hide it any longer...

Suddenly, the music cut out completely.

The stadium fell into a hushed, electric silence, leaving only the hum of the amplifiers and the collective held breath of the crowd. **Ryan O'Connell**, the lead guitarist, wheeled into the spotlight next to Cassidy. He looked out at the sea of faces—his fellow alumni.

"Can you feel that, CPI?" Cassidy whispered into the mic, his voice intimate and echoing. "That's the sound of a new world. No more gray. Just... color."

Jordan felt a chill run down his spine. It felt as if the band were speaking directly to him, acknowledging the gray prison he had escaped and the vibrant life he had found.

Cassidy pointed a finger toward Ryan. "Here comes the solo!"

Ryan struck a note that screamed through the stadium. The solo soared, a high, piercing melody that seemed to reach up and touch the stars above them. The lights went wild, sweeping across the stands.

Jordan watched, mesmerized. He looked at the field where the football team had fallen, where he had felt so small and defeated. That ghost was gone. This wasn't the place where he had failed. This was the place where he was celebrating. He was surrounded by his pact, by his family, and he was seeing everything in electric color.

### Part 6: The Grand Finale

The song reached its breakdown. The driving 80s beat dropped out, leaving only a shimmering, suspended synth pad that hung in the air like stardust. Cassidy stepped to the edge of the stage, looking out at the sea of 60,000 illuminated faces.

"Just you!" she commanded, pointing to the stands. "Let me hear you sing it!"

She cut the mic.

Sixty thousand voices sang in unison, a massive, acapella choir echoing against the concrete walls of the stadium.

Oh-oh-oh! Electric Color!

Jordan joined in. His voice, usually so quiet, was strong and clear, blending perfectly with the thousands of others. He wasn't just an observer; he was a participant. He was part of the Forger Nation.

"I can't hear you!" Cassidy shouted, cupping her ear. "For the end of the tour!"

The crowd roared the chant again, louder this time, shaking the very foundations of The Crucible.

Oh-oh-oh! Electric Color!

And then, the explosion happened.

Tyler O’Connell stood up behind his kit and slammed into the drums with a force that shook the ground. The full weight of the band crashed back in—Evan's driving bass line locking in with the drums, while Ryan's guitar screamed a high, triumphant melody that soared over the stadium.

The sound was a colossal wall of 80s arena rock power.

"COME ON!"

The finale was a massive explosion of sound and light. Pyrotechnics erupted from the stage roof, shooting pillars of fire into the night sky. Confetti cannons fired from every corner of the field, filling the air with a blizzard of maroon and orange paper.

IT'S AN ELECTRIC-COLORED FEELING AND IT ALL STARTED WITH YOU!

Jordan watched the confetti rain down, the maroon and orange colors no longer symbols of a failed football game, but of a triumphant night. He felt the bass vibrating in his chest, overwriting the memory of the Homecoming Humiliation with a new, indelible memory of joy.

The song ended on a massive, sustained chord, the guitar feedback blending with the swelling synths, punctuated by a giant, final gong hit from Tyler's percussion riser that rang out into the night.

"Thank you Forger Nation! Thank you Blacksburg! We love you! We're home! Goodnight!"

The stage went black for a second before the house lights flooded the stadium, bright and abrupt.

Jordan sat in his chair, covered in maroon and orange confetti, breathless and beaming. His ears were ringing, his heart was racing, and he had never felt more alive. He looked at Aubrie, Madison, and the rest of his family, who were all grinning just as widely.

"That," he said, his voice filled with awe, "was way better than football."

The memory of the 38-0 loss was gone, buried under a mountain of electric color. He had reclaimed The Crucible.

### Part 7: The Departure Loop

The lights of **The Crucible** faded behind them as the Group of 11 navigated the crush of 60,000 fans exiting the stadium. The air was cool, filled with the smell of exhaust and the buzzing, high-energy chatter of the post-concert crowd.

They reached the **Crucible Bus Loop**, which had been transformed into a massive, humming transit hub. A long line of Blue Ridge Transit buses idled in the dedicated lanes, their engines rumbling in a low unison.

As they moved toward their designated sector, they passed a knot of people standing near a pole marked **ROANOKE EXPRESS**. They were shouting at a weary-looking transit supervisor.

"What do you mean it's gone?" a man in a denim jacket yelled. "We need to get back to the Third Street Station!"

"Sir, as the signs stated," the supervisor said, pointing to a large, illuminated board, "the last Roanoke Express departed at 7:45 PM. RVT service has ended for the night."

"But the concert just ended!" a woman next to him cried. "How are we supposed to get home?"

"There is no return service," the supervisor repeated. "You will need to call a cab or a rideshare."

Jordan watched them as he rolled past, feeling a profound relief that his family had planned their logistics with military precision.

Just past the angry Roanoke crowd, however, was a very different scene. At the **Bluewater Transit** bay, a large, familiar coach bus was idling, its engine purring. It was the last **Maroon Express** of the night.

**Sign:** MAROON EXPRESS ... BLUEWATER via FALLING BRANCH **Voice:** "Bluewater Transit Maroon Express: Bluewater via Falling Branch."

"Look at that," Madison said, pointing to the happy fans boarding the coach. "My hometown bus didn't leave them stranded. 10:15 PM departure. Right on time."

Unlike the Roanoke fans, the Bluewater crew was cheering, high-fiving the driver as they boarded. The concert had finished just in time for the final scheduled run, saving them from being stuck.

They continued to **Sector 4**. The Blue Ridge Transit queue was organized chaos.

To their left, a bus idled with its doors open. **Sign:** FOXRIDGE ... FORGERS SHUTTLE **Voice:** *"Shuttle to Foxridge. GO FORGERS!"*

Ahead of them, another bus was loading a massive crowd of students. **Sign:** HETHWOOD ... FORGERS SHUTTLE **Voice:** *"Shuttle to Hethwood. GO FORGERS!"*

And right in front of them, waiting at the curb with the ramp already deploying, was their bus. **Sign:** FALLING BRANCH ... FORGERS SHUTTLE **Voice:** *"Shuttle to Falling Branch. GO FORGERS!"*

"That's us," Daniel said, cutting through the noise. "Let's go home."

Jordan wheeled himself up the ramp. The driver, looking exhausted but happy, nodded at him. "Great show, right?"

"The best," Jordan said.

He maneuvered into the securement spot. Aubrie took the seat next to him, and the rest of the family filled the rows behind them. The doors hissed shut, cutting off the noise of the crowd.

"Next stop, Falling Branch," the automated voice announced, followed by one last, triumphant, *"GO FORGERS!"*

The bus pulled away, merging onto the dedicated lane and leaving the chaos of the stadium in the rearview mirror. Jordan leaned his head on Aubrie's shoulder, his ears ringing with the phantom sound of the gong, his heart full of electric color. The night was over, but the memory was just beginning.

# Book: Summer 2016

## Chapter 1: The Foundation

### Part 1: The Pact

The conversation happened quietly one evening in the basement "Adults' Wing" of the newly completed compound.

It was the summer of 2016. They had just finished their sophomore year at CPI, and while graduation was still two years away, the air was thick with the excitement of settling into their permanent home.

The **seven** of them—Jordan, Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and **Megan**—were sprawled out together in their familiar "puppy pile" in the **Sunken Sanctuary**. They lay on the massive, custom-built foam mattress that filled the pit, a sea of comfort that held them all safely.

They had been discussing the practicalities of their future, and the topic of legal marriage had come up.

"It should be one of us," Zoe said, her voice cutting through the comfortable silence with characteristic logic. "For legal reasons. Power of attorney, next of kin... we need a firewall against his mother if something goes wrong."

A gentle quiet fell over the group. Jordan looked at the faces of the women he loved, a wave of emotion making it hard to speak.

It was Madison who broke the silence. She shifted, propping herself up on an elbow to look directly at Aubrie.

"It has to be you, Aubrie," she said, her voice soft but certain.

Aubrie looked surprised, her brow furrowed. "Mads, why? We're all..." She trailed off, but her thought was clear in the eyes of everyone present: *We're all in love with him as coequals.*

"Because you were first," Madison said, her voice full of a fierce, protective love. "That very first night. When he was so broken and scared he couldn't even speak."

*Broken?* The word echoed in Jordan's mind, a strange and distant descriptor. He didn't feel broken. He felt whole, strong, surrounded by a love so complete it had rebuilt him from the ground up. He looked down at his own hands, steady and sure, and realized with a jolt that he hadn't seen that shy, terrified boy in the mirror for a very long time.

Madison continued, her voice unwavering. "You were the one who crawled into that bed and just held him. You didn't ask questions, you didn't try to fix him. You were just... there. You were the first person to show him an ounce of kindness in his entire life. That's a place none of the rest of us can ever claim. It's only right that it's you."

Megan, who was curled up on Jordan’s other side, nodded slowly. "She's right," she said softly. "I came later, and I love this family more than anything, but even I can see it. Aubrie is the foundation we all built on."

Tears welled in Aubrie's eyes as she looked at Madison and Megan, a wave of overwhelming gratitude washing over her.

Jordan reached out, his hand covering Madison’s. He looked from her clear, steady gaze to Aubrie's tear-streaked face, his heart so full it felt like it might burst.

"I love you both," he whispered, his voice thick with emotion.

He then looked around at the other four women—Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and Megan—his gaze lingering on each of them.

"I love all **six** of you. So much. And I am so, so grateful that this... us... works. That there's no jealousy, no heartache, no confusion or competition. Just this."

"We will always be coequals," Zoe said, her voice firm with conviction. "That never changes."

"But Madison's right," Lauren added softly, and Sarah nodded in agreement. "That first night... that's the reason. It has to be."

And so, surrounded by the warmth and weight of their collective love, they all agreed. It was their path forward. They would all remain coequals in love and life, but when the time came, Aubrie would be the one to legally marry Jordan.

The reason was singular and sacred: she was the one who had climbed into bed and comforted a scared, broken boy on his very first night of freedom, an act of kindness that had planted the seed for the extraordinary family that had bloomed around them. It was a foundation that could never be equaled.Chapter: The Double Wedding

## Chapter 2: The Double Wedding

### Part 1: The Morning Of

The morning of **Saturday, July 16, 2016**, dawned bright and clear over the newly completed Kelly Family Compound on U.S. 11. The usual calm of their rural sanctuary was replaced by a low, buzzing hum of excited energy.

Down in the basement "Adults' Wing"—the massive, custom-built space they had moved into just weeks before—the first to stir was Hannah. She was too keyed up to sleep, her heart a happy flutter in her chest. She slipped out of the "Anderson Suite," the private bedroom she shared with Mark, leaving him to sleep for a few more precious minutes. As she padded past the double doors of the adjacent "Pact Suite," she smiled. Behind those doors lay the "Sunken Sanctuary," where Jordan and his six partners—Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and Megan—were likely still entangled in their massive, comfortable "puppy pile."

She headed up the main stairs to the great room and found she wasn't the first one awake. Daniel and Laura Brooks were already in the massive kitchen, the aroma of fresh coffee filling the air.

"Morning, sweetie," Laura said, her smile as warm as the sunrise streaming through the large windows. "Couldn't sleep either?"

"Not a wink," Hannah admitted with a grin.

One by one, the rest of the family emerged from the basement.

- **Mark**, looking surprisingly well-rested as he joined Hannah from their private suite.

- **Zoe and Lauren**, the first to emerge from the Pact Suite, already reviewing the logistical timeline on a tablet with quiet efficiency.

- **Sarah and Madison**, practically vibrating with excitement.

- **Megan**, checking her camera gear to ensure she captured every moment for the family archives.

- And finally, **Jordan and Aubrie**, holding hands, their faces glowing with a serene, profound happiness born of the deep, committed love they shared with their entire pact.

Breakfast was a deliberately simple affair, a small island of normalcy in a day that would be anything but. There was no big, fancy meal. Toast was made, fruit was passed around, and Jordan, true to his routine, sat at the massive dining table with a simple bowl of cereal and milk, a comforting and familiar start to the most important day of his life.

After breakfast, the organized chaos began. The group split off to shower and get dressed in their respective suites. The sounds of hairdryers, laughing, and last-minute steaming of dresses and suits filled the house.

Finally, it was time. Looking resplendent in their wedding attire—the women in their beautiful, coordinated dresses, Mark in his sharp suit, and Jordan looking handsome and confident in his own tuxedo—they all gathered in the great room.

Caleb Brooks, who had arrived earlier from his own place, gave his dad a nod. "I'm heading out now, Dad. I'll get the van parked near the ballroom and wait for you all there." With a wave, he was gone, their quiet backup plan in motion.

The family then walked down the long driveway together to the newly paved bus stop at the end of their property. As the **Blue Ridge Transit Route 3** bus—a standard 35-foot city bus running its regular route between Christiansburg and Radford—approached and hissed to a stop, Jordan felt a surge of pride.

He maneuvered his wheelchair onto the ramp and into the bus with a practiced ease he hadn't possessed just a few short years ago. He didn't need a special ride. He didn't need a favor. He was taking himself to his own wedding.

His family filed in behind him, their happy chatter filling the front of the bus. As the vehicle pulled away from their home and headed toward the **Christiansburg Pepper Plaza Transit Center**, Jordan looked out the window, his hand held firmly in Aubrie's.

He was independent. He was free. And he was surrounded by everyone he loved.

### Part 2: The Final Touches

The bus ride to **Pepper Plaza** was short and filled with a happy, nervous energy. As they disembarked, the weather seemed to smile on them; it was a perfect, glorious **July day**, warm and sunny, with a gentle summer breeze rustling the full, green leaves on the trees.

Caleb Brooks was already there, waving from the parking lot where he had stashed the family van, having successfully transported the decorations and supplies ahead of the bus.

The venue, "**The Blacksburg Ballroom**," was a beautifully restored historic building right next to the courthouse. The main ballroom was on the second floor, and the family took the large elevator up, the doors opening to reveal a sun-drenched space with high ceilings and polished wood floors.

The room was already largely set up—the chairs arranged in neat rows, the flowers delivered—but it needed the family's personal touch to be perfect. **Zoe**, armed with a clipboard and a determined expression, immediately stepped into her role as the day's logistical commander.

"Okay, team, we have thirty minutes until doors open," Zoe announced, her voice calm but authoritative. "Let's optimize this setup."

She pointed to the tables. "**Lauren**, let's make sure the centerpieces are perfect. **Megan**, can you help her? And please keep that camera ready; we need documentation of the 'before' state."

"On it," Megan said with a bright smile, adjusting the strap of her camera before moving to help Lauren tweak a floral arrangement.

"**Mark**, can you and **Sarah** double-check the sound system? We need to ensure the microphones are balanced for the vows," Zoe continued.

"**Hannah**, let's you and I make sure the guest book table is exactly right. We need the pens tested and the signage visible."

While the others buzzed around with their tasks, creating a hive of purposeful activity, **Daniel and Laura Brooks** took a moment to stand with the two people at the center of it all.

"How are you feeling, son?" Daniel asked Jordan, placing a warm, grounding hand on his shoulder.

"Good," Jordan said, and he meant it. He looked around the beautiful, sunlit room, at the faces of his family all working together—Megan laughing with Lauren, Mark testing a mic check with Sarah, Zoe and Hannah aligning the guest book. "I feel... ready."

"You look beautiful, sweetie," Laura said to **Aubrie**, reaching out to gently adjust a small, stray piece of her hair. "Both of you do."

She looked over at **Hannah** across the room, who was beaming as she worked next to Zoe. Laura’s eyes misted over slightly. "All of you. This whole family looks beautiful today."

The room was filled with a quiet, purposeful hum. It wasn't the frantic energy of a stressful event, but the calm, coordinated effort of a family working together to make sure every detail was perfect. They weren't just setting up a venue; they were putting the final, loving touches on the first day of the rest of their lives.

### Part 3: The Gathering

With the last centerpiece adjusted and the final sound check complete, a hush of anticipation fell over The Blacksburg Ballroom. It was time.

**Daniel and Laura Brooks**, acting as the patriarch and matriarch for the entire extended clan, took their places near the entrance to greet the arriving guests. They were joined by the women of the pact—**Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and Megan**.

While the others stood in a receiving line, **Megan** moved with a happy, purposeful energy. She balanced her role as a greeter with her role as the family archivist, pausing between hugs to snap beautiful, candid photos of guests entering the sunlit space, capturing the genuine joy on their faces.

The two couples—**Jordan and Aubrie**, and **Mark and Hannah**—remained tucked away in a small side room, out of sight until the ceremony began, holding hands and breathing in the reality of the moment.

A small, elegant sign on an easel near the guest book set the tone for the event: *"Choose a seat, not a side. We're all family here."*

The first to arrive were the extended **Brooks clan**—a happy, boisterous flood of aunts, uncles, and cousins who enveloped Daniel and Laura in warm hugs. They were followed by **Hannah’s family (the Parkers)** and **Mark’s family (the Andersons)**, who had traveled in from different parts of the state.

There was a joyful energy as the families met, many for the first time. They shook hands and introduced themselves not as separate entities or in-laws, but as the newly connected branches of one large, sprawling family tree. Caleb, Owen, Lucas, and Jacob Brooks acted as ushers, guiding everyone not to specific sides, but simply to open chairs, physically mixing the families together.

Soon, the room began to fill with a diverse mix of friends and colleagues. Classmates from **CPI**, their faces familiar from late-night study groups and shared projects, came to support their friends.

For Jordan, watching from the crack in the side room door, seeing them arrive was particularly meaningful. These were people who knew him simply as "**Jordan**," the intelligent and kind classmate from Astrophysics, not as the sheltered, terrified boy from his past. A few of his coworkers from his part-time job at the campus **library** arrived, clapping him on the shoulder in spirit, wishing him well.

The absence of certain people was a quiet, unspoken statement of the family's victory. **No one from the Sinclair or Fuller families was present.** They hadn't been invited; they didn't even know this day was happening. Jordan's past was completely and totally severed from his present.

Every person in the room was there because they loved and supported him, Hannah, Mark, and Aubrie for *who* they were now.

As the guests found their seats, filling the two curved sections of the hall, the room buzzed with happy, low chatter. There was no division, no "bride's side" or "groom's side." There was only a single, unified community, a testament to the life and the family the eight of them—plus Megan, Bob, and Emily—had built together, ready to witness the beautiful ceremony that was about to begin.

### Part 4: The Vows

The music began, a soft, beautiful melody that silenced the happy chatter in the ballroom. All eyes turned to the wide central aisle.

At the front, **Mark Anderson** stood waiting, his expression a perfect mix of awe and adoration. Beside him, **Jordan Kelly**, dressed in a sharp tuxedo, sat in his wheelchair. His hands were resting calmly in his lap, but his heart pounded a steady, joyful rhythm as he locked his gaze on the doors at the back of the room.

**Hannah** came down the aisle first, on the arm of her father, looking radiant and serene. She joined Mark, and the two of them beamed at each other, a silent promise passing between them.

Then, the music swelled. **Aubrie Jean** came down the aisle on the arm of her own father, Thomas Kelly. He was a kind-faced man with eyes that shone with pride and unshed tears as he escorted his daughter to her future.

The room was packed with the people who made this moment possible. In the front rows sat the families who had embraced this unconventional union without hesitation.

- **Daniel and Laura Brooks**, the parents who had taken them all in.

- **Diane and David Scott**, Madison’s parents, sat beaming. Between them sat two teenagers who were watching with wide, awestruck eyes: **Bob Scott** and **Emily Jenkins**.

- **Sarah’s father**, Mr. Barnes, sat proudly nearby, having fully embraced the family that had given his daughter a home.

- **Lauren’s parents**, the Blacks, were there, witnessing their daughter’s happiness.

Thomas placed Aubrie's hand in Jordan's, gave his new son-in-law a warm, approving nod, and then took his seat. The two families, now officially joined, smiled at each other.

The two couples stood together, a perfect, happy foursome, ready to make their vows.

But before the officiant could begin, he smiled kindly. "Before we proceed," he announced, "there is another vow that must be made first. A promise to be made between family."

He looked to the first row, where **Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and Megan** sat together.

They stood and came to the front, forming a semi-circle around Jordan and Aubrie.

The room went completely still. Everyone present understood the unique dynamic of this family. They knew that Jordan loved all **six** of these women as equal romantic partners. This ceremony wasn't about saying goodbye; it was about honoring the structure that allowed them all to thrive. It was an acknowledgment that while they were all his wives in spirit, Aubrie was the foundation upon which their lives were built.

**Lauren** stepped forward first, her voice clear and strong, addressing not just the couple, but the entire room. "Our family is built on a promise," she began, "a pact of love and support that extends to everyone standing up here. As part of that pact, the six of us share a deep, loving, and romantic bond with Jordan. Today, as he and Aubrie take this beautiful and important step into marriage, we want to honor that pact, and their union, with a promise of our own. This is the Releasing of Jordan."

She turned her warm gaze to Jordan. "You took my broken heart and showed me what real, selfless kindness was. I release you to the one who taught you first."

**Sarah** came next, her eyes shining with tears. "You took in a stranger and called her your family from day one. I release you to the one who is your truest home."

**Zoe** followed, her words logical yet full of profound emotion. "Our cooperative dynamic is the most efficient and supportive system I have ever been a part of. To ensure its continued success, I release you to her."

**Megan** stepped forward next. She looked at Jordan, the boy she had helped nurse back to health, the man who had become her heart. "You gave me a place to belong when I was lost. I release you to the one who built the foundation."

Finally, **Madison** took Jordan's free hand. "I was there in Room 214 with you two," she said, her voice thick with love and history. "I watched a shy, scared boy who was afraid of the dark become the man who stands before us today. And I know he became that man because you, Aubrie Kelly, were the first light in his darkness."

After the “Releasing of the Groom” ceremony, Madison gently placed Jordan's hand in Aubrie's. The five women—his partners, his loves, his family—returned to their seats, leaving a profound and sacred silence in their wake. There was not a dry eye in the house.

The officiant then turned to Mark and Hannah.

"I, **Mark Shawn Anderson**," Mark began, his voice thick with emotion, "take you, **Hannah Ashlee Parker**, to be my wife. They say every good team needs a rock, an anchor. You're my anchor. You're the anchor for all of us. I promise to be your rock, to support you, to love you, and to build a life with you that is as strong and steady as you are. I love you."

Hannah smiled through her tears. "I, **Hannah Ashlee Parker**, take you, **Mark Shawn Anderson**, to be my husband. You are the kindest, most decent man I have ever known. You see the best in everyone, and you make us all better for it. I promise to love you, to laugh with you, and to always be your partner in this crazy, wonderful life we've built. I love you, and I am so proud to become Hannah Parker Anderson."

Then, all eyes turned to Jordan and Aubrie. But before Jordan could speak his vows to her, Mark stepped forward slightly.

"Jordan," Mark said, his voice firm with brotherly love. "You are the brother of my heart. In front of everyone here, I offer you my middle name, **Shawn**, to take as your own, as a sign of our bond."

Jordan looked at his best man, his brother, and nodded, his eyes shining with unshed tears. He then turned to his bride.

"Aubrie," he began, his voice clear and steady. "Before I met you, my world was small, and it was dark. I didn't know what it felt like to be safe. You were the first person who ever saw me, not just my chair or my diagnosis. You didn't just open a door for me; you built a whole new world and invited me in. I promise to spend the rest of my life exploring that world with you, to love you, and to be the man you always knew I could be."

Aubrie looked at him, her heart in her eyes. "Jordan... I used to look up at the stars and feel so small, so alone. I felt like a single particle drifting in a giant, empty universe. The day I met you, it was like a star fell to earth. You are my other half, my missing piece. You make my universe whole."

She took a deep breath, her voice full of a love that was a gift.

"And so, I, **Aubrie Jean Kelly**, take you, Jordan, to be my husband. And I offer you my last name, **Kelly**, to take as your own, to make our family whole."

The officiant beamed. "By the power vested in me, and with the new names you have so lovingly chosen, I now pronounce you, and you, husbands and wives."

The room erupted in a joyous, tearful standing ovation. But the celebration wasn't starting just yet. Here, in front of his family, **Jordan Shawn Kelly** had just been born, but in the eyes of the Commonwealth of Virginia, there was one final, necessary step to take. The reception would begin in exactly as long as it took for a court hearing. Everything had been meticulously pre-approved by a sympathetic Judge and Clerk of Court; the hearing was a formality, a final seal on a done deal. It was time to make it legal.

### Part 5: The Legal Seal

As the last echoes of the joyful applause faded in the ballroom, the officiant smiled and addressed the guests.

"Ladies and gentlemen, the reception will begin shortly. Please enjoy the refreshments and each other's company while the families handle one final piece of official business next door. The happy couples will join you soon."

While the guests began to mingle, the **"Group of 17"**—the two newlywed couples, the five other women of the Pact (Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and Megan), the Brooks parents (Daniel and Laura), the Scott parents (David and Diane with Bob and Emily), Sarah's father, and Lauren's parents—slipped out the side door.

Their destination was just a few steps away: the **Montgomery County Courthouse**.

Weeks ago, Daniel Brooks had made a special appointment. He had explained the unique circumstances of Jordan's life and his wedding day to a sympathetic court clerk, who had passed the story along. The paperwork was ready; the docket was cleared.

They entered the quiet, stately building and were ushered immediately into a small, private courtroom where a judge was waiting for them, his expression kind and welcoming.

"Mr. Fuller," the judge began, his voice gentle but formal. "Please approach."

For the very last time in his life, he was **Aubrey Jordan Fuller**.

He wheeled himself forward, his massive, blended family forming a silent, supportive phalanx behind him.

The legal proceedings were brief, a formality that had been well-prepared in advance.

The judge reviewed the petition, his eyes scanning the documents before him.

"This court," the judge said, looking up and directly at Jordan, "recognizes that a name is more than just a word. It is the foundation of an identity. It is clear to me that the name 'Aubrey Jordan Fuller' represents a past that this young man has, with courage and with the love of this extraordinary family, overcome."

He picked up a pen and signed the official order with a firm, deliberate flourish.

He then looked up, his formal demeanor replaced by a warm, genuine smile.

"By the power vested in me by the Commonwealth of Virginia, I hereby grant this petition. **Mr. Kelly**," he said, the new name ringing with the force of law, "congratulations on your wedding day. Please consider this my gift to you."

It was done. Aubrey Jordan Fuller was gone, erased by the stroke of a pen.

He turned his wheelchair and faced his family. He was, now and forever, legally and officially, **Jordan Shawn Kelly**.

Together, the entire happy, complete family walked out of the courthouse and back toward their reception, ready to celebrate the first day of their new life.

### Part 7: The First Night

The last **Route 3** bus of the evening hummed quietly as it carried them home along the dark ribbon of U.S. 11. The boisterous energy of the reception had faded, replaced by a comfortable, contented exhaustion. Outside the windows, the humid July air was filled with the song of crickets.

Caleb Brooks, having already driven the van back with the decorations, was waiting for them at the end of the long driveway when the bus pulled away.

The walk up to the house was quiet. Inside the massive, silent compound, the two happy couples officially parted ways for the night.

Mark scooped a sleepy Hannah up into his arms and carried her, bridal-style, toward their private suite on the second floor, whispering goodnight to the others.

The remaining **seven**—Jordan, Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and **Megan**—made their way down to the basement "Adults' Wing."

The day, with all its joy and emotional weight, had taken its toll. Jordan, especially, was drained, both mentally and physically. He navigated his wheelchair into their master suite—the "Sunken Sanctuary"—and let out a long, tired sigh.

He wheeled himself to the edge of the pit and looked down. Bathed in the soft, ambient light, the massive, custom-built 15-foot bed looked like a calm blue sea of comfort waiting for them.

Aubrie came and knelt beside his chair. "Tired?" she asked softly.

He nodded, reaching for her hand. "So tired. Can we... can we just go to bed?"

He looked around at the **six** women who were his entire world.

"But," he added, his voice soft but firm, "I still... I want everyone here. I want the puppy pile."

A wave of gentle, understanding smiles went through the room. There was never any doubt.

"Of course," Madison said, her voice full of warmth. "Wouldn't have it any other way."

The pact still existed. The no-jealousy rule was as solid as the foundation of the house. **Aubrie Jean Kelly** was his wife, but **Madison Elizabeth Scott** was still his girlfriend, and his love for Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, and **Megan** was just as deep and real as it had been that morning.

There was a quiet, unspoken agreement as they all prepared for bed. This was their sanctuary, their most intimate space. As they had done many nights before, they slipped into their finest birthday suits, a comfortable and natural expression of the romantic bond they all shared.

Jordan wheeled his chair to the top of the gentle wooden ramp that ran along the wall. He rolled down the incline, the rubber tires humming on the smooth wood, until he reached the **Transfer Dock** at the bottom.

He parked his chair and locked the brakes. The seat was perfectly flush with the surface of the massive foam mattress.

With a simple, practiced shift of his weight, he slid from the hard seat of his chair onto the soft, welcoming surface of the bed. No lifting. No climbing. Just ease.

He scooted to the middle of the vast sea of foam, grounding himself.

**Aubrie** curled up on his left side, her head resting on his chest. **Madison** curled up on his right. **Megan**, moving with the comfort of someone who had truly found her home, settled in next to Madison. **Zoe, Lauren, and Sarah** found their familiar spots, the incredible 15-foot width of the pit giving everyone plenty of room to sprawl while still touching.

It was his first night as a married man, but nothing had really changed. He was simply home, surrounded by his family, safe in the heart of the pit.

He closed his eyes, listening to the soft, steady breathing of the six women he loved, and fell into the most peaceful sleep of his life.

# Book: Fall 2016

## Chapter 1: A Familiar Face

### Part 1: Reunion in the Hallway

It was the first day of their junior year, Fall 2016. The Group of 8 was gathered in the hallway outside their Latin 101 class, a small classroom designed for around thirty students. Jordan Shawn Kelly, his legal name now reflecting his new life, was chatting with Aubrie, his wife, while Mark and Madison argued playfully about the upcoming football season.

A new student approached, looking at the room number, her face a little nervous. She glanced at the group, and then her gaze locked onto Jordan. She stopped, her expression shifting from uncertainty to a look of profound, hopeful shock.

"Aubrey?" she asked, her voice hesitant.

Jordan looked up from his conversation with Aubrie. He froze. He couldn't believe it. He was looking into those same warm, welcoming hazel eyes he hadn't seen in four years. The face was more mature, but the kindness was identical.

"Jessica?" he breathed, his voice full of shocked disbelief. "Is it really you?"

"Yes, Aubrey, it's me! It's Jessica!" she said, her own smile widening as tears welled in her eyes.

The rest of the Group of 8 went quiet, instantly recognizing that something powerful and private was happening. Aubrie, his wife, watched, her expression open and supportive.

"Oh, Jess," Jordan said, his voice thick with emotion. Jordan was the only one ever allowed to call her Jess. "I missed you so much."

He pushed his wheelchair forward slightly, his arms opening. "I go by Jordan now," he said, just as he pulled her into a hug that was four long, painful years in the making.

Jessica knelt to meet his embrace, holding him tightly. "I missed you, too," she whispered into his shoulder, her voice cracking. "I thought about you all the time. I... I'm so sorry about your mother. I never..."

"I know," he said, pulling back. He understood. He had been rescued by his mother's actions, even as she had tried to destroy his life.

The Group of 8 looked on, seeing the profound, healing weight of the moment for their friend. Jessica finally stood, wiping a tear from her eye. Jordan, his face glowing, turned to the woman at his side.

"Jess, this is... my family," he said. He started with the woman holding his hand. "This is my wife, Aubrie Jean Kelly."

Jessica’s eyes went wide, but she smiled, seeing the pure, unconditional love between them. Jordan then proceeded to introduce her to Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, Hannah, and Mark.

"Wow," Jessica said, looking at the circle of kind, smiling faces. "It's... it's amazing to meet all of you."

"You, too," Hannah said warmly, speaking for the group. "It looks like we're in the same class. You should sit with us, unless there are assigned seats".

Jessica looked back at Jordan—the boy she had tried to save, who was now a man surrounded by a fortress of love. She smiled, a real, genuine smile. "I'd like that. I'd like that a lot."

### Part 2: The Cosmic Coincidence

The Latin 101 class ended. The "Group of 8" gathered their things, buzzing with the excitement of the reunion. As they filed out of the small classroom, the group began to split off.

"Alright, J, we'll see you at lunch," Mark said, giving him a supportive nod as he and Hannah headed toward the engineering quad.

It was just Jordan, Aubrie, and Jessica left in the hallway. Jordan turned to Jessica, his heart full but already bracing for the goodbye. "It was so good to see you, Jess. I still can't believe it. Which way are you heading?"

"Oh," Jessica said, pointing down the main walkway. "I have to get to the science complex. My next class is Astrophysics."

Jordan and Aubrie both stopped, staring at her. "No way!" Jordan said, his voice full of excitement. "Us too! You should walk with us".

Jessica immediately looked hesitant, her gaze flickering to Aubrie. She was acutely aware that this was his *wife*. "Are you sure?" she asked, her voice quiet. "I don't want to intrude on you two."

Aubrie, seeing the hesitation, immediately stepped in, her smile warm and genuine. "Intrude? Don't be silly. Of course you're walking with us". She looped her arm through Jessica's. "What professor do you have?"

"Dr. Alistair, at 10:30," Jessica replied.

Jordan and Aubrie stopped again, this time looking at each other in shared, stunned delight. "Us too!" Jordan nearly shouted. "And the Tuesday morning lab section?"

"Yes!" Jessica said, finally letting herself believe the coincidence. "I can't believe it!"

"Then it's settled," Aubrie said, her voice full of a happy finality as she started walking, pulling Jessica along with her. "You're sitting with us".

Jordan wheeled his chair forward, placing himself between Aubrie and Jessica. He was so excited. He couldn't believe it. The friend he had lost, the one his mother had stolen from him, was back. And now, by some miracle, she was in not only his Latin class, but also his Astrophysics class. His past and his present had just collided, and his world was, once again, made whole.

### Part 3: An Unexpected Free Block

The trio made their way to the science complex, arriving at the large lecture hall for their 10:30 AM Astrophysics class. But when they got to the door, they saw a single piece of paper taped to the glass.

"Oh, look," Aubrie said, leaning in to read it. "Class is canceled. The professor is sick".

"Huh," Jordan said, a little disappointed, but mostly relieved. "Well, that gives us a 50-minute free block". He turned to Jessica, his face bright. "We can actually talk. We should go grab a drink at Newman Hall."

Jessica's smile immediately faltered. She glanced nervously at Aubrie, then back at Jordan. "Oh, I shouldn't... I mean, you two are married. I don't want to intrude on your time".

Aubrie just laughed, a warm, genuine sound that dismissed the very idea. "Intrude? Jessica, you're the first real friend he ever had. You're not intruding, you're catching up. We insist. We're not letting you go".

Jessica still looked hesitant, her high school memories of social rules and boundaries making her anxious. Aubrie, seeing this, decided to be more direct. She gently took Jessica's hand. Then she took Jordan's hand. She placed Jessica's hand into Jordan's and gently, deliberately, laced their fingers together.

"See?" Aubrie said, her voice full of bliss as she looked at their joined hands. "No jealousy. He's your friend. Hold his hand".

Jessica was stunned. She was holding a married man's hand, and his *wife* was smiling at her. It was the most radical, unconditional act of trust she had ever witnessed.

Jordan's grip tightened on her hand, a grounding, familiar connection. He needed to tell her. "Jess," he said, using the old nickname that only he was allowed to use. "So much has happened. I... I never got to thank you. My mother..."

At the mention of his mother, Jessica flinched, the memory still sharp and painful. She **remembers this** all too well. She wasn't just told to stay away; Aubrie Sinclair had found her home phone number. Jessica remembered the rude and nasty voice on the line, the sound of her own mother's shocked silence as Aubrie Sinclair went on a tirade, telling them that Jessica was a manipulative predator and was to have nothing to do with Aubrey ever again.

Seeing the fear flash in Jessica's eyes, Jordan's grip tightened. "It's okay, Jess. She's... she's not..."

"She's gone," Aubrie Kelly said, her voice calm and absolute. She sat on Jessica's other side, taking her free hand. "Jessica, I need you to hear this. **Jordan has cut his mother completely out of his life**".

Aubrie proceeded to give her the short, factual version of the war they had all survived: the failed Fall Break confrontation, the official no-contact orders filed with the university and the police, and the final, humiliating court battle where a judge had dismissed his mother's guardianship case with prejudice.

"She has no power," Aubrie finished. "She can't find him, and she can't hurt him. Not ever again. **You are absolutely welcomed back into Jordan’s life**".

Jessica looked from Aubrie's fierce, protective face to Jordan's, who was nodding, his eyes full of relief. The fear that she had carried for four years—the fear of that horrible woman—finally began to dissolve.

"Now," Jordan said, a quiet pride in his voice. "About that 4.0 GPA...".

He continued his story, telling her about the Homecoming Humiliation, and then his voice dropped. "But... it hasn't all been easy. Last year... Jess, I had a heart attack".

Jessica's head snapped toward him, her eyes wide with terror. "Wait, what? A heart attack? Jordan, you're only 20! What do you mean, a heart attack?".

Aubrie, sitting on Jessica's other side, took her hand. "It's true," she said softly, and she began to fill in the details of the Homecoming Humiliation, the failed balloon angioplasty, and the open-heart CABG surgery.

Jordan's grip tightened on Jessica's hand as his wife spoke, the memory of the trauma still fresh. Jessica was reeling, trying to process everything: his new life, his marriage, his near-death, and the impossible, wonderful fact that she was holding his hand while his wife, Aubrie Kelly, looked on with nothing but love. This was a family unlike any she had ever known, and she knew, with a sudden, dawning clarity, that she wanted to be a part of it.

### Part 4: The Fortress of Truth (Redux)

With their 50-minute Astrophysics block unexpectedly free, the trio decided to walk from the science complex to Newman Hall to grab a coffee and find a quiet place to talk. Jordan’s heart was hammering, a mixture of joy and disbelief. He was still holding Jessica’s hand, his fingers laced with hers.

They found a quiet table in a corner of the bustling student union. Jessica was still trying to process the situation. She was holding a married man's hand, and his wife, Aubrie, was sitting right next to her, smiling and encouraging it. It was a wonderful, terrifying, and completely illogical feeling.

"It's almost lunchtime," Aubrie said, pulling out her mPhone. "We should get the others."

She sent a quick message to the Jordan Comms chat. "Hey everyone, class was canceled. Jordan and I are already at Newman Hall. We've got Jessica with us, and we have news we'd like to share".

A few minutes later, the rest of the Group of 8—Mark, Hannah, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, and Sarah—arrived, their own morning classes finished.

"Hey!" Madison called out, her voice cheerful as they approached the table. "What's the big—"

She stopped, her eyes landing on Jordan's and Jessica's intertwined hands. The rest of the group saw it, too. Jessica tensed, a flush rising on her neck, bracing herself for the inevitable confrontation.

But the confrontation never came. The Group of 8 was completely unfazed. They were used to Jordan being openly affectionate with the women of his pact; to them, seeing him hold hands with a new woman wasn't shocking, just new.

"So, who's your new friend, J?" Mark asked, grabbing a chair and sitting down.

"That's the news," Jordan said, his voice thick with emotion. He looked at Aubrie, who gave him an encouraging nod. "Guys, this is Jessica. She's... she's not a new friend."

The group leaned in, and Jordan, still holding Jessica's hand, told them the entire story. He told them how he and Jess—a nickname he alone was allowed to use —had a secret relationship back in high school, how she was his first real friend and girlfriend. He explained how she was also autistic and understood him, and how his mother, Aubrie Sinclair, had discovered their secret.

His voice grew cold as he recounted how his mother, in a bitter rage, had called Jessica's parents with rude, nasty accusations, forbidding him from ever seeing her again and successfully shutting down the only good relationship he had ever known.

The group was silent, stunned. This wasn't just a new friend; this was the *original* victim of his mother's abuse.

Hannah, the group's mom friend, was the first to speak, her eyes full of warmth as she looked at Jessica. "Oh, Jessica," she said, her voice soft. "I am so, so sorry that happened to you. But you're here now."

"Yeah," Madison added, her voice firm. "You're with us now." She smiled, breaking the tension. "And we're all starving. Jessica, you have to join us for lunch".

Jessica looked around at the circle of kind, smiling, and fiercely protective faces. She looked down at Jordan, who was squeezing her hand. "I'd love to," she whispered.

### Part 5: The Separation Crash

The first day of their junior year, Monday, was drawing to a close. The group of nine—the Group of 8 plus Jessica—had spent the entire day together, moving from Latin, to lunch, to their shared Astrophysics lecture, and then to the library for a study session. Jordan was vibrating with a joy he hadn't felt in years, his hand securely holding Jessica's.

They arrived at the CPI Transit Center, the central hub where their paths would have to diverge. The reality of the moment began to settle in. The express bus back to the Christiansburg compound was arriving, and Jessica, who lived in a dorm on campus, would have to walk back.

It was time to say goodbye.

"Well," Jessica said, her voice hesitant as she started to gently pull her hand away. "This was... this was an amazing day, Jordan. I'll see you in class tomorrow."

The simple act of her letting go was the trigger.

Jordan's grip on her hand tightened with a desperate, white-knuckled strength. "No," he whispered, his voice suddenly small and tight. "You... you can't leave."

"Jordan, I have to," she said gently. "My dorm is that way."

"No!" he said, his voice cracking. The terror of his 16-year-old self resurfaced, the memory of his mother's rude and nasty phone call and the agony of losing his first, secret girlfriend. His brain couldn't distinguish between a temporary goodbye and the permanent, traumatic separation his mother had forced on them. He was losing her all over again.

A low, guttural hum started in his chest. His body began to rock back and forth in his wheelchair. He was having a massive autistic meltdown, right there on the crowded bus platform.

The Group of 8, who could not bear to see Jordan in so much emotional distress, immediately formed their protective fortress around him.

"J, you're okay, you're safe," Madison said, her hand on his shoulder.

Mark and Hannah exchanged a panicked look. They knew he was no longer on sternal precautions, but his sternum was still surgically repaired. A full, violent meltdown where he tensed his chest was still dangerous. They had to pull him out of the meltdown, not just wait for it to pass.

"Jordan, stop tensing!" Mark said, his voice firm.

But Jordan was lost, his eyes squeezed shut, shaking his head. "She's leaving... she's leaving..."

Aubrie Kelly, his wife, immediately understood. This wasn't a normal overload; this was a trauma response. She stepped forward, putting her hand on Jessica's arm.

"He can't say goodbye," Aubrie said, her decision absolute. "So don't. Come home with us. To Christiansburg. Just for the night. He just needs to know you're not gone."

Jessica stared at her, her eyes wide with confusion. "What? To your... your *house*?" she stammered. "Aubrie, I can't. You're his *wife*. I'm not... I can't intrude."

"This is the No Jealousy Pact," Aubrie insisted, her voice firm but kind. "He's hurting. He needs *you*. That's all that matters. Please."

Jessica looked past Aubrie at Jordan, who was now openly crying, his face a mask of terror, still rocking. She couldn't stand to see him in this much pain. Then, she thought of her own dorm. She thought of her loud, obnoxious roommates she was not getting along with. A night away from them, in a safe, quiet house, was a blessing in disguise.

She made her decision. She took Jordan's other hand. "Okay," she said, her voice clear. "Okay, Jordan. I'm not leaving. I'm coming with you."

Instantly, the rocking slowed. The low hum in his chest subsided. He let out a long, shuddering, tearful breath. He was still overwhelmed, but the trigger—the separation—was gone.

### Part 6: Forge Hall

Zoe, the logistician, immediately took charge. "Okay. We missed the 5:15 PM express. Jessica, you need to pack an overnight bag. We'll all go with you."

The entire group of nine left the transit center, their formation a familiar, protective bubble around Jordan and Jessica. They walked across campus to Forge Hall, one of the newer dorms that had been built after Bennett Hall was demolished.

Jessica swiped her Poly Pass to let them in. When she unlocked her dorm room door, the Group of 8 saw for themselves exactly why she had been so quick to accept their offer. The room was a mess, loud music was blaring from a speaker, and two other girls were sitting on their beds, barely looking up as nine people filed into their small space.

"Hey, Jess," one roommate said, not unkindly, but with a total lack of warmth. "Who are all these people?"

"They're my friends," Jessica said quietly. "I'm just grabbing some things. I'm staying with them tonight."

The other roommate just scoffed, not even looking up from her phone. "Whatever."

The Group of 8 exchanged a single, silent look. They saw for themselves that the environment was cold, cliquey, and that Jessica clearly did not get along with her roommates.

Hannah, ever the "mom friend," immediately went into action. "Okay, Jess, where's your bag?"

The group helped her pack everything she'd need: toiletries, her laptop, and clothes for the night and for class the next day. They were in and out in less than five minutes.

As the door clicked shut behind them, Madison put a supportive arm around Jessica. "Okay, yeah, you're *definitely* better off with us."

The group of nine walked back to the CPI Transit Center, arriving just in time for the 6:15 PM express bus to Christiansburg. Jessica, with her overnight bag at her feet, sat next to Jordan (he transferred out of his wheelchair to be with her), her hand safely and securely back in his.

### Part 7: The Christiansburg Sanctuary

The express bus from CPI pulled into the Falling Branch Park & Ride just after 6:45 PM. The group of nine, now including Jessica, made the short, quiet walk to the Brooks' house. Jessica was nervous, clutching the straps of her overnight bag, her mind still reeling from the events at the transit center.

They entered the house to the warm smell of dinner cooking. Daniel and Laura Brooks, who had already been briefed via the Jordan Comms chat, greeted them at the door.

"You must be Jessica," Laura said, her voice warm and immediately putting Jessica at ease. She didn't look surprised or judgmental, just kind. "We're so glad you're here. Welcome to our home."

"Thank you for having me, ma'am," Jessica said politely.

"Oh, it's just Laura," she smiled.

A woman with Zoe's analytical eyes and a kind smile came over. "Hi, Jessica, I'm Megan, Zoe's older sister." She offered a hand. "I just got integrated into this crazy family myself. Don't worry, they're the best."

It was a little past 7 PM by the time the entire, expanded family—now twelve people—was gathered around the massive dining table for dinner. Jessica sat between Jordan and Megan. The atmosphere was loud, loving, and chaotic. She watched as this massive family passed plates, argued about classes, and included Jordan in every conversation. It was overwhelming, but in the best way.

### Part 8: The Sofa Assumption

After dinner, the Group of 8 and Jessica migrated down to the basement, their designated territory. Mark and Hannah settled onto one of the queen beds to watch a movie. The others—Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, and Sarah—piled onto the massive Alaskan King bed with Jordan, a familiar, comfortable "puppy pile."

Jessica stood awkwardly by the stairs, her overnight bag at her feet. She was an island in their ocean of intimacy. She assumed, logically, that she would be sleeping on the sectional sofa or in a guest room.

Megan, sensing her unease, got up from the table where she'd been working and sat with her on the sofa. "Hey," Megan said softly. "It's... a lot, isn't it?"

"I just... I don't understand," Jessica whispered, looking at the king bed where Jordan was cuddled between his wife, Aubrie, and Madison. "How is she... okay with this?"

"Because she means it," Megan said. "It took me a long time to get it, too. I'm 27, and my brain just about broke trying to understand. But the pact is real. The no jealousy rule is real. They just... love him. And they love each other. They're not just a couple; they're a system."

### Part 9: The Separation Trigger

As the hour grew later, Jessica knew it was time. She stood up, her stomach twisting. "Well," she said, her voice small. "I should probably figure out the sofa..."

The word sofa cut through Jordan's relaxed state. He had been fine, surrounded by his pact, knowing Jessica was in the room. But sofa meant separate. It meant other." It meant she wasn't *with* him.

His breath hitched. He sat up, his eyes wide with a familiar, rising panic. He looked at Jessica, and his mind was no longer in the safe basement; it was back on the bus platform, watching her walk away. He was terrified of waking up and finding her gone, a repeat of the trauma his mother had inflicted.

"No," he said, his voice tight. "You... you're not leaving, are you?"

"I'm just going to the sofa, Jordan..."

"No!" he said, his voice cracking. He started to rock, the low hum of his anxiety returning. "You can't. Please. Don't leave."

### Part 10: The Invitation

The pact, seeing the pre-meltdown signals, immediately went on high alert. Aubrie, his wife, slid off the bed and knelt in front of Jessica.

"He's not melting down because of you," Aubrie said, her voice calm and steady. "He's terrified of losing you again. His mother's 'shut down' must have done more damage than any of us knew."

"But... what do I do?" Jessica asked, her own eyes wide with distress. "I don't want to upset him!"

"Then don't leave," Aubrie said, her smile warm. "We don't want you on the sofa. We want you *here*. With us. In the puppy pile."

Jessica was floored. "In... in the *bed*? With you? And... and all of you?"

"Yes," Madison confirmed from the bed. "But you have a choice. This is the most important rule. You *always* have a choice. We have a guest room upstairs if you want it. No one will be mad. But we want you to know you are welcome here. We *want* you to join us."

Jessica looked at Jordan. He was still rocking, his face a mask of fear, terrified she would choose the guest room. She knew he wouldn't calm down until he knew she was secure. She didn't want to see him go through another meltdown.

She took a deep breath. "Okay," she whispered. "I... I want the puppy pile."

### Part 11: The Rules of the Pile

The relief that washed over Jordan was visible; the rocking stopped instantly. He let out a long, shaky breath.

As Jessica hesitantly moved toward the massive Alaskan King bed, Hannah stepped in.

"Okay," Hannah said, her "mom friend" voice gentle but firm. "Welcome to the family, Jessica. Now, the rules."

Jessica tensed again. "Rules?"

"Just one, really," Hannah said, "and it's for his safety. You know he had open-heart surgery, right?" Jessica nodded.

"His sternum is surgically repaired," Hannah explained. "It's healed, but it's a lifelong change. That means the puppy pile is calm. No sudden weight on his chest, no jostling, and no pulling on his arms or chest. We're here to cuddle and keep him safe, not to be chaotic. We just... we have to be careful."

Jessica nodded, understanding. This wasn't just an invitation; it was an act of shared care.

She kicked off her shoes and climbed onto the massive bed. The six other women of the pact made room for her, and Jordan, with a look of profound, tearful gratitude, pulled her into the pile. She was home.

### Part 12: The Final Straw

On Tuesday afternoon, the Group of 8 walked Jessica back to Forge Hall. The plan was for her to quickly grab her books and a change of clothes before heading to the transit center with them. Jordan, Aubrie, and Madison accompanied her up to her room while the rest of the group waited in the lobby.

"I'll just be a second," Jessica said, fumbling for her Poly Pass.

She unlocked her door and pushed it open. The three of them froze on the threshold.

The room was dark, the curtains drawn, but it was not empty. Loud, thumping bass music was still playing, as it always was. And in the dim light, they could clearly see that both of her roommates were in their respective beds, and neither was alone. They were actively engaged with their boyfriends, completely oblivious to the door opening. The room was a mess, clothes strewn everywhere, and the air felt thick and stale.

Jessica let out a small, strangled sound, a mixture of disgust and humiliation. This was her *home*. Her *space*. And it had been completely **violated**.

"Oh, my god," Madison whispered, her face a mask of disgust.

Jessica immediately slammed the door shut, her entire body shaking. "I... I can't," she whispered, leaning against the wall in the hallway. "I'm not going back in there. I **never want to come back** to this room. Ever".

Aubrie, her face a mask of cold fury, put her arm around Jessica's shoulders. "You're not," she said, her voice an absolute promise. She pulled out her mPhone. "You're moving out. Tonight."

She immediately sent a message to the Jordan Comms chat.

**Aubrie:** \[URGENT\] \[LOGISTICS\] Change of plans. Jessica is moving out. Now. Her roommates are disgusting pigs. We're at Forge Hall. Daniel, Laura, we need a car. We're getting her things.

### Part 13: The Extraction

The group's response was instantaneous. Zoe, Mark, and the others came up from the lobby. Within fifteen minutes, **Daniel and Laura Brooks arrived** in the minivan, Daniel's face set with a grim determination.

This time, Mark and Daniel led the charge. They didn't knock. Mark threw the door open, flooding the room with light from the hallway.

The occupants of the beds scrambled, shouting in protest.

"Everyone out," Daniel's voice boomed, carrying the full authority of a father. "Now. You have sixty seconds to become decent and leave the room. My daughter's friends are moving her things."

The roommates and their boyfriends, terrified and embarrassed, grabbed their clothes and fled into the hallway, muttering curses.

The Group of 8 descended on the room like a practiced extraction team. Hannah and Lauren grabbed her bedding, Zoe and Sarah packed her desk, while the others grabbed her clothes. They had all of her possessions packed and loaded into the minivan in under ten minutes.

As they walked away from Forge Hall for the last time, Jessica looked at the chaotic, loud, and disrespectful dorm. She then looked at Jordan, waiting for her by the van, his hand outstretched. She knew, with absolute certainty, that she would rather have the puppy pile and its serene calmness than the chaos of her roommates. She had found her real home.

### Part 14: The New Social Script

It was Tuesday night, their second night with Jessica. After the traumatic "extraction" from Forge Hall, the group settled into the basement. The mood was calm, safe, and pointedly non-sexual, a direct response to the trauma Jessica had experienced. As bedtime approached, the group began their puppy pile ritual, climbing onto the massive Alaskan King bed.

Jessica, however, stood awkwardly by the sofa, her overnight bag clutched in her hands. She was quietly sobbing, her body shaking as she processed the day. She was completely overwhelmed. Her mind was stuck on the scene from her dorm. It was not just the violation of her space; it was the betrayal. She had witnessed affairs in progress. She had seen two girls casually cheating on their boyfriends. Her old social programming was screaming at her: This is what happens. Intimacy is a lie. Relationships are a violation.

Aubrie Kelly paused and looked over, her expression soft. "Jess? You're not sleeping on the sofa. Come on."

Jessica flinched, her sob catching in her throat. "Oh, no, I... I can't. That's... that's your bed. With... with your husband. I can't."

Her autistic mind was in a deadlock. Her old social programming was clear: You do not climb into a bed with another woman's husband. That is what my disgusting roommates did. That is an affair.

Aubrie, whose own autistic mind understood the need for clear, logical rules, knew she had to provide an explicit override. She slid off the bed and walked over to Jessica, leading her to the bed. She took her by the hands.

"Hey. Look at me," Aubrie said, her voice kind but firm. "This is not Forge Hall. You didn't just see a betrayal. You saw a violation without consent or respect. This," she gestured to the king bed, "is our family. It is built on consent and respect."

She began to walk Jessica toward the bed. "I am Jordan's wife," she continued, "and I am giving you permission".

Jessica tensed, her mind still fighting it.

Aubrie understood. She knew this would take time and she would need to be reminded a few times. "He needs his friends, Jess. He needs you. He had a meltdown yesterday because he was terrified of losing you again. We are not letting that happen."

She sat Jessica on the edge of the bed, then sat next to her, between her and Jordan. She took Jordan's hand, then took Jessica's hand, placing them near each other on the blanket. "This is okay," Aubrie said. "This is safe. This is what we do."

Jordan, sensing her hesitation, just gave her a small, hopeful smile. He didn't push, he just waited.

Jessica's mind was lagging, trying to process the new data. The wife is encouraging this. The wife is sitting right here. This is not a violation. This is a new rule. It was the first step. For tonight, she just sat on the edge of the bed, her hand near his, and let herself be part of the serene calmness until she finally, nervously, agreed to lie down on the very edge of the massive mattress.

Aubrie knew this reprogramming would need to be reminded a few times. She would have to lead this exercise every night, patiently demonstrating that in this family, it's perfectly acceptable to cuddle the man they all loved.

### Part 15: The First Hug

It was time for bed. The Group of 8 had finished their evening rituals, and the quiet, calm energy of the basement was a healing balm after the chaos of Jessica's dorm room. Mark and Hannah, seeing the emotional night ahead, gave Jordan and the women supportive hugs before heading upstairs to their own room.

This left the seven women of the pact in the main living area. Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, and Sarah all began their familiar puppy pile ritual, climbing onto the massive Alaskan King bed.

Jessica, however, stood awkwardly by the sofa, her overnight bag clutched in her hands. She was quietly sobbing, her body shaking as she processed the day. She was completely overwhelmed. Her mind was stuck on the scene from her dorm. It was not just the violation of her space; it was the betrayal. She had witnessed affairs in progress. She had seen two girls casually cheating on their boyfriends. Her old social programming was screaming at her: *You do not climb into a bed with another woman's husband. That is what my disgusting roommates did. That is an affair.*

Aubrie Kelly paused and looked over, her expression soft. "Jess? You're not sleeping on the sofa. Come on."

Jessica flinched, her sob catching in her throat. "Oh, no, I... I can't. That's... that's your bed. With... with your husband. I can't."

Her autistic mind was in a deadlock. Her old social programming was clear: You do not climb into a bed with another woman's husband. That is what my disgusting roommates did. That is an affair.

Aubrie, whose own autistic mind understood the need for clear, logical rules, knew she had to provide an explicit override. She slid off the bed and walked over to Jessica, leading her to the bed. She took her by the hands.

"Hey. Look at me," Aubrie said, her voice kind but firm. "This is not Forge Hall. You didn't just see a betrayal. You saw a violation *without* consent or respect. This," she gestured to the king bed, "is our family. It is built *on* consent and respect."

She began to walk Jessica toward the bed. "I am Jordan's wife," she continued, "and I am giving you permission".

Jessica tensed, her mind still fighting it.

Aubrie understood. She knew this would take time and she would need to be reminded a few times. "He needs his friends, Jess. He needs *you*. He had a meltdown yesterday because he was terrified of losing you again. We are *not* letting that happen."

She sat Jessica on the edge of the bed, then sat next to her, between her and Jordan. She took Jordan's hand, then took Jessica's hand, placing them near each other on the blanket. "This is okay," Aubrie said. "This is safe. This is what we do."

Jordan, sensing her hesitation, just gave her a small, hopeful smile. He didn't push, he just waited.

Jessica's mind was lagging, trying to process the new data. *The wife is encouraging this. The wife is sitting right here. This is not a violation. This is a new rule.* It was the first step. For tonight, she just sat on the edge of the bed, her hand near his, and let herself be part of the serene calmness until she finally, nervously, agreed to lie down on the very edge of the massive mattress.

Aubrie knew this reprogramming would need to be reminded a few times. She would have to lead this exercise every night, patiently demonstrating that in this family, it's perfectly acceptable to cuddle the man they all loved.

Jessica's social script was slowly rewritten; she began to accept that cuddling with a married man was okay, in this specific case with Jordan. The other women of the pact, seeing her hesitation, slowly gathered onto the bed, gently introducing Jessica to the concept of the puppy pile. They were all careful of Jordan's surgically repaired sternum, which was healed but still a lifelong consideration.

Finally, Jessica embraced Jordan, cuddling with him properly. It was the hug she had been wanting to give him since he was 16 years old, four long years in the making.

The physical contact, the reality of finally holding him, broke the last of her composure. She was sobbing quietly as she clung to him, not from sadness, but from a profound, overwhelming sense of relief.

Jordan just held her, his own tears running silently into her hair. "I missed you so much, Jess," he whispered.

"I missed you, too," she cried.

The rest of the women of the pact were in the bed, making sure that Jessica was okay. Aubrie, his wife, settled in on Jordan's other side, placing her hand on his arm. Madison, Megan, and the others found their spots, creating the full, calm, and protective puppy pile. They were a fortress of love, and Jessica was finally, truly, safe inside.

## Chapter 2: Homecoming 2016

### Part 1: Midnight at The Foundry

It was 11:55 PM on Thursday night, the eve of the album release. A line of excited students snaked out the doors of The Foundry, winding through the lobby of Newman Hall. In 2014, Jordan had felt like an invisible ghost in this building, too poor to buy a hoodie and too scared to speak.

Tonight, he was at the front of the line.

The entire Group of 9 was with him. They were all decked out in their maroon and orange gear, buzzing with caffeine and excitement. Jessica, fully integrated into the family, stood comfortably between Jordan and Aubrie, her hand resting on the handle of his wheelchair.

At midnight, the doors opened. The industrial-chic interior of The Foundry was transformed. Neon teal and magenta lights—the colors of the Electric Color tour—washed over the exposed brick walls, mixing with the university's maroon and orange branding.
