Jordan wheeled himself directly to the display tower in the center of the store. There it was. The Deluxe Vinyl Box Set of Live at The Crucible. The cover art was a stunning, high-contrast shot of the stadium lit up during the finale, the confetti raining down like stardust.

He picked up the heavy box set. He didn't look at the price tag with fear this time. He pulled out his wallet, took out his debit card—linked to the bank account he managed with Hannah's help —and paid for it himself.

"Receipt in the bag?" the cashier asked.

"No," Jordan smiled, holding the box set tight against his chest. "I'll carry it."

### Part 2: The Homecoming Concert

Later that Friday afternoon, the celebration moved outside. As part of the official Homecoming festivities, **The Stardust Engine** was playing a free, open-air mini-concert on the steps of **Newman Hall**, facing down the Alumni Mall toward the Pylons.

But it wasn't just a concert; it was a feast.

The air wasn't just crisp with autumn leaves; it was thick with the delicious, savory smell of burgers, fries, and tacos. Lined up along the bottom of the steps were a dozen official CPI Dining Services food carts. But there were no cash registers, and no one was swiping a meal plan.

A massive banner draped over the center cart read: **LUNCH IS ON THE ENGINE ROOM. EAT UP, FORGERS.**

Jordan sat in his wheelchair in the front row, a half-eaten taco on a paper plate in his lap and a cold soda in the cupholder of his chair. His family formed their familiar, protective semi-circle around him. On his left, his wife Aubrie held his hand. On his right, Jessica held the other. They were all eating, laughing, and surrounded by 5,000 other students who were currently enjoying the universal currency of college: free food.

Ryan O'Connell, the band's guitarist, stepped up to the mic, strumming a lazy, happy chord on his acoustic guitar.

"How's the food?" Ryan asked, his voice booming over the lawn. "You guys getting enough to eat?"

The crowd cheered, many raising their paper plates and sodas in a toast, mouths full.

"Good," Ryan said, his smile turning a little wistful as he looked up at the building behind him. "Because I remember starving on these exact steps waiting for a student loan check that never came. We used to scrape up nickels just to buy a bag of chips in that hallway behind me."

He gestured to the carts. "But today... the menu is open, and the tab is settled. Eat up, Forgers!"

The crowd roared their appreciation. It was the ultimate "Rich Alumni" move, but it felt grounded and real. They weren't buying a library; they were buying lunch for the kids who needed it.

"This set is for the ones building the future on an empty stomach," Ryan said, kicking the band into a tight, high-energy set of their hits.

They played for an hour, feeding off the energy of the home crowd. Then, as the sun began to dip lower, **Cassidy O’Connell**, the lead singer, stepped to the mic for the finale.

"We couldn't leave without playing this one," she said, smiling at the crowd. "This is for everyone who found their color right here in Blacksburg."

She hit the opening piano motif of **"Electric Color"**.

The crowd cheered, but for Jordan, the world fell away. He wasn't thinking about the football game, or his mother, or the surgery. He was just feeling the music.

Oh, I'm seeing in electric color!

When the chorus hit, Jordan sang along at the top of his lungs, his voice joining the thousands of other Forgers. He squeezed Aubrie's hand. He squeezed Jessica's hand. He looked back at Mark and Hannah, at Zoe and Madison, at Lauren and Sarah and Megan.

He was so happy. The song swelled to its final, triumphant chord, ringing out across the campus. Jordan closed his eyes, letting the sound wash over him. He was home, he was loved, and his life was bright, electric, and entirely his own.

### Part 3: The Basement Premiere

"Screw the football game," he said, looking at the TV with a dismissive wave. "I want to be back there."

He looked at the two options in his hands. The Blu-Ray disc offered the visuals, but the TV in the basement was just a standard 1080p set with basic built-in stereo speakers. It would sound tinny and flat.

Then he looked at the Maroon and Orange splatter vinyl. He looked over at Mark’s setup—the high-end turntable, the dedicated pre-amp, and the floor-standing speakers that filled the room with rich, warm sound.

"Vinyl," Jordan stated decisively. "I want to feel it. The TV speakers can't handle the gong."

Mark grinned, a look of audiophile pride on his face. "Excellent choice, brother. Let's spin the wax."

The Group of 9 settled into their comfortable spots. Mark carefully removed the heavy, splatter-colored record from its sleeve, placing it on the platter. He cleaned it with a brush, dropped the needle, and cranked the volume on the pre-amp.

The basement filled with the warm, crackling silence of the lead-in groove, and then... BOOM.

The sound was massive. The high-fidelity system captured the depth of the stadium drums and the roar of the 60,000 fans in a way the TV never could. It wasn't just noise; it was a physical presence in the room.

They listened to the entire concert, singing along, eating pizza, and ignoring the football stats rolling in on their phones.

When the final side of the third LP began—the encore—the room went quiet. The piano motif of "Electric Color" floated out of the speakers, crystal clear and heartbreakingly beautiful.

Jordan closed his eyes. He wasn't watching a screen; he was back in his chair in the front row. He could feel Aubrie's hand in his left, Jessica's in his right. He could feel the vibration of the bass in his chest.

When the final gong hit rang out, decaying slowly into the silence of the room, Jordan opened his eyes. He looked at his family, bathed in the warm light of their sanctuary.

"That," he whispered, staring at the spinning record, "is what victory sounds like."

He didn't need to see the game. He had already won.

# Book 3: Spring 2018

## Chapter 16: The Name on the Paper

### Part 1: The Regalia Check

The morning of Saturday, May 12, 2018, was bright and clear. The construction on the new "Compound" out on U.S. 11 was nearly complete, but for now, the Brooks’ house in Christiansburg was still the center of the universe. It was Graduation Day.

In the basement, the air was thick with hairspray, excitement, and the rustle of synthetic fabric. Ten graduates—the Group of 8 plus Jessica and Megan (who was there for moral support as an alumna)—were getting ready.

Jordan sat in his wheelchair in front of the full-length mirror. He wore a crisp white shirt and a tie that matched the university’s maroon. He adjusted his graduation gown, smoothing the fabric over his chest. But his eyes weren't on the gown. They were fixed on the card resting on his desk—the small, white index card he had to hand to the reader before crossing the stage.

He picked it up. In bold, typed letters, it read: JORDAN SHAWN KELLY Bachelor of Science, Astrophysics Magna Cum Laude

He traced the letters with his thumb. Kelly. Shawn. There was no Fuller. There was no Aubrey.

Those names had died in a courtroom six months ago, on the same day he had married Aubrie Jean. It had been a dual ceremony of love and law—exchanging vows with his wife and then, moments later, standing before a judge to legally sever the last tie to his biological father and his abusive mother.

"Hey, husband," a soft voice said.

Jordan looked up in the mirror. Aubrie Jean Kelly, also in her cap and gown, stood behind him. She wrapped her arms around his neck, resting her chin on his shoulder.

"You okay?" she asked, looking at the card in his hand.

"Yeah," Jordan whispered, a smile spreading across his face. "I was just thinking... she’s not graduating today. Aubrey isn't graduating. He doesn't exist anymore. I am."

"Damn right you are," Mark said, walking by and clapping Jordan on the shoulder. "Jordan Shawn Kelly. Has a nice ring to it, brother."

### Part 2: The Love Letter from Uncle Sam

While the graduates were finishing their prep, Daniel Brooks came down the stairs. He held a familiar, ominous-looking envelope in his hand.

"Jordan," Daniel said, his voice calm but serious. "I hate to do this on a day like today, but the mail came early. It’s another one."

The room went quiet. Everyone knew what "another one" meant.

Jordan took the envelope. It was from the Department of the Treasury, Internal Revenue Service. Notice CP87A.

He opened it with a steady hand. He scanned the dense text. We received a tax return from another taxpayer claiming the same dependent... You must amend your return or provide proof...

"She did it again," Jordan said, his voice flat. "She tried to claim 'Aubrey Fuller' again for the 2017 tax year."

"She’s digging her own grave," Zoe stated from across the room, adjusting her mortarboard. "We filed your return in January. We have your marriage license proving you are an independent head of household. We have the court order for your name change. The IRS computers have already flagged her return as the anomaly."

"She’s stubborn," Jordan shook his head, tossing the letter onto the desk next to his diploma card. "She thinks if she just keeps typing 'Aubrey' on the forms, the government will agree with her."

"Let her," Daniel said with a grim satisfaction. "She’s fighting a war she’s already lost. She’s going to get hit with penalties and interest that will ruin her. But that’s her problem. Today is about your success, not her failure."

Jordan looked at the IRS notice, then at his name card. Jordan Shawn Kelly. "You're right," he said, turning his wheelchair away from the desk. "Let's go get our degrees."

### Part 3: The Roll Call

The massive floor of The Crucible—the football stadium—was transformed. Thousands of chairs were arranged in neat rows on the turf where the football team played (and occasionally lost). The stands were packed with families.

Jordan wheeled himself down the center aisle, surrounded by his fortress. The Group of 8 sat together, a block of maroon robes.

When the moment came for the College of Science, the line began to move. Jordan rolled up the ramp to the stage. He handed his card to the reader, a professor who smiled warmly at him.

The professor leaned into the microphone. His voice boomed across the stadium, echoing off the concrete walls where Jordan had once fled in terror.

"JORDAN SHAWN KELLY!"

The cheer that went up from Section 104 was deafening. It wasn't just polite applause. It was a roar. Daniel, Laura, Megan, Bob, Emily, Diane, David, and Sarah’s dad were all on their feet, screaming. On the field, the rest of the pact—Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, Hannah, and Mark—were cheering so loud they drowned out the announcer for a solid ten seconds.

Jordan wheeled across the stage. He shook the Dean’s hand. He took the diploma cover. He looked out at the sea of faces. He thought of the woman in Hampton Roads who was currently staring at a rejection letter from the IRS, insisting that "Aubrey" was hers.

He’s not, Jordan thought, gripping the leather folder. He’s gone. I’m right here.

### Part 4: The Photo Finish

After the ceremony, the chaos of the "Crucible Bus Loop" was in full swing, but the family didn't rush to leave. They gathered near the construction site of the old Bennett Hall. The "Old Lady on the Hill" was gone, replaced by a sleek, modern glass-and-steel dorm.

"Okay, photo time!" Megan announced, wielding a professional camera.

The Group of 8 lined up. Mark and Hannah stood in the back, tall and proud. Zoe, Lauren, and Sarah stood in the middle. In the front, Jordan sat in his chair, his diploma displayed prominently on his lap. Aubrie sat on his left armrest. Madison sat on his right.

"Say 'Family'!" Megan called out.

"FAMILY!" they shouted.

Click.

As the group broke apart, laughing and hugging, Jordan looked down at his diploma again. He traced the name Jordan Shawn Kelly.

"Hey," Aubrie said, taking his hand. "You ready to go home? To the new house?"

The "Compound" on U.S. 11 was finally finished. The Certificate of Occupancy had been issued yesterday. The Alaskan King bed was being delivered tomorrow.

Jordan looked at his wife, then at his best friends. He looked at the diploma that proved he was capable, and the name that proved he was free.

"Yeah," he said, his smile brighter than the sun over Blacksburg. "Let's go home."

## Chapter 17: The Sunken Sanctuary

### Part 1: The Reveal

The construction on the U.S. 11 compound was finally complete. The Group of 8 (plus Jessica and Megan) stood in the threshold of the massive basement master suite.

The room was vast, bathed in light from the high clerestory windows that protected their privacy. But the floor was not flat. In the center of the room, occupying a massive footprint, was a sunken architectural feature.

"We couldn't buy a bed big enough," Daniel Brooks explained, wiping sawdust from his hands. "And we knew an Alaskan King—which is 9 feet by 9 feet—might be tight for eight people. So, we built something custom. We call it The Pit."

"It's huge," Jessica breathed.

Zoe stepped forward with her tablet. "I calculated the dimensions based on maximum occupancy and height variables. Specifically, the 'Emily Factor.' Since she measured 5'10" at age 15, we projected her final height could exceed 6 feet. Therefore, a standard 6.5-foot mattress was insufficient. The sleeping surface is 10 feet long to accommodate all potential heights."

### Part 2: The Architecture of Access

Jordan wheeled himself to the edge of the pit. He looked down. It wasn't just a mattress on the floor; it was a feat of engineering designed specifically for him.

The Layout:

1.  The Upper Rim: The main floor of the room, tiled in warm wood.

2.  The First Ramp: A gentle, ADA-compliant slope cut into the floor, leading down into the sunken area.

3.  The Transfer Deck (The Landing): The ramp ended at a polished wooden platform that ran along the entire left side of the pit.

4.  The Mattress Sea: Filling the rest of the pit was a custom-cut expanse of high-density memory foam, upholstered in a single, seamless sheet of dark blue jersey cotton.

"Here's the magic," Daniel said, pointing to the relationship between the Transfer Deck and the Mattress.

The wooden Transfer Deck was built at a specific elevation. The foam mattress was sunk slightly deeper into the concrete foundation.

The result? The top surface of the mattress was perfectly flush with the wooden deck.

"Go ahead, Jordan," Daniel said. "Test the transfer."

### Part 3: The Slide

Jordan wheeled down the gentle ramp. It was wide and smooth. He rolled onto the Transfer Deck and turned his chair 90 degrees.

He parked his wheelchair. The seat of his chair was exactly level with the wooden deck, which in turn was exactly level with the blue foam sea.

He locked his brakes. He removed his armrest. There was no climbing. There was no lifting. There was no gap.

He simply shifted his weight, sliding from his chair, across the smooth wood of the deck, and directly onto the soft, supportive surface of the bed.

"It works," Jordan whispered, realizing he could get in and out of this massive bed completely independently. He rolled away from the edge, out into the center of the 15-foot-wide sea. The foam was firm enough to support him but soft enough to be cozy—just like the crash mats from his childhood memories.

### Part 4: The Puppy Pile 3.0

"Okay, capacity test!" Madison cheered.

She, Aubrie, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, Megan, and Jessica all kicked off their shoes. They hurried down the ramp.

Because the bed was 15 feet wide and 10 feet long, they didn't have to squeeze.

- Jordan lay in the center, near the transfer deck.

- Aubrie and Madison curled up on his immediate sides.

- Zoe and Megan took the next spots.

- Lauren, Sarah, and Jessica sprawled out on the far side.

Even with all eight of them lying down, there was still room at the foot of the bed—plenty of space for Emily, if she ever visited, to stretch out her 6-foot-plus frame without her feet hanging off the edge.

"This is efficient," Zoe noted, staring up at the ceiling. "The sunken design eliminates the 'Roll-Off Risk' entirely. The floor is the bed."

Jordan looked at his wheelchair, parked safely on the wooden dock at eye level. He looked at the walls of the pit, which created a cozy, safe container for his family. He felt the warmth of Aubrie and Madison beside him.

"We're keeping this," Jordan said, closing his eyes. "Forever."

# Book: Daisy

## Chapter 3: The Summer Solstice

### Part 1: The Diversion (June 19, 2020)

The call came late on the evening of June 19th. Hannah was in early labor, pacing the living room of the Compound. Mark was on the phone with the local hospital in Blacksburg, his face tightening with stress.

"Diversion?" Mark repeated, his voice rising slightly. "What do you mean you're on diversion?"

He listened for a moment, then hung up, turning to the anxious group gathered in the kitchen. "The maternity ward at Montgomery Regional is full. They’re at capacity and redirecting all incoming labor patients."

"Where do we go?" Hannah asked, gripping the back of a chair as a contraction hit.

"Roanoke," Daniel Brooks said immediately, grabbing the car keys. "Star City General."

Jordan flinched. The name **Star City General** sent a shiver through him. That was where he had spent the longest, most terrifying nights of his life recovering from his open-heart surgery. It was a place of trauma, monitors, and sternal precautions.

But he looked at Hannah, who was scared and in pain. "It’s a good hospital," Jordan said, his voice steadying. "They saved my life. They’ll take care of her."

### Part 2: The Parking Lot Vigil

The convoy sped up I-81, the highway empty in the deep of the night. When they arrived at Star City General, the reality of the pandemic hit hard.

"One support person only," the nurse at the entrance said firmly, her face obscured by a mask and shield. "Just the father."

Mark hugged Hannah tight. "I've got you." He turned to the family—Daniel, Laura, Jordan, Aubrie, and the rest of the pact—who were standing by the sliding doors. "I'll text you. Every five minutes."

Mark and Hannah disappeared inside. The rest of the family walked back to the parking lot. They couldn't come in, but they wouldn't leave.

"We stay," Aubrie said, helping Jordan settle comfortably in the passenger seat of the minivan. "We wait right here."

For hours, the Group of 17 (minus Mark and Hannah) held a vigil in the hospital parking garage. They sat in their cars with the windows rolled down, passing phones back and forth, waiting for updates.

For Jordan, sitting in the shadow of the building where he had once laid helpless, the waiting was a strange reversal. **Five years ago**, they had waited for him in this very building while he fought for his life in the CVICU. Now, they were waiting for a new life to begin.

### Part 3: The Arrival (June 20, 2020)

The night slowly turned to gray, then to the soft pink of dawn. It was the morning of the Summer Solstice, the longest day of the year.

At **5:43 AM**, just as the sun crested over the Blue Ridge Mountains, Mark’s text came through to the group chat.

**Mark:** *She's here. Daisy May Anderson. 7lbs 4oz. Everyone is healthy.*

A cheer erupted in the parking garage, echoing off the concrete walls. Tears streamed down Jordan’s face. The hospital that had been a place of fear was now, forever, the place where his niece was born.

Minutes later, Mark initiated a video call. The family huddled around Daniel’s phone.

There was Hannah, exhausted but glowing in the hospital bed, holding a tiny, pink bundle wrapped in a standard hospital blanket.

"Hi, everyone," Hannah whispered, her voice raspy. "Meet Daisy."

Jordan looked at the screen, at the tiny face of the next generation. "Hi, Daisy," he whispered. "I'm Uncle Jordan."

### Part 4: The Solstice Protocol

A few days later, Mark and Hannah brought Daisy home to the Compound. The family gathered on the back patio, keeping a safe distance at first due to the pandemic protocols, just watching the baby sleep in her bassinet.

"June 20th," Zoe mused, checking the date. "She really waited for the Solstice."

"The day with the most light," Aubrie said, smiling at Jordan. "It fits. After everything... the darkness of the surgery, the pandemic... she came with the light."

Jordan looked at his wives—Aubrie, Madison, Zoe, Lauren, Sarah, Megan, and Jessica. He looked at the baby. An idea formed—a logical, beautiful system for their future.

"We should aim for this," Jordan said. "When we start... for us. We should try to have them on the Solstice. Like Daisy."

Zoe’s mind ran the calculations immediately. "To achieve a mid-June birth date, conception must occur within a specific window in late September or early October of the previous year. It allows for efficient batching of resources and shared birthday celebrations."

"Super Birthdays," Madison grinned. "One massive party every few years. All the cousins together."

"The Solstice Protocol," Sarah agreed.

It was decided. The **Procreation Pact** now had a schedule. They would wait, helping raise Daisy and surviving the pandemic, and then, they would aim for the **Solstice of 2024** to begin the first wave of the Kelly children.
